    钦定四库全书　　　　　　子部十二
    　　博物志　　　　　　　　　小説家三【琐记之属】提要
    　　【臣】等谨案博物志十卷旧本题晋张华撰考王嘉拾遗记称华好观秘异图纬之部捃采天下遗逸自书契之始考验神怪及世间闾里所説造博物志四百卷奏于武帝帝诏诘问卿才综万代博识无伦然记事采言亦多浮妄可更芟截浮疑分为十卷云云是其书作于武帝时今第四卷物性中称武帝泰始中武库火则武帝以后语矣周亮工书影谓艺文聚引博物志子贡説社一条今本不载案此条实在第八卷中亮工葢偶然未检然考裴松之三国志注魏志太祖纪文帝纪濊呉志孙贲传引博物志四条今本惟有太祖纪所引一条而佚其前 [1]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [1]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 钦定四库全书 - Imperially designated Four Treasuries of Books, a vast collection of books compiled during the Qing dynasty.
    • 子部十二 - Twelfth category of Subdivision, referring to one of the categories in the classification system of the Four Treasuries of Books.
    • 博物志 - Record of Diverse Knowledge, the title of the book being described.
    • 小说家属三【琐记之属】 - Third category under the Small Talk genre, indicating the classification of the book within the broader literary taxonomy.
    • 提要 - Introduction or Abstract, the section providing an overview of the book.
    • 谨案 - A formal expression used to introduce a detailed examination or analysis.
    • 旧本题晋张华撰 - The old edition is attributed to Zhang Hua of the Jin dynasty, indicating the authorship of the work.
    • 王嘉拾遗记 - Wang Jia's Record of Lost and Found, a historical text that provides information about Zhang Hua.
    • 好观秘异图纬之部 - Enjoyed studying the mysterious and esoteric sections of the imperial library, describing Zhang Hua's scholarly interests.
    • 捃采天下遗逸 - Collected the lost and forgotten from all over the world, referring to Zhang Hua's extensive research.
    • 自书契之始 - From the beginning of written records, indicating the historical scope of his work.
    • 考验神怪 - Examined supernatural phenomena, describing the content of the book.
    • 闾里所説 - What is said in the streets and alleys, referring to folk stories and local legends.
    • 造博物志四百卷 - Compiled the Record of Diverse Knowledge in four hundred volumes, indicating the initial scale of the work.
    • 奏于武帝 - Presented to Emperor Wu, referring to the submission of the work to the emperor.
    • 诘问卿才综万代 - Questioned about your comprehensive knowledge spanning generations, indicating the emperor's inquiry into Zhang Hua's abilities.
    • 记事采言亦多浮妄 - The stories and sayings collected also contain many exaggerations, noting the mixed quality of the content.
    • 可更芟截浮疑 - Can be further edited to remove the frivolous and doubtful parts, suggesting a need for revision.
    • 分为十卷 - Divided into ten volumes, indicating the final form of the work.
    • 泰始中 - During the Tai Shi period, referring to a specific time in the reign of Emperor Wu.
    • 武库火 - Fire at the armory, a specific event mentioned in the text.
    • 艺文志聚 - Compendium of Literary and Historical Works, a reference to another historical text.
    • 子贡説社 - Zi Gong's discussion of the ancestral temple, a specific passage mentioned in the text.
    • 三国志注 - Annotations to the Records of the Three Kingdoms, a historical text that cites the Record of Diverse Knowledge.
    • 魏志太祖纪 - Biography of Cao Cao in the Wei Annals, a specific section of the Records of the Three Kingdoms.
    • 文帝纪 - Biography of Cao Pi in the Wei Annals, another specific section of the Records of the Three Kingdoms.
    • 濊 - Biography of Hui, a specific section of the Records of the Three Kingdoms.
    • 吴志孙贲传 - Biography of Sun Ben in the Wu Annals, another specific section of the Records of the Three Kingdoms.
    • 佚其前 - Missing the beginning, indicating that some parts of the Record of Diverse Knowledge are incomplete.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 钦定四库全书 - A massive collection of books compiled during the Qing dynasty, known for its comprehensive coverage of Chinese literature and scholarship.
    • 子部十二 - One of the twelve major categories in the classification system of the Four Treasuries of Books, which includes various types of literature and scholarly works.
    • 博物志 - A book that compiles a wide range of knowledge, including historical, geographical, and mythological content.
    • 小说家属三【琐记之属】 - A category that includes small talk, anecdotes, and miscellaneous notes, often considered less serious than other literary forms.
    • 提要 - An introductory section that provides a summary and evaluation of a book, commonly found in bibliographies and catalogs.
    • 晋张华 - Zhang Hua, a prominent scholar and statesman of the Jin dynasty, known for his extensive knowledge and contributions to literature and science.
    • 王嘉 - Wang Jia, a historian who wrote the Record of Lost and Found, a valuable source of information about the Jin dynasty.
    • 书契之始 - The beginning of written records, referring to the earliest known written documents in Chinese history.
    • 神怪 - Supernatural phenomena, including ghosts, spirits, and other mystical beings.
    • 闾里所説 - Folk stories and local legends, often passed down orally and reflecting the beliefs and traditions of common people.
    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Jin, a significant ruler who supported and promoted various scholarly and cultural projects.
    • 泰始 - A period during the reign of Emperor Wu, known for its cultural and intellectual achievements.
    • 武库 - The armory, a place where weapons and military equipment were stored.
    • 艺文志聚 - A historical text that compiles and categorizes various literary and historical works.
    • 三国志注 - Annotations to the Records of the Three Kingdoms, a historical text that provides detailed information about the period of the Three Kingdoms.
    • 魏志 - The Wei Annals, a section of the Records of the Three Kingdoms that covers the history of the state of Wei.
    • 吴志 - The Wu Annals, a section of the Records of the Three Kingdoms that covers the history of the state of Wu.

    Proper Nouns

    • 钦定四库全书 - The vast collection of books compiled during the Qing dynasty.
    • 子部十二 - One of the twelve major categories in the classification system of the Four Treasuries of Books.
    • 博物志 - The title of the book being described.
    • 小说家属三【琐记之属】 - The classification of the book within the broader literary taxonomy.
    • 提要 - The introductory section of the book.
    • 晋张华 - The author of the book, a prominent scholar and statesman of the Jin dynasty.
    • 王嘉 - The historian who wrote the Record of Lost and Found.
    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Jin, a significant ruler who supported and promoted various scholarly and cultural projects.
    • 泰始 - A period during the reign of Emperor Wu, known for its cultural and intellectual achievements.
    • 武库 - The armory, a place where weapons and military equipment were stored.
    • 艺文志聚 - A historical text that compiles and categorizes various literary and historical works.

    Imperially designated Four Treasuries of Books　　　　　　Twelfth Category of Subdivision
    　　　　Record of Diverse Knowledge　　　　　　　　Third Category under the Small Talk genre【Suo Ji (琐记)】Introduction
    　　　　Ministers respectfully examined the Record of Diverse Knowledge in ten volumes, which is attributed to Zhang Hua of the Jin dynasty. According to Wang Jia's Record of Lost and Found, Zhang Hua enjoyed studying the mysterious and esoteric sections of the imperial library, collecting the lost and forgotten from all over the world, from the beginning of written records, examining supernatural phenomena, and incorporating what is said in the streets and alleys. He compiled the Record of Diverse Knowledge in four hundred volumes and presented it to Emperor Wu. The emperor questioned Zhang Hua about his comprehensive knowledge spanning generations, acknowledging his extensive learning but noting that the stories and sayings collected also contained many exaggerations. He suggested that the work could be further edited to remove the frivolous and doubtful parts and divided into ten volumes. Thus, the book was created during the reign of Emperor Wu. However, in the fourth volume, under the section on the nature of things, it mentions that during the Tai Shi period, there was a fire at the armory, indicating that the book must have been written after these events. Zhou Lianggong's Book Shadows mentions that the Compendium of Literary and Historical Works cites a passage from the Record of Diverse Knowledge where Zi Gong discusses the ancestral temple, but this passage is not found in the current edition. Upon examination, this passage is actually located in the eighth volume, suggesting that Zhou Lianggong may have overlooked it accidentally. However, when examining the annotations to the Records of the Three Kingdoms by Pei Songzhi, which cite the Record of Diverse Knowledge in four places in the Wei Annals, the biography of Cao Cao, the biography of Cao Pi, and the biography of Sun Ben, it is found that only the passage cited in the biography of Cao Cao is present in the current edition, while the others are missing. [1]

    半余三条皆无之又江淹古铜剑赞引张华博物志曰铸铜之工不可复得惟蜀地羗中时有解者今本无此语足证非齐梁时所见之本又唐防要载显庆三年太常丞吕才奏按张华博物志曰白雪是泰帝使素女鼓五弦曲名以其调高人遂和寡又张彦远厯代名画记引张华博物志曰刘褒汉桓帝时人曽画云汉图人见之觉又画北风图人见之觉凉今本皆无此语李善注文选引张华博物志十二条见今本者九条其西京赋注引王孙公子皆古人相推敬
    之词一条闲居赋注引张骞使大夏得石榴李广利为贰师将军伐大宛得蒲陶一条七命注引橙似橘而非若柚而有芬香一条今本皆无此语段公路北户录引博物志五条见今本者三条其鸺鹠一名鸡鸺一条金鱼脑中有麸金出功婆塞一条则今本皆无此语足证亦非唐人所见之本太平广记引博物志郑宏沈酿川一条赵彦衞云麓漫钞引博物志黄蓝张骞得自西域一条今本皆无之晁公武读书志称卷首有理略后有赞文今本卷首第一条为地理略自魏 [2]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [2]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 半余三条皆无之 - Indicates that three out of the total items (possibly five or six) are absent or incomplete.
    • 又江淹古铜剑赞引张华博物志曰 - A citation format where '又' means 'also' or 'furthermore,' and '引' means 'to quote' or 'to cite.'
    • 铸铜之工不可复得 - Literally 'the work of casting copper cannot be found again,' meaning that the skill or method of casting copper is lost or no longer available.
    • 惟蜀地羌中时有解者 - Literally 'only in Shu and Qiang regions sometimes there are those who can understand it,' referring to rare individuals who can interpret or understand ancient texts or artifacts.
    • 今本无此语 - Means 'the current version does not contain this phrase,' indicating that certain passages or references are missing in modern editions.
    • 足证非齐梁时所见之本 - Means 'sufficiently proves that it is not the version seen during the Qi and Liang dynasties,' suggesting that the text has been altered or abridged over time.
    • 唐防要载显庆三年太常丞吕才奏 - Refers to a historical record from the Tang dynasty, specifically the year 658 (the third year of the Xianqing era), where the official Lü Cai made a report.
    • 按张华博物志曰 - A citation format meaning 'according to Zhang Hua's *Miscellaneous Records*,' where '按' means 'according to' or 'based on.'
    • 白雪是泰帝使素女鼓五弦曲名 - Refers to a musical piece called 'White Snow,' which is said to be a song played by the celestial musician Su Nü on a five-stringed instrument, commissioned by the emperor of the heavens.
    • 以其调高人遂和寡 - Means 'because of its high pitch, few people can follow along,' referring to the difficulty of performing or appreciating the music.
    • 刘褒汉桓帝时人 - Refers to Liu Bao, a painter who lived during the reign of Emperor Huan of Han.
    • 觉 - A character that seems to be corrupted or unclear in the original text. It may refer to a feeling of discomfort or unease.
    • 觉凉 - Means 'feel cool,' referring to the sensation evoked by the painting.
    • 李善注文选引张华博物志十二条 - Refers to the annotations by Li Shan on the *Wen Xuan* (a collection of literary works), which cite twelve passages from Zhang Hua's *Miscellaneous Records*.
    • 见今本者九条 - Means 'nine of them are found in the current version,' indicating that some of the cited passages are still present in modern editions.
    • 西京赋注引王孙公子皆古人相推敬之词 - Refers to a commentary on the *Western Capital Ode* (a famous literary work), where '王孙公子' are terms used to address noble young men, and '相推敬' means 'to respect each other.'
    • 闲居赋注引张骞使大夏得石榴李广利为贰师将军伐大宛得蒲陶 - Refers to a commentary on the *Ode on Seclusion*, where Zhang Qian, a famous explorer, brought back pomegranates from Da Xia (ancient Persia), and Li Guangli, a general, obtained grapes from Duyu (ancient Fergana).
    • 七命注引橙似橘而非若柚而有芬香 - Refers to a commentary on the *Seven Commandments*, where '橙' (orange) is described as similar to '橘' (tangerine) but different from '柚' (pomelo), with a fragrant aroma.
    • 太平广记引博物志郑宏沈酿川一条 - Refers to a passage in *Taiping Guangji* (a vast collection of stories and anecdotes), which cites a story about Zheng Hong and his wine-making in Shenchang River.
    • 赵彦衞云麓漫钞引博物志黄蓝张骞得自西域一条 - Refers to a passage in Zhao Yanwei's *Yunlu Manzhao* (a collection of notes and observations), which cites a story about the blue dye (indigo) obtained by Zhang Qian from the Western Regions.
    • 晁公武读书志称卷首有理略后有赞文 - Refers to a passage in Chao Gongwu's *Book of Reading Records*, which states that the beginning of the book contains a summary of principles, followed by a praise or commendation.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 江淹 - A famous poet and writer from the Southern Dynasties period, known for his literary contributions.
    • 张华 - A scholar and official from the Western Jin dynasty, known for his encyclopedic work *Miscellaneous Records* (*Baohu Zhi*).
    • 博物志 - Zhang Hua's encyclopedic work, covering a wide range of topics including history, geography, and folklore.
    • 齐梁 - Refers to the Qi and Liang dynasties, two of the Southern Dynasties in Chinese history.
    • 唐防要 - A historical record from the Tang dynasty, likely referring to a collection of important documents or memorials.
    • 显庆三年 - The third year of the Xianqing era (658 CE) during the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Tang.
    • 太常丞 - An official position in the Tang dynasty, responsible for rituals and ceremonies.
    • 吕才 - A scholar and official from the Tang dynasty, known for his contributions to music theory and astronomy.
    • 白雪 - A legendary musical piece, often associated with the celestial musician Su Nü.
    • 泰帝 - The emperor of the heavens, a figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 素女 - A celestial musician in Chinese mythology, often associated with music and harmony.
    • 刘褒 - A painter from the Eastern Han dynasty, known for his work *Cloud and River Picture*.
    • 汉桓帝 - Emperor Huan of Han, who reigned from 146 to 168 CE.
    • 李善 - A scholar from the Tang dynasty, known for his annotations on the *Wen Xuan*.
    • 文选 - A collection of literary works compiled by Xiao Tong during the Liang dynasty.
    • 张骞 - A famous explorer and diplomat from the Han dynasty, known for his missions to Central Asia.
    • 大夏 - An ancient kingdom in Central Asia, corresponding to modern-day Afghanistan.
    • 大宛 - An ancient kingdom in Central Asia, corresponding to modern-day Kyrgyzstan and Tajikistan.
    • 蒲陶 - The Chinese word for 'grape,' which was introduced to China through the Silk Road.
    • 赵彦衞 - A scholar from the Southern Song dynasty, known for his work *Yunlu Manzhao*.
    • 晁公武 - A scholar from the Southern Song dynasty, known for his *Book of Reading Records*.
    • 太平广记 - A vast collection of stories and anecdotes compiled during the Song dynasty.
    • 云麓漫钞 - A collection of notes and observations by Zhao Yanwei.

    A third of the entries are missing. Jiang Yan's Praise for an Ancient Bronze Sword quotes Zhang Hua's Miscellaneous Records, stating that the skill of casting copper cannot be found again, only occasionally understood in Shu and Qiang regions. The current version lacks this phrase, proving it is not the version seen during the Qi and Liang dynasties. The Tang Collection of Important Documents records that in the third year of the Xianqing era, the official Lü Cai submitted a report based on Zhang Hua's Miscellaneous Records, which states that "White Snow" is a song played by the celestial musician Su Nü on a five-stringed instrument, commissioned by the emperor of the heavens, and because of its high pitch, few people can follow along. Zhang Yanyuan's Records of Famous Paintings Through the Ages quotes Zhang Hua's Miscellaneous Records, saying that Liu Bao, a painter during the reign of Emperor Huan of Han, once painted the Cloud and River Picture, and people who saw it felt uncomfortable; he also painted the Northern Wind Picture, and people who saw it felt cool. The current version lacks these phrases. Li Shan's annotations on the Wen Xuan quote twelve passages from Zhang Hua's Miscellaneous Records, nine of which are found in the current version. The Commentary on the Western Capital Ode quotes the terms "prince and nobleman," which are words used to address noble young men in ancient times. The Commentary on the Ode on Seclusion quotes Zhang Qian's mission to Da Xia, where he brought back pomegranates, and Li Guangli's campaign against Duyu, where he obtained grapes. The Commentary on the Seven Commandments quotes a description of oranges, which are similar to tangerines but different from pomelos, with a fragrant aroma. The current version lacks these phrases. The Northern Circuit Record quotes five passages from Miscellaneous Records, three of which are found in the current version: one about the owl, also known as the chicken owl, and one about the goldfish brain containing flour gold, which comes from the kingdom of Gongpasa. The Taiping Guangji quotes a passage from Miscellaneous Records about Zheng Hong brewing wine in Shenchang River. Zhao Yanwei's Yunlu Manzhao quotes a passage from Miscellaneous Records about the blue dye (indigo) obtained by Zhang Qian from the Western Regions. The current version lacks these phrases. Chao Gongwu's Book of Reading Records states that the beginning of the book contains a summary of principles, followed by a praise or commendation. The current version lists the first entry as a geographical summary, starting from the Wei dynasty. [2]

    氏日以前云云无所谓理略赞文惟地理有之亦不在卷后又赵与旹宾退录称张华博物志卷末载湘夫人事亦误以为尧女今本此条乃在八卷之首不在卷末皆相矛盾则并非宋人所见之本或原书防佚好事者掇取诸书所引博物志而杂采他小説以足之故证以艺文聚太平御览所引亦往往相符其余为他书所未引者则大抵剽剟大戴礼春秋繁露孔子家语本草经山海经拾遗记搜神记异苑西京杂记汉武内传列子诸书饾饤成帙不尽华之原文也又
    　　刘昭续汉志注律厯志引博物记一条舆服志引博物记一条五行志引博物记二条郡国志引博物记二十九条齐东野语引其中日南野女一条谓博物记当是秦汉间古书张华取其名而为志杨慎丹铅录亦称据后汉书注博物记乃唐所作今观裴松之三国志注引博物志四条又于魏志凉茂传中引博物记一条灼然二书更无疑义此本惟载江河水赤一条又载汉末闗中女子及范明友奴发冡重生一条而分为两条又载 [3]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [3]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 氏日以前云云无所谓理略赞文惟地理有之亦不在卷后 - Elliptical phrase suggesting that certain ideas or comments made before a certain date are not particularly meaningful or relevant, and that geographical references are only found at the end of the text.
    • 今本此条乃在八卷之首不在卷末皆相矛盾 - Elliptical phrase stating that in the current edition, the entry is at the beginning of the eighth volume, not at the end, and that this contradicts earlier accounts.
    • 则并非宋人所见之本或原书防佚好事者掇取诸书所引博物志而杂采他小説以足之 - Elliptical phrase suggesting that the current version is not the one seen by Song dynasty scholars, and that it may be a compilation of references from various books, mixed with other short stories to make up the text.
    • 故证以艺文类聚太平御览所引亦往往相符 - Elliptical phrase indicating that when compared to references in *Yi Wen Le Ji* and *Tai Ping Yu Lian*, the current version often matches.
    • 其余为他书所未引者则大抵剽剟大戴礼春秋繁露孔子家语本草经山海经拾遗记搜神记异苑西京杂记汉武内传列子诸书饾饤成帙不尽华之原文也 - Elliptical phrase suggesting that other parts of the text, which are not cited in other books, are mostly taken from various sources such as *Da Dai Li*, *Chun Qiu Fan Lu*, *Kong Zi Jia Yu*, *Ben Cao Jing*, *Shan Hai Jing*, *Shi Yi Ji*, *Suo Shen Ji*, *Yi Yuan*, *Xi Jing Za Ji*, *Han Wu Ne Chuan*, and *Lie Zi*, and are not entirely original to Zhang Hua.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 赵与旹宾退录 - A collection of anecdotes and historical notes compiled by Zhao Yushi.
    • 张华博物志 - *Wu Bo Zhi* (博物志), a famous encyclopedic work compiled by Zhang Hua during the Jin dynasty.
    • 湘夫人 - A figure from Chinese mythology, often associated with the Xiang River.
    • 尧女 - The daughter of Yao, a legendary Chinese emperor.
    • 艺文类聚 - *Yi Wen Le Ji* (艺文类聚), a large anthology of literature compiled during the Sui dynasty.
    • 太平御览 - *Tai Ping Yu Lian* (太平御览), a massive compendium of knowledge compiled during the Song dynasty.
    • 大戴礼 - *Da Dai Li* (大戴礼), a collection of ritual texts.
    • 春秋繁露 - *Chun Qiu Fan Lu* (春秋繁露), a philosophical work by Dong Zhongshu.
    • 孔子家语 - *Kong Zi Jia Yu* (孔子家语), a collection of sayings and stories about Confucius.
    • 本草经 - *Ben Cao Jing* (本草经), an early pharmacological text.
    • 山海经 - *Shan Hai Jing* (山海经), an ancient Chinese text describing mythical creatures and geography.
    • 拾遗记 - *Shi Yi Ji* (拾遗记), a collection of historical and mythological anecdotes.
    • 搜神记 - *Suo Shen Ji* (搜神记), a collection of stories about gods and spirits.
    • 异苑 - *Yi Yuan* (异苑), a collection of strange and unusual events.
    • 西京杂记 - *Xi Jing Za Ji* (西京杂记), a collection of anecdotes about the Western Capital (Chang'an).
    • 汉武内传 - *Han Wu Ne Chuan* (汉武内传), a text about Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 列子 - *Lie Zi* (列子), a philosophical text attributed to Lie Yukou.
    • 刘昭续汉志 - *Xu Han Zhi* (续汉志), a continuation of the *Han Shu* (汉书) by Liu Zhao.
    • 齐东野语 - *Qi Dong Ye Yu* (齐东野语), a collection of anecdotes and historical notes.
    • 杨慎丹铅录 - *Dan Qian Lu* (丹铅录), a collection of notes and annotations by Yang Shen.

    The text you provided is quite complex and contains many references that are not easily translatable without additional context. However, I will do my best to provide a faithful translation based on the information available.

    Translation:

    Before the time of the "氏" (a reference to a specific date or era), the comments made were not particularly meaningful or relevant, and geographical references were only found at the end of the text. Zhao Yushi's Bin Tui Lu mentions that Zhang Hua's Wu Bo Zhi records the story of Xiang's wife, but mistakenly identifies her as the daughter of Yao. In the current edition, this entry is at the beginning of the eighth volume, not at the end, and this contradicts earlier accounts. Therefore, this is not the version seen by Song dynasty scholars, or perhaps the original book was lost, and good-hearted people compiled references from various books, mixed with other short stories to make up the text. When compared to references in Yi Wen Le Ji and Tai Ping Yu Lian, the current version often matches. Other parts of the text, which are not cited in other books, are mostly taken from various sources such as Da Dai Li, Chun Qiu Fan Lu, Kong Zi Jia Yu, Ben Cao Jing, Shan Hai Jing, Shi Yi Ji, Suo Shen Ji, Yi Yuan, Xi Jing Za Ji, Han Wu Ne Chuan, and Lie Zi, and are not entirely original to Zhang Hua.

    Liu Zhao's Xu Han Zhi references Wu Bo Ji in several sections: one entry in the Lüli (律历志) section, one in the Yufu (舆服志) section, two in the Wuxing (五行志) section, and twenty-nine in the Jun Guo (郡国志) section. Qi Dong Ye Yu mentions one entry about a woman from the southern region of Nanyang, suggesting that Wu Bo Ji is an ancient book from the Qin and Han periods, and that Zhang Hua used its name to create his own work. Dan Qian Lu also suggests that Wu Bo Ji was compiled during the Tang dynasty based on annotations in the Hou Han Shu (后汉书). When we look at the references in Pei Songzhi's San Guo Zhi (三国志) annotations, there are four entries from Wu Bo Zhi, and another entry in the Liang Mao (凉茂传) section of the Wei Shi (魏志), clearly indicating that these two books are beyond doubt. This version only includes one entry about the red water of the Yangtze and Han rivers, and another entry about a woman from Guanzhong at the end of the Han dynasty and a slave of Fan Mingyou who was reborn after being exhumed, dividing it into two entries. [3]

    日南野女一条讹羣行不见夫句为羣行见丈夫讹其状皛且白句为状晶目其余三十一条则悉遗漏岂非偶于他书见此三条以博物二字相同不辨为两书而贸贸采入乎至于杂记下所载豫章衣冠人有数妇一条乃隋书地理志之文唐人所撰华何自见之尤杂合成编之明证矣书中间有成句或称卢氏或称周日用案文献通考载周卢注博物志六卷此所载寥寥数条殆非完本或亦后人偶为摘附欤乾隆四十六年六月恭校上
    　　总纂官【臣】纪昀【臣】陆锡熊【臣】孙士毅
    　　总　校　官【臣】陆　费　墀

    　　钦定四库全书
    　　博物志卷一
    　　晋　张华　撰
    　　余视山海经及禹贡尔雅説文地志虽曰悉备各有所不载者作畧説出所不见麄言逺方陈山川位吉凶有征诸国境界犬牙相入春秋之后并相侵伐其土地不可具详其山川地泽畧而言之正国十二博物之士览而鉴焉 [4]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [4]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 日南野女 - Literal phrase meaning 'wild women of the southern sun,' likely referring to a specific group or region.
    • 群行不见夫 - Phrase describing a group of women traveling without a husband, possibly metaphorical for independence or social status.
    • 群行见丈夫 - Phrase describing a group of women traveling with a husband, contrasting with '群行不见夫'.
    • 其状皛且白 - Describes the appearance as 'bright and white,' which could be a physical description or a metaphor for purity.
    • 其状晶目 - Describes the eyes as 'crystal clear,' suggesting physical or metaphorical clarity.
    • 杂记下所载豫章衣冠人有数妇 - Indicates a record of several women from the region of Yuzhang (present-day Nanchang, Jiangxi) who were part of the gentry class.
    • 隋书地理志之文 - Refers to the 'Geographical Records' section of the *Sui Shu* (History of the Sui Dynasty).
    • 唐人所撰华何自见之 - Indicates that the text was compiled by Tang dynasty scholars, possibly from various sources.
    • 尤杂合成编之明证矣 - Suggests that the compilation is a clear example of a mixed and compiled work.
    • 书中间有成句或称卢氏或称周日用 - Indicates that some parts of the book are attributed to Lu Shi or Zhou Rizhong.
    • 文献通考载周卢注博物志六卷 - Refers to the *Wenxian Tongkao* (Comprehensive Examination of Literature and History), which mentions a six-volume commentary on *Bamboo Annals* by Zhou and Lu.
    • 此所载寥寥数条殆非完本 - Suggests that the current version is incomplete, containing only a few entries.
    • 或亦后人偶为摘附欤 - Proposes that some entries might have been added later by others.
    • 乾隆四十六年六月恭校上 - Indicates the date and the person responsible for the final proofreading and submission.
    • 总纂官【臣】纪昀【臣】陆锡熊【臣】孙士毅 - Lists the officials responsible for compiling the text.
    • 总校官【臣】陆费墀 - Lists the official responsible for the final proofreading.
    • 钦定四库全书 - Refers to the *Four Treasuries*, a large collection of Chinese literature compiled during the Qing dynasty.
    • 晋张华撰 - Indicates that the original text was written by Zhang Hua of the Jin dynasty.
    • 余视山海经及禹贡尔雅说文地志虽曰悉备各有所不载者作略说出所不见麄言远方陈山川位臘吉凶有征诸国境界犬牙相入春秋之后并相侵伐其土地不可具详其山川地泽略而言之正国十二博物之士览而鉴焉 - A long passage summarizing the scope and purpose of the text, emphasizing its coverage of various regions and the importance of geographical and historical knowledge.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 日南 - Refers to the southernmost part of ancient China, often associated with the region south of the Yangtze River.
    • 野女 - Refers to women living in the countryside or rural areas.
    • 豫章 - Refers to the region of Yuzhang, present-day Nanchang, Jiangxi province.
    • 衣冠人 - Refers to members of the gentry class, indicating a higher social status.
    • 隋书地理志 - Refers to the 'Geographical Records' section of the *Sui Shu* (History of the Sui Dynasty), a historical text.
    • 唐人所撰 - Indicates that the text was compiled by scholars of the Tang dynasty.
    • 文献通考 - Refers to the *Wenxian Tongkao* (Comprehensive Examination of Literature and History), a historical text.
    • 博物志 - Refers to the *Bamboo Annals*, a historical text.
    • 晋张华 - Indicates that the original text was written by Zhang Hua of the Jin dynasty.
    • 山海经 - Refers to the *Shan Hai Jing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas), an ancient Chinese text describing mythical creatures and places.
    • 禹贡 - Refers to the *Yu Gong* (Tribute of Yu), a chapter in the *Shang Shu* (Book of Documents) that describes the division of China into nine provinces.
    • 尔雅 - Refers to the *Er Ya*, an ancient Chinese dictionary.
    • 说文地志 - Refers to the *Shuo Wen* (Explanation of Characters) and *Di Zhi* (Geographical Records), which are important texts on Chinese geography and etymology.
    • 正国十二 - Refers to the twelve major states or regions in ancient China.

    Proper Nouns

    • 日南 - Southernmost part of ancient China.
    • 豫章 - Region of Yuzhang (present-day Nanchang, Jiangxi).
    • 隋书地理志 - 'Geographical Records' section of the *Sui Shu* (History of the Sui Dynasty).
    • 唐人 - Scholars of the Tang dynasty.
    • 文献通考 - *Wenxian Tongkao* (Comprehensive Examination of Literature and History).
    • 博物志 - *Bamboo Annals*.
    • 晋张华 - Zhang Hua of the Jin dynasty.
    • 山海经 - *Shan Hai Jing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas).
    • 禹贡 - *Yu Gong* (Tribute of Yu).
    • 尔雅 - *Er Ya* (Dictionary).
    • 说文地志 - *Shuo Wen* (Explanation of Characters) and *Di Zhi* (Geographical Records).
    • 正国十二 - Twelve major states or regions in ancient China.
    • 纪昀 - Ji Yun, a Qing dynasty scholar and official.
    • 陆锡熊 - Lu Xixiong, a Qing dynasty scholar and official.
    • 孙士毅 - Sun Shiyi, a Qing dynasty scholar and official.
    • 陆费墀 - Lu Feicheng, a Qing dynasty official.
    • 钦定四库全书 - *Four Treasuries*.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. [4]

    地理畧自魏氏日已前夏禹治四方而制之
    　　河图括地曰地南北三亿三万五千五百里地部之位起形高大者有昆仑山广万里高万一千里神物之所生圣人仙人之所集也出五色云气五色流水其泉南流入中国名曰河也其山中应于天最居中八十城布绕之中国东南隅居其一分是偏域也
    　　中国之域左濵海右通流沙方而言之万五千里东至蓬莱西至陇右后跨京北前及衡岳尧舜时万里三代时七千里亦无常随德优劣也
    　　尧别九州舜为十二
    　　秦前有蓝田之镇后有胡苑之塞左崤函右陇蜀西通流沙险阻之国也
    　　蜀汉之土与秦同域南跨卭笮北阻襃斜西即隈碍隔以劒阁穷险极峻独守之国也
    　　周在中区西阻崤谷东望荆山南面少室北背太岳三河之分雷风所起四险之国也
    　　魏前枕黄河背漳水瞻王屋望梁山有蓝田之寳浮池之渊也
    　　赵东临九州西瞻恒岳有沃瀑之流飞狐井陉之险至于颍阳涿鹿之野 [5]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [5]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 地理畧 - A concise term meaning 'a brief account of geography.'
    • 自魏氏日已前 - Refers to the period before the Wei dynasty, with '魏氏' meaning the Wei dynasty.
    • 夏禹治四方而制之 - Describes the legendary Emperor Yu the Great's governance over the four directions.
    • 河圖括地 - Refers to the 'Book of the Yellow River Map,' a legendary text on geography.
    • 地南北三億三萬五千五百里 - A hyperbolic description of the north-south extent of the land.
    • 地部之位起形高大者有昆仑山 - Describes the prominent geographical feature of Mount Kunlun.
    • 廣万里高万一千里 - Describes the vastness and height of Mount Kunlun.
    • 神物之所生圣人仙人之所集也 - Indicates that Mount Kunlun is a place where divine beings and sages gather.
    • 出五色云气五色流水 - Describes the colorful clouds and waters emanating from Mount Kunlun.
    • 其泉南流入中国名曰河也 - Describes the southern flow of the river from Mount Kunlun into China, named 'River' (referring to the Yellow River).
    • 其山中应于天最居中八十城布绕之 - Describes the central position of Mount Kunlun, surrounded by eighty cities.
    • 中国东南隅居其一分是偏域也 - Indicates that China occupies one part of the southeastern corner of the world.
    • 中国之域左滨海右通流沙 - Describes the geographical features of China, with the sea on the left and the desert on the right.
    • 方而言之万五千里 - Describes the square extent of China as 15,000 li.
    • 东至蓬莱西至陇右后跨京北前及衡岳 - Describes the eastern and western boundaries of China, with specific locations mentioned.
    • 尧舜时万里三代时七千里 - Describes the changing extent of China during different dynastic periods.
    • 秦前有蓝田之镇后有胡苑之塞 - Describes the geographical features of the Qin region, with specific locations mentioned.
    • 左崤函右陇蜀西通流沙 - Describes the geographical features of the Qin region, with specific locations mentioned.
    • 蜀汉之土与秦同域 - Indicates that the land of Shu and Han is similar to that of Qin.
    • 南跨卭笮北阻襃斜西即隈碍隔以劒阁穷险极峻 - Describes the geographical features of the Shu and Han region, with specific locations mentioned.
    • 周在中区西阻崤谷东望荆山南面少室北背太岳 - Describes the geographical features of the Zhou region, with specific locations mentioned.
    • 三河之分雷风所起 - Describes the geographical features of the Zhou region, with specific locations mentioned.
    • 魏前枕黄河背漳水瞻王屋望梁山 - Describes the geographical features of the Wei region, with specific locations mentioned.
    • 有蓝田之寳浮池之渊 - Describes the natural resources and geographical features of the Wei region, with specific locations mentioned.
    • 赵东临九州西瞻恒岳 - Describes the geographical features of the Zhao region, with specific locations mentioned.
    • 有沃瀑之流飞狐井陉之险 - Describes the geographical features of the Zhao region, with specific locations mentioned.
    • 至于颍阳涿鹿之野 - Describes the geographical features of the Zhao region, with specific locations mentioned.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 夏禹 - Refers to the legendary Emperor Yu the Great, known for his flood control efforts and governance.
    • 河图括地 - Refers to the 'Book of the Yellow River Map,' a legendary text on geography.
    • 昆仑山 - A mythical mountain in Chinese mythology, often associated with divine beings and immortals.
    • 蓬莱 - A mythical island in Chinese mythology, often associated with immortality.
    • 陇右 - Refers to the western region of the Qin area.
    • 京北 - Refers to the northern region of the Qin area.
    • 衡岳 - Refers to Mount Heng, a famous mountain in southern China.
    • 蓝田 - Refers to a region known for its jade deposits.
    • 胡苑之塞 - Refers to a defensive position in the Qin region.
    • 崤函 - Refers to a mountain pass in the Qin region.
    • 陇蜀 - Refers to the western and southern regions of the Qin area.
    • 卭笮 - Refers to a region in the Shu and Han area.
    • 褒斜 - Refers to a mountain pass in the Shu and Han area.
    • 隈碍 - Refers to a difficult terrain in the Shu and Han area.
    • 劒阁 - Refers to a famous mountain pass in the Shu and Han area.
    • 周 - Refers to the Zhou dynasty.
    • 崤谷 - Refers to a mountain pass in the Zhou region.
    • 荆山 - Refers to a mountain in the Zhou region.
    • 少室 - Refers to a mountain in the Zhou region.
    • 太岳 - Refers to a mountain in the Zhou region.
    • 三河 - Refers to the three rivers (Yellow River, Wei River, and Fen River) in the Zhou region.
    • 魏 - Refers to the Wei dynasty.
    • 黄河 - Refers to the Yellow River.
    • 漳水 - Refers to the Zhang River.
    • 王屋 - Refers to Mount Wangwu, a famous mountain in the Wei region.
    • 梁山 - Refers to Mount Liang, a famous mountain in the Wei region.
    • 赵 - Refers to the Zhao state.
    • 九州 - Refers to the nine provinces of ancient China.
    • 恒岳 - Refers to Mount Heng, a famous mountain in the Zhao region.
    • 沃瀑之流 - Refers to a waterfall in the Zhao region.
    • 飞狐井陉之险 - Refers to a difficult terrain in the Zhao region.
    • 颍阳 - Refers to a region in the Zhao area.
    • 涿鹿 - Refers to a region in the Zhao area.

    A Brief Account of Geography

    Before the Wei dynasty, Emperor Yu the Great governed the four directions and established the land. The "Book of the Yellow River Map" states that the land extends north-south for three hundred million three thousand five hundred and fifty li. Among the prominent geographical features, Mount Kunlun stands out, spanning ten thousand li in width and one hundred thousand li in height. It is the birthplace of divine beings and the gathering place of sages and immortals. From this mountain, five-colored clouds and waters flow, and its southern springs flow into China, known as the "River" (referring to the Yellow River). The mountain is centrally located, with eighty cities surrounding it, and China occupies one part of the southeastern corner of the world.

    The domain of China lies on the left by the sea and on the right by the desert. In terms of square extent, it measures fifteen thousand li, extending east to Penglai and west to Longyou, and spanning the northern and southern regions. During the reigns of Yao and Shun, it measured ten thousand li, and during the Three Dynasties, it measured seven thousand li, varying with the moral quality of the rulers.

    Emperor Yao divided the land into nine provinces, while Emperor Shun divided it into twelve.

    The Qin region is bordered by the Lantian stronghold to the front and the Huyuan barrier to the rear. To the left are the Xiaohan and Hangu passes, and to the right are the Long and Shu regions, with the desert to the west. It is a land of perilous terrain.

    The land of Shu and Han is similar to that of Qin, extending south to Qiongzhuo and north to Baoshan, with the difficult terrain of the Jianmen Pass to the west. It is a land of extreme danger and height, suitable for defense.

    The Zhou region lies in the central area, with the Xiaogou pass to the west, and facing the Jingshan to the east, the Shaoshi to the south, and the Taiyue to the north. It is a land divided by the Sanhe (three rivers) and where thunder and wind originate, a land of four dangers.

    The Wei region lies with the Yellow River to the front and the Zhang River to the back, facing Wangwu and looking toward Liangshan. It possesses the treasures of Lantian and the deep pools of Fucheng.

    The Zhao region lies with the nine provinces to the east and the Hengshan to the west, with the waterfall of Wobu and the perilous terrain of Feihuxing. It extends to the plains of Yingle and Zhuolu. [5]

    燕却背沙漠进临易水西至军都东至于辽长蛇带塞险陆相乘也
    　　齐南有长城巨防阳闗之险北有河济足以为固越海而东通于九夷西界岱岳配林之坂险固之国也
    　　鲁前有淮水后有岱岳防羽之向洙泗之流大埜广土曲阜尼丘
    　　宋北有泗水南迄睢濄有孟潴之泽砀山之塞也楚后背方城前及衡岳左则彭蠡右则九疑有江汉之流实险阻之国也
    　　南越之国与楚为邻五岭已前至于南海海之交趾之土谓之南裔
    　　呉左洞庭右彭蠡后濵长江南至豫章水戒险阻之国也
    　　东越通海处南北尾闾之间三江流入南海通东瓯山高海深险絶之国也
    　　衞南跨于河北得洪水南过汉上左通鲁泽右指黎山
    　　赞曰
    　　地理广大　四海八方　遐逺别域　畧以难详侯王设险　守国保疆　逺遮川塞　近备城隍司察奸非　禁御不良　勿恃危阨　恣其淫荒无德则败　有德则昌　安屋犹惧　乃可不亡进用忠良　社稷永康　教民以孝　舜化以彰地 [6]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [6]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 却背沙漠 - Turns one's back on the desert.
    • 进临易水 - Approaches the Yi River.
    • 长蛇带塞 - Describes a snake-like formation winding through perilous terrain.
    • 长城巨防 - Great Wall and other fortifications.
    • 阳闍之险 - Refers to the dangers associated with the Yangguan Pass.
    • 河济 - Yellow River and Ji River.
    • 九夷 - Nine eastern barbarian tribes.
    • 岱岳 - Mount Tai.
    • 配林之坂 - Forest-covered slopes.
    • 淮水 - Huai River.
    • 防羽 - A river.
    • 洙泗之流 - Refers to the flow of the Shu and Si rivers.
    • 大埜广土 - Vast and fertile land.
    • 曲阜尼丘 - Qufu, the hometown of Confucius.
    • 泗水 - Si River.
    • 睢濄 - Su River.
    • 孟潴之泽 - A lake.
    • 砀山 - A mountain.
    • 方城 - Fangcheng.
    • 衡岳 - Mount Heng.
    • 彭蠡 - Poyang Lake.
    • 九疑 - The Nine Peaks.
    • 江汉之流 - Yangtze and Han Rivers.
    • 五岭 - Five Ridges.
    • 南海 - South Sea.
    • 海之 - Coast.
    • 交趾 - Jiaozhi.
    • 南裔 - Southern frontier.
    • 洞庭 - Dongting Lake.
    • 豫章 - Yuzhang.
    • 三江 - Three rivers.
    • 东瓯 - Dongou.
    • 险絶 - Extremely dangerous.
    • 河北 - North bank of the Yellow River.
    • 洪水 - Yellow River.
    • 汉上 - Upper reaches of the Han River.
    • 鲁泽 - Luzhe.
    • 黎山 - Lishan.
    • 赞曰 - Traditional Chinese way of introducing a concluding statement or poem.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 燕 - Ancient Chinese state.
    • 齐 - Ancient Chinese state.
    • 鲁 - Ancient Chinese state.
    • 宋 - Ancient Chinese state.
    • 楚 - Ancient Chinese state.
    • 南越 - Ancient Chinese state.
    • 呉 - Ancient Chinese state.
    • 东越 - Ancient Chinese state.
    • 衞 - Ancient Chinese state.
    • 易水 - Yi River.
    • 军都 - An ancient name for Beijing.
    • 辽 - Liaodong.
    • 长城 - Great Wall.
    • 阳闍 - Yangguan Pass.
    • 河济 - Yellow River and Ji River.
    • 九夷 - Nine eastern barbarian tribes.
    • 岱岳 - Mount Tai.
    • 淮水 - Huai River.
    • 防羽 - A river.
    • 洙泗之流 - Refers to the flow of the Shu and Si rivers.
    • 大埜广土 - Vast and fertile land.
    • 曲阜尼丘 - Qufu, the hometown of Confucius.
    • 泗水 - Si River.
    • 睢濄 - Su River.
    • 孟潴之泽 - A lake.
    • 砀山 - A mountain.
    • 方城 - Fangcheng.
    • 衡岳 - Mount Heng.
    • 彭蠡 - Poyang Lake.
    • 九疑 - The Nine Peaks.
    • 江汉之流 - Yangtze and Han Rivers.
    • 五岭 - Five Ridges.
    • 南海 - South Sea.
    • 海之 - Coast.
    • 交趾 - Jiaozhi.
    • 南裔 - Southern frontier.
    • 洞庭 - Dongting Lake.
    • 豫章 - Yuzhang.
    • 三江 - Three rivers.
    • 东瓯 - Dongou.
    • 险絶 - Extremely dangerous.
    • 河北 - North bank of the Yellow River.
    • 洪水 - Yellow River.
    • 汉上 - Upper reaches of the Han River.
    • 鲁泽 - Luzhe.
    • 黎山 - Lishan.
    • 赞曰 - Traditional Chinese way of introducing a concluding statement or poem.

    Proper Nouns

    • 燕 - Ancient Chinese state.
    • 齐 - Ancient Chinese state.
    • 鲁 - Ancient Chinese state.
    • 宋 - Ancient Chinese state.
    • 楚 - Ancient Chinese state.
    • 南越 - Ancient Chinese state.
    • 呉 - Ancient Chinese state.
    • 东越 - Ancient Chinese state.
    • 衞 - Ancient Chinese state.
    • 易水 - Yi River.
    • 军都 - An ancient name for Beijing.
    • 辽 - Liaodong.
    • 长城 - Great Wall.
    • 阳闍 - Yangguan Pass.
    • 河济 - Yellow River and Ji River.
    • 九夷 - Nine eastern barbarian tribes.
    • 岱岳 - Mount Tai.
    • 淮水 - Huai River.
    • 防羽 - A river.
    • 洙泗之流 - Refers to the flow of the Shu and Si rivers.
    • 大埜广土 - Vast and fertile land.
    • 曲阜尼丘 - Qufu, the hometown of Confucius.
    • 泗水 - Si River.
    • 睢濄 - Su River.
    • 孟潴之泽 - A lake.
    • 砀山 - A mountain.
    • 方城 - Fangcheng.
    • 衡岳 - Mount Heng.
    • 彭蠡 - Poyang Lake.
    • 九疑 - The Nine Peaks.
    • 江汉之流 - Yangtze and Han Rivers.
    • 五岭 - Five Ridges.
    • 南海 - South Sea.
    • 海之 - Coast.
    • 交趾 - Jiaozhi.
    • 南裔 - Southern frontier.
    • 洞庭 - Dongting Lake.
    • 豫章 - Yuzhang.
    • 三江 - Three rivers.
    • 东瓯 - Dongou.
    • 险絶 - Extremely dangerous.
    • 河北 - North bank of the Yellow River.
    • 洪水 - Yellow River.
    • 汉上 - Upper reaches of the Han River.
    • 鲁泽 - Luzhe.
    • 黎山 - Lishan.
    • 赞曰 - Traditional Chinese way of introducing a concluding statement or poem.

    The Yan region, turning its back on the desert and approaching the Yi River, extends westward to Jundu (an ancient name for Beijing) and eastward to Liaodong. Its terrain is like a long snake winding through dangerous lands.

    The Qi state has the Great Wall and other fortifications to the south, with the Yangguan Pass and the Yellow and Ji Rivers to the north, providing ample defenses. It crosses the sea to the east, connecting with the nine eastern barbarian tribes, and is bordered by Mount Tai and forest-covered slopes to the west, making it a well-fortified state.

    The Lu state has the Huai River to the front and Mount Tai to the rear, with the Fangyu and Zisun rivers flowing through it. Its vast and fertile land includes Qufu, the hometown of Confucius.

    The Song state has the Si River to the north and the Su River to the south, with a lake and the Dangshan mountains providing additional defenses. The Chu state has Fangcheng to the rear and Mount Heng to the front, with Poyang Lake to the left and the Nine Peaks to the right, all bordered by the Yangtze and Han Rivers, making it a strategically difficult state.

    The Southern Yue state, neighboring Chu, extends to the South Sea, with its coastal and Jiaozhi territories being known as the southern frontier.

    The Wu state has Dongting Lake to the left and Poyang Lake to the right, with the Yangtze River to the rear and Yuzhang to the south, making it a state with natural barriers.

    The Eastern Yue state, connecting to the sea between the northern and southern outlets, has the three rivers flowing into the South Sea, connecting with Dongou, a state with high mountains and deep seas, making it extremely dangerous.

    The Wei state, spanning the north bank of the Yellow River, has the Yellow River to the south, crossing the upper reaches of the Han River, with Luzhe to the left and Lishan to the right.

    In praise:

    The geography is vast, encompassing the four seas and eight directions. The distant and separate domains are difficult to fully detail. The feudal lords set up defenses to protect their states and borders. They guard the rivers and passes from afar and prepare the city walls nearby. They inspect and punish evil, prevent and control the wicked, and do not rely on dangerous terrain to indulge in debauchery. Without virtue, they will fail; with virtue, they will flourish. Even in a secure position, they remain cautious, thus avoiding destruction. By promoting loyal and virtuous officials, the state will be forever peaceful. By teaching the people filial piety, the influence of Shun will be evident. [6]

    天地初不足故女娲氏炼五色石以补其阙断鼇足以立四极其后共工氏与颛顼争帝而怒触不周之山折天柱絶地维故天后倾西北日月星辰就焉地不满东南故百川水注焉
    　　昆仑山北地转下三千六百里有八幽都方二十万里地下有四柱四柱广十万里地有三千六百轴犬牙相举
    　　泰山一曰天孙言爲天帝孙也主召人魂魄东方万物始成知人生命之长短
    　　考灵曜曰地有四游冬至地上北而西三万里夏至地下南而东三万里春秋二分其中矣地常动不止譬如人在舟而坐舟行而人不觉七戎六蛮九夷八狄经总而言之谓之四海言皆近海海之言晦昬无所覩也
    　　地以名山为辅佐石为之骨川为之脉草木为之毛土为之肉三尺以上为粪三尺以下为地
    　　山
    　　五岳华岱恒衡嵩
    　　按北太行山而北去不知山所限极处亦如东海不知所穷尽也 [7]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [7]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 天地初不足故 - At the beginning, the heavens and earth were insufficient, indicating a state of incompleteness or deficiency.
    • 女娲氏炼五色石以补其阙 - Nüwa smelted five-colored stones to fill its gaps, referring to the mythological act of Nüwa patching the sky with stones.
    • 断鼇足以立四极 - Cutting the legs of the turtle to establish the four corners of the earth, a mythological act to stabilize the world.
    • 共工氏与颛顼争帝而怒触不周之山 - Gonggong and Zuanxu fought for the throne, and in anger, Gonggong collided with the Mountain of Non-Obstruction, a mythological event causing the collapse of the sky pillar.
    • 折天柱絶地维 - Breaking the sky pillar and severing the earth's girdle, referring to the collapse of the cosmic supports.
    • 地不满东南故百川水注焉 - The earth is not full in the southeast, so all rivers flow there, explaining the geographical phenomenon of water flowing to the southeast.
    • 昆仑山北地转下三千六百里 - Mount Kunlun's northern land turns downward for 3,600 miles, describing the mountain's vast and descending terrain.
    • 八玄幽都 - Eight Dark Hells, referring to the underworld or a place of darkness.
    • 泰山一曰天孙 - Mount Tai is also called Heavenly Grandson, indicating its divine status.
    • 考灵曜曰 - The *Kao Lingyao* says, referencing an ancient text or commentary.
    • 地有四游 - The earth has four movements, referring to the earth's seasonal shifts.
    • 七戎六蛮九夷八狄 - Seven Rong, six Man, nine Yi, and eight Di, referring to ancient ethnic groups.
    • 三尺以上为粪 - Above three feet is manure, indicating the layer of soil above which is considered waste.
    • 三尺以下为地 - Below three feet is earth, indicating the solid ground beneath the surface.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 女娲氏 - Nüwa, the mythical goddess who repaired the sky and created humans.
    • 共工氏与颛顼 - Gonggong and Zuanxu, mythological figures who fought for the throne.
    • 不周之山 - The Mountain of Non-Obstruction, a mythical mountain in Chinese mythology.
    • 昆仑山 - Mount Kunlun, a mythical mountain associated with the divine and the underworld.
    • 泰山 - Mount Tai, a sacred mountain in Chinese culture, often associated with the emperor's ritual of heaven.
    • 考灵曜 - An ancient text or commentary, possibly referring to a work on astronomy or cosmology.
    • 七戎六蛮九夷八狄 - Refers to ancient ethnic groups in China, often used to describe the diversity of peoples.
    • 五岳 - The Five Sacred Mountains (Hua, Dai, Heng, Heng, and Song), each associated with a cardinal direction and a virtue.

    Proper Nouns

    • 女娲氏 - Nüwa, the mythical goddess.
    • 共工氏 - Gonggong, the mythological figure.
    • 颛顼 - Zuanxu, the mythological figure.
    • 不周之山 - The Mountain of Non-Obstruction.
    • 昆仑山 - Mount Kunlun.
    • 泰山 - Mount Tai.
    • 考灵曜 - An ancient text or commentary.
    • 七戎六蛮九夷八狄 - Ancient ethnic groups.
    • 五岳 - The Five Sacred Mountains (Hua, Dai, Heng, Heng, and Song).

    At the beginning, the heavens and earth were insufficient, so Nüwa smelted five-colored stones to fill their gaps and cut the legs of the turtle to establish the four corners of the earth. Afterward, Gonggong and Zuanxu fought for the throne, and in anger, Gonggong collided with the Mountain of Non-Obstruction, breaking the sky pillar and severing the earth's girdle. As a result, the heavens later tilted to the northwest, and the sun, moon, and stars moved there, while the earth was not full in the southeast, so all rivers flowed there.

    Mount Kunlun's northern land turns downward for 3,600 miles, with the Eight Dark Hells spanning 20,000 miles. Beneath the ground, there are four pillars, each 10,000 miles wide, and the earth has 3,600 axes, interlocking like dog's teeth.

    Mount Tai is also called Heavenly Grandson, as it is said to be the grandson of the Heavenly Emperor, and it is the master of summoning human souls. In the east, all things begin to form, and it knows the length and duration of human life.

    The Kao Lingyao states that the earth has four movements: at the winter solstice, the earth moves north and west for 3,000 miles; at the summer solstice, it moves south and east for 3,000 miles; and during the spring and autumn equinoxes, it is in the middle. The earth is constantly in motion, like a person sitting in a boat while the boat moves, and the person does not feel it. The Seven Rong, Six Man, Nine Yi, and Eight Di, when summarized, are called the Four Seas, meaning they are all near the sea. The word "sea" refers to darkness and obscurity, where nothing can be seen.

    The earth is supported by famous mountains, which are its bones; rivers are its veins; grass and trees are its fur; and soil is its flesh. Above three feet is manure, and below three feet is earth.

    Mountains:
    The Five Sacred Mountains: Hua, Dai, Heng, Heng, and Song.

    According to the Shan Hai Jing, to the north of Mount Taihang and beyond, it is unknown where the mountain's limits end, just as it is unknown where the East Sea ends. [7]

    石者金之根甲石流精以生水水生木木含火
    　　水
    　　漠北广逺中国人尠有至北海者汉使骠骑将军霍去病北伐单于至瀚海而还有北海明矣【周日用曰余闻北海言苏武牧羊之所去部落甚迩秪一池号北海苏武牧羊当在于是耳北地见有苏武湖非北溟之海】
    　　汉使张骞渡西海至大秦西海之濵有小昆仑高万仞方八百里东海广漫未闻有渡者
    　　南海短狄未及西南夷以穷携家渡南海至交趾者不絶也
    　　史记封禅书云威宣燕昭违人乘舟入海有蓬莱方丈瀛州三神山神人所集欲采仙药盖言先有至之者其鸟兽皆白金银为宫阙悉在渤海中去人不逺
    　　四渎河出昆仑墟江出岷山济出王屋淮出桐栢八流亦出名山渭出鸟鼠汉出嶓冢洛出熊耳泾出少室汝出燕泉泗出陪尾沔出月台汶出泰山水有五色有浊有清汝南有黄水华山有黒水泞水渊或生明珠而岸不枯山泽通气以兴雷云气触石肤寸而合不崇朝以雨
    　　江河水赤名曰泣血道路惊骇山河为墟也 [8]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [8]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 石者金之根 - Metaphorical statement meaning 'stone is the root of gold,' suggesting that stone is the source or foundation of gold.
    • 甲石流精以生水 - Poetic way of saying 'stone produces or generates water.'
    • 水生木木含火 - Chain of metaphors: 'water gives birth to wood, and wood contains fire,' symbolizing the interconnectedness of elements in nature.
    • 漠北广逺 - Refers to the vast and remote northern desert.
    • 中国人尠有至北海者 - Indicates the rarity of Chinese exploration in the northern seas.
    • 汉使骠骑将军霍去病北伐单于至瀚海而还有北海明矣 - Historical reference to General Huo Qubing's expedition to the Great Sea (Haihai) and the existence of the North Sea.
    • 汉使张骞渡西海至大秦西海之濵有小昆仑高万仞方八百里 - Refers to Zhang Qian's famous mission to the West and the discovery of a small Kunlun Mountain in the Western Sea.
    • 东海广漫未闻有渡者 - Indicates the difficulty of crossing the East Sea.
    • 南海短狄未及西南夷以穷携家渡南海至交趾者不絶也 - Suggests the continuous migration to the southern regions.
    • 史记封禅书云威宣燕昭违人乘舟入海有蓬莱方丈瀛州三神山神人所集欲采仙药盖言先有至之者其鸟兽皆白金银为宫阙悉在渤海中去人不逺 - Describes the legendary journey of King Yan of Yan to the mythical islands of Penglai, Fangzhang, and Yingzhou, where the immortals gather and where one can obtain elixirs of immortality.
    • 四渎河出昆仑墟江出岷山济出王屋淮出桐栢八流亦出名山 - Describes the origins of major rivers, with the Yellow River flowing from the Kunlun Mountains, the Yangtze River from Mount Minshan, the Ji River from Mount Wangwu, and the Huai River from Mount Tongbai.
    • 渭出鸟鼠汉出嶓冢洛出熊耳泾出少室汝出燕泉泗出陪尾沔出月台汶出泰山 - Continues the description of river origins, with the Wei River flowing from Mount Niaoshu, the Han River from Mount Baozhong, the Luo River from Mount Xionger, the Jing River from Mount Shaoshi, the Yu River from Yanquan, the Si River from Mount Peiwei, the Mian River from the Moon Platform, and the Wei River from Mount Tai.
    • 水有五色有浊有清汝南有黄水华山有黒水泞水渊或生明珠而岸不枯山泽通气以兴雷云气触石肤寸而合不崇朝以雨 - Poetic description of water and its properties, including the presence of pearls in muddy waters and the interconnectedness of mountains and lakes.
    • 江河水赤名曰泣血道路惊骇山河为墟也 - Vivid description of rivers and lakes turning red, called 'weeping blood,' causing roads to be filled with horror, and mountains and rivers to become ruins.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 漠北 - Refers to the vast northern desert regions of China, historically associated with the Xiongnu.
    • 北海 - The North Sea, which in Chinese historical context could refer to the Arctic Ocean or other northern waters.
    • 汉使骠骑将军霍去病 - General Huo Qubing, a famous Han dynasty military leader known for his campaigns against the Xiongnu.
    • 瀚海 - The Great Sea, often used in Chinese literature to refer to the vast northern seas.
    • 汉使张骞 - Zhang Qian, a Han dynasty envoy famous for his expeditions to the Western Regions and beyond.
    • 大秦 - The Roman Empire, as it was known to the Chinese.
    • 小昆仑 - A mythical mountain, often associated with the Kunlun Mountains in Chinese mythology.
    • 东海 - The East Sea, historically associated with the East China Sea.
    • 南海 - The South Sea, historically associated with the South China Sea.
    • 交趾 - An ancient Chinese province in southern China, corresponding to modern Vietnam.
    • 史记封禅书 - The *Book of Altar and Sacrifice* from the *Records of the Grand Historian* (Shiji), which includes accounts of legendary journeys and mythical islands.
    • 四渎 - The Four Rivers: the Yellow River, Yangtze River, Ji River, and Huai River, considered sacred in Chinese culture.
    • 昆仑墟 - The Kunlun Mountains, a mythical and sacred mountain range in Chinese mythology.
    • 汝南 - A historical region in central China.
    • 华山 - Mount Hua, one of the Five Great Mountains of China, known for its black water.
    • 山泽通气 - A concept in Chinese cosmology where mountains and lakes are believed to be interconnected and influence each other.
    • 江河水赤 - A vivid description of rivers and lakes turning red, possibly referring to a natural disaster or mythological event.

    Proper Nouns

    • 漠北 - The northern desert regions.
    • 北海 - The North Sea.
    • 汉使骠骑将军霍去病 - General Huo Qubing.
    • 瀚海 - The Great Sea.
    • 汉使张骞 - Zhang Qian.
    • 大秦 - The Roman Empire.
    • 小昆仑 - A mythical mountain.
    • 东海 - The East Sea.
    • 南海 - The South Sea.
    • 交趾 - An ancient Chinese province.
    • 史记封禅书 - The *Book of Altar and Sacrifice* from the *Records of the Grand Historian*.
    • 四渎 - The Four Rivers.
    • 昆仑墟 - The Kunlun Mountains.
    • 汝南 - A historical region.
    • 华山 - Mount Hua.
    • 山泽通气 - A concept in Chinese cosmology.
    • 江河水赤 - A vivid description of rivers and lakes turning red.

    Stone is the root of gold, and from stone, essence flows to give birth to water. Water gives birth to wood, and wood contains fire.

    Water

    The northern desert is vast and remote, and few Chinese have reached the North Sea. The Han envoy, General-in-Chief Huo Qubing, during his northern campaign against the Xiongnu, reached the Great Sea (Haihai) and returned, clearly indicating the existence of the North Sea. [Zhou Rizhong said: I have heard that the North Sea is the place where Su Wu tended sheep. The tribes there are very close, and there is only a pool called the North Sea. Su Wu tended sheep there, and the North Sea Lake in the north is not the same as the North Sea.]

    The Han envoy Zhang Qian crossed the Western Sea to the border of Da Qin (Roman Empire), where there is a small Kunlun Mountain, 10,000 feet high and 800 miles square. The Eastern Sea is vast and uncharted, with no one having crossed it.

    The southern barbarians of the South Sea are not as advanced as the southwestern tribes, but those who carry their families across the South Sea to Jiaozhi (modern Vietnam) are unceasing.

    The Records of the Grand Historian (Shiji) in the Book of Altar and Sacrifice says that King Yan of Yan, defying convention, sailed to the sea to find the mythical islands of Penglai, Fangzhang, and Yingzhou, where the immortals gather and where one can obtain elixirs of immortality. This is said to be the story of those who first reached them, where all the birds and beasts are white, and the palaces are made of gold and silver, all located in the Bohai Sea, not far from people.

    The Four Rivers: the Yellow River flows from the Kunlun Mountains, the Yangtze River from Mount Minshan, the Ji River from Mount Wangwu, and the Huai River from Mount Tongbai. The eight rivers also flow from famous mountains. The Wei River flows from Mount Niaoshu, the Han River from Mount Baozhong, the Luo River from Mount Xionger, the Jing River from Mount Shaoshi, the Yu River from Yanquan, the Si River from Mount Peiwei, the Mian River from the Moon Platform, and the Wei River from Mount Tai.

    Water has five colors, some turbid and some clear. In the southern part of the Huai River, there is yellow water; on Mount Hua, there is black water. In the muddy waters, pearls may be found, but the banks do not dry up. Mountains and lakes communicate with each other, generating thunder and clouds. When the mist touches the stone, it condenses into rain within a short time.

    The rivers and lakes are red, called "weeping blood," causing roads to be filled with horror, and mountains and rivers to become ruins. [8]

    山水总论
    　　五岳视三公四渎视诸侯诸侯赏封内名山者通灵助化位相亚也故地动臣叛名山崩王道讫川竭神去国随以亡海投九仞之鱼流水涸国之大诫也泽浮舟川水溢臣盛君衰百川沸腾山冢崒崩高岸为谷深谷为陵小人握命君子陵迟白黒不别大乱之征也
    　　援神契曰五岳之神圣四渎之精仁河者水之伯上应天汉太山天帝孙也主召人魂魄东方万物始成故知人生命之长短
    　　五方人民
    　　东方少阳日月所出山谷清其人佼好
    　　西方少隂日月所入其土窈防其人高鼻深目多毛南方太阳土下水浅其人大口多傲
    　　北方太隂土平广深其人广面缩颈
    　　中央四析风雨交山谷峻其人端正
    　　南越巢居北朔穴居避寒暑也
    　　东南之人食水产西北之人食陆畜食水产者蚌螺蛤以为珍味不觉其腥也食陆畜者狸兔鼠雀以为珍味不觉其膻也
    　　有山者采有水者渔山气多男泽气多女平衍气仁高陵气犯丛林气躄故择其所居居在高中之平下中之高则产好人 [9]

    Final Translation

    Source Text Translation
    山水总论 General Discussion of Mountains and Waters
    五岳视三公四渎视诸侯诸侯赏封内名山者通灵助化位相亚也 The Five Sacred Mountains are compared to the Three Dukes, and the Four Rivers to feudal lords. Those feudal lords who are rewarded with famous mountains within their territories are considered to be spiritually connected and assist in transformation, their status being slightly inferior to that of the Five Sacred Mountains.
    地动臣叛名山崩王道讫川竭神去国随以亡 An earthquake leads to rebellion, the collapse of a sacred mountain, the end of the king's way, the drying up of rivers, the departure of spirits, and the subsequent fall of the nation.
    海投九仞之鱼流水涸国之大诫也 If a nine-foot-long fish is thrown into the sea, the water dries up, symbolizing a major warning for the state.
    泽浮舟川水溢臣盛君衰百川沸腾山冢崒崩高岸为谷深谷为陵小人握命君子陵迟白黒不别大乱之征也 When lakes overflow with boats, rivers overflow, ministers rise and the king declines, all rivers boil, mountains collapse, high banks become valleys, deep valleys become hills, the common people hold power, the virtuous decline, black and white are no longer distinguishable, these are signs of great chaos.
    援神契曰五岳之神圣四渎之精仁河者水之伯上应天汉太山天帝孙也主召人魂魄东方万物始成故知人生命之长短 According to Yuan Shen Qi, the Five Sacred Mountains are sacred, and the Four Rivers are pure. The Yellow River is the lord of water, corresponding to the Milky Way. Mount Tai is the grandson of the Heavenly Emperor, and is responsible for summoning human souls. In the east, all things begin to form, hence it is known that the length of human life can be determined.
    五方人民 People of the Five Directions
    东方少阳日月所出山谷清其人佼好 In the east, the lesser yang, where the sun and moon rise, the valleys are clear, and the people are handsome.
    西方少阴日月所入其土窈防其人高鼻深目多毛 In the west, the lesser yin, where the sun and moon set, the land is deep and dark, and the people have high noses, deep eyes, and much hair.
    南方太阳土下水浅其人大口多傲 In the south, the greater yang, the soil is low and the water is shallow, and the people have large mouths and are often arrogant.
    北方太阴土平广深其人广面缩颈 In the north, the greater yin, the soil is flat and extensive, and the people have broad faces and short necks.
    中央四析风雨交山谷峻其人端正 In the center, the four directions intersect, where wind and rain meet, the valleys are steep, and the people are upright.
    南越巢居北朔穴居避寒暑也 In the south, people live in nests to avoid the heat, and in the north, they live in caves to avoid the cold.
    东南之人食水产西北之人食陆畜食水产者蚌螺蛤以为珍味不觉其腥也食陆畜者狸兔鼠雀以为珍味不觉其膻也 People in the southeast eat seafood, and people in the northwest eat land animals. Those who eat seafood consider turtles, clams, snails, and abalone as delicacies and do not find them fishy. Those who eat land animals consider weasels, rabbits, mice, and birds as delicacies and do not find them膻.
    有山者采有水者渔山气多男泽气多女平衍气仁高陵气犯丛林气躄故择其所居居在高中之平下中之高则产好人 Those who live near mountains gather resources, and those who live near water fish. The air of mountains is more masculine, and the air of marshes is more feminine. The air of flat and expansive areas is benevolent, and the air of high and steep areas is aggressive. The air of dense forests is weak. Therefore, when choosing a place to live, one should choose a location that is high but level, or low but elevated, to produce good people.

    This translation aims to be both faithful to the original Classical Chinese and natural in English, incorporating the insights from the pre-drafting research to handle idiomatic expressions, cultural allusions, syntactic ambiguities, and proper nouns appropriately. [9]

    居无近絶溪羣冢狐虫之所近此则死气隂匿之处也山居之民多瘿肿疾瘿由于饮泉之不流者今荆南诸山郡东多此疾肿由于践土之无卤者今江外诸山县偏多此病也【卢氏曰不然也在山南人有之北人及呉楚无此病盖南出黒水水土然也今处不流泉之界者固无此病也】
    　　物产
    　　地性含水土山泉者引地气也山有沙者生金有谷者生玉名山生神芝不死之草上芝为车马中芝为人形下芝为六畜土山多云铁山多石五土所宜黄白宜种禾黒坟宜麦黍苍赤宜菽芋下泉宜稻得其宜则利百倍
    　　和气相感则生朱草山出车泽出神马陵出黒丹阜出土怪江出大贝海出明珠人主寿昌民延寿命天下太平
    　　名山大川孔穴相纳和气所出则生石脂玉膏食之不死神龙灵行于穴中矣
    　　神宫在高石沼中有神人多麒麟灵芝神草有英泉饮之眠三百嵗乃觉不死去琅琊四万五千里
    　　三株树生赤水之上
    　　员丘山上有不死树食之乃寿有赤泉饮之不老多大蛇为人害不得居也 [10]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [10]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 居无近絶溪羣冢狐虫之所近此则死气隂匿之处也 - Describes a location near a desolate stream, cemeteries, and areas inhabited by foxes and insects, which is considered a place of death and hidden evil.
    • 山居之民多瘿肿疾 - Refers to the prevalence of goiter among mountain-dwelling people.
    • 瘿由于饮泉之不流者 - Explains that goiter is caused by drinking stagnant water.
    • 今荆南诸山郡东多此疾 - Indicates that the disease is common in the eastern part of the mountains in Jingnan.
    • 肿由于践土之无卤者 - Suggests that swelling is caused by walking on soil lacking salt.
    • 今江外诸山县偏多此病也 - States that this condition is prevalent in the counties outside the Jiang region.
    • 物产 - Refers to the products or resources of a place.
    • 地性含水土山泉者引地气也 - Describes how the nature of the land, containing water, soil, and mountain springs, attracts earthly qi (vital energy).
    • 山有沙者生金有谷者生玉 - Indicates that mountains with sand produce gold and those with valleys produce jade.
    • 名山生神芝不死之草 - Suggests that famous mountains produce divine mushrooms and immortal grass.
    • 和气相感则生朱草 - States that harmonious qi can produce red grass.
    • 山出车泽出神马 - Describes how mountains produce chariots and marshes produce divine horses.
    • 陵出黒丹阜出土怪 - Indicates that hills produce black cinnabar and mounds produce strange creatures.
    • 江出大贝海出明珠 - States that rivers produce large shells and seas produce pearls.
    • 人主寿昌民延寿命天下太平 - Suggests that the ruler will have a long life, the people will extend their lifespan, and the world will be peaceful.
    • 名山大川孔穴相纳和气所出则生石脂玉膏食之不死 - Describes how famous mountains and rivers, with their holes and caves, produce stone fat and jade oil, which, when eaten, grant immortality.
    • 神龙灵行于穴中矣 - States that divine dragons and turtles live in the caves.
    • 神宫在高石沼中有神人多麒麟灵芝神草有英泉饮之眠三百嵗乃觉不死去琅琊四万五千里 - Describes a divine palace in a high stone marsh with divine beings, many qilins, divine mushrooms, and sacred grass, and a spring that, when drunk, allows one to sleep for three hundred years and wake up immortal, located 45,000 miles from Langya.
    • 三株树生赤水之上 - States that three trees grow on the Red River.
    • 员丘山上有不死树食之乃寿有赤泉饮之不老多大蛇为人害不得居也 - Describes the Round Hill Mountain with an immortal tree that grants longevity and a red spring that prevents aging, but it is inhabited by large snakes that are harmful to humans, making it unsuitable for habitation.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 瘿肿疾 - A historical and medical reference to goiter, a condition caused by iodine deficiency.
    • 荆南 - Refers to the region of Jingnan, a historical area in southern China.
    • 江外 - Refers to areas outside the Jiang River, which historically marked the boundary of certain regions.
    • 名山大川 - Refers to famous mountains and rivers, often associated with sacred or significant locations in Chinese culture.
    • 神宫 - Refers to a divine palace, often associated with mythological or spiritual significance.
    • 琅琊 - Refers to Langya, a historical region in China known for its cultural and historical significance.
    • 赤水 - Refers to the Red River, a mythical or significant river in Chinese mythology.
    • 员丘山 - Refers to the Round Hill Mountain, a mythical or significant mountain in Chinese mythology.

    Proper Nouns

    • 荆南 - A historical region in southern China.
    • 江外 - Areas outside the Jiang River.
    • 琅琊 - A historical region in China.
    • 赤水 - The Red River.
    • 员丘山 - The Round Hill Mountain.

    One should not live near desolate streams, cemeteries, or areas inhabited by foxes and insects, as these are places of death and hidden evil. Mountain-dwelling people often suffer from goiter, which is caused by drinking stagnant water. This disease is prevalent in the eastern part of the mountains in Jingnan. Swelling is caused by walking on soil lacking salt, and this condition is common in the counties outside the Jiang region. [Lu Shi said that this is not true; people in southern mountains have this disease, while people in the north and Wu and Chu do not. This is because the south produces black water and soil. Those who live in areas with stagnant water are naturally free from this disease.]

    Products

    The nature of the land, containing water, soil, and mountain springs, attracts earthly qi. Mountains with sand produce gold, and those with valleys produce jade. Famous mountains produce divine mushrooms and immortal grass. Harmonious qi can produce red grass. Mountains produce chariots, and marshes produce divine horses. Hills produce black cinnabar, and mounds produce strange creatures. Rivers produce large shells, and seas produce pearls. The ruler will have a long life, the people will extend their lifespan, and the world will be peaceful.

    Famous mountains and rivers, with their holes and caves, produce stone fat and jade oil, which, when eaten, grant immortality. Divine dragons and turtles live in the caves.

    A divine palace is located in a high stone marsh, where divine beings, many qilins, divine mushrooms, and sacred grass can be found, along with a spring that, when drunk, allows one to sleep for three hundred years and wake up immortal, located 45,000 miles from Langya.

    Three trees grow on the Red River.

    The Round Hill Mountain has an immortal tree that grants longevity and a red spring that prevents aging, but it is inhabited by large snakes that are harmful to humans, making it unsuitable for habitation. [10]

    博物志卷一
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　博物志卷二
    　　晋　张华　撰
    　　外国
    　　西海内西北有轩辕国在穷山之际其不寿者八百嵗渚沃之埜鸾自舞民食鳯卵饮甘露
    　　白民国有乘黄状如狐背上有角乘之寿三千嵗君子国人衣冠带劒使两虎民衣野丝好礼让不争土千里多薫华之草民多疾风气故人不蕃息好让故为君子国
    　　三苗国昔唐尧以天下让于虞三苗之民非之帝征之有苗之民叛浮入南海为三苗国
    　　驩兠国其民尽似仙人帝尧司徒驩兠之后民常捕海岛中人面鸟口去南国万六千里尽似仙人也
    　　大人国其人孕三十六年生白头其儿则长大能乘云而不能走盖龙去防稽四万六千里
    　　厌光国民光出口中形尽似猿猴黒色
    　　结胸国有灭防鸟竒肱民善为拭扛以杀百禽能为飞车从风逺行汤时西风至吹其车至豫州汤破其车不以视民十年东风至乃复作车遣返而其国去玉门闗四万里 [11]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [11]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 博物志 - Title meaning 'Records of Rare Things' or 'A Book of Miscellaneous Knowledge.'
    • 卷一/卷二 - Refers to the first and second volumes of the text.
    • 钦定四库全书 - Title indicating the text is part of the *Four Treasuries*, a massive compilation of Chinese literature commissioned by the Qing dynasty.
    • 撰 - Means 'compiled' or 'authored.'
    • 西海内西北有轩辕国 - Geographical and mythological statement introducing the *Xuanyuan* (Yellow Emperor) country.
    • 穷山之际 - Refers to the remote or distant mountains, often used to describe mythical or legendary places.
    • 鸾自舞 - Poetic description of the *luan* (a mythical bird) dancing on its own, symbolizing grace and beauty.
    • 不寿者八百嵗 - Refers to a lifespan of 800 years, emphasizing longevity.
    • 渚沃之埜 - Poetic description of a fertile and abundant land.
    • 乘黄 - Mythical creature, often depicted as a horse with a single horn, symbolizing immortality.
    • 君子国 - Mythical country of virtuous people, often associated with harmony and peace.
    • 好礼让 - Refers to the practice of yielding and respecting others, a key virtue in Confucian thought.
    • 薰华之草 - Refers to fragrant and beautiful plants, often used to describe a paradise-like environment.
    • 疾风气 - Refers to a windy climate, which may affect the fertility of the land and its people.
    • 不蕃息 - Means 'not prolific' or 'not numerous,' indicating a low population.
    • 三苗国 - Mythical or historical kingdom, often associated with rebellion against the rule of the Yellow Emperor.
    • 驩兠国 - Mythical country, named after the figure *Huan Dou*, who is often depicted as a wise and virtuous ruler.
    • 仙人 - Refers to immortals or deities, often associated with longevity and supernatural abilities.
    • 大人国 - Mythical country of giants, often associated with strength and power.
    • 结胸国 - Mythical country, the name suggests a people with a particular physical characteristic or cultural trait.
    • 厌光国 - Mythical country, the name suggests a people with a particular physical characteristic or cultural trait.
    • 灭防鸟 - Mythical bird, often associated with death or destruction.
    • 竒肱民 - Refers to a people with a unique physical characteristic, often depicted as having one arm.
    • 飞车 - Mythical vehicle that can fly, often associated with immortality or supernatural abilities.
    • 玉门关 - Historical pass in the western part of China, often associated with the Silk Road and trade with the West.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 轩辕国 - The *Xuanyuan* (Yellow Emperor) country, a mythical place associated with the legendary Yellow Emperor, a key figure in Chinese mythology and history.
    • 三苗国 - The *Sanmiao* kingdom, often associated with a historical rebellion against the rule of the Yellow Emperor.
    • 驩兠国 - The *Huan Dou* country, named after the figure *Huan Dou*, who is often depicted as a wise and virtuous ruler.
    • 大人国 - The *Daren* country, a mythical place associated with giants, often symbolizing strength and power.
    • 结胸国 - The *Jietong* country, a mythical place with a unique cultural or physical characteristic.
    • 厌光国 - The *Yanguang* country, a mythical place with a unique cultural or physical characteristic.
    • 灭防鸟 - The *Miefang* bird, a mythical creature often associated with death or destruction.
    • 竒肱民 - The *Qigong* people, a mythical group with a unique physical characteristic, often depicted as having one arm.
    • 飞车 - The *Feiche* (flying chariot), a mythical vehicle associated with immortality or supernatural abilities.
    • 玉门关 - The *Yumen Pass*, a historical pass in the western part of China, often associated with the Silk Road and trade with the West.

    Proper Nouns

    • 博物志 - Title meaning 'Records of Rare Things' or 'A Book of Miscellaneous Knowledge.'
    • 钦定四库全书 - Title indicating the text is part of the *Four Treasuries*, a massive compilation of Chinese literature commissioned by the Qing dynasty.
    • 张华 - The author of the text, a prominent scholar and official during the Jin dynasty.
    • 轩辕国 - The *Xuanyuan* (Yellow Emperor) country, a mythical place associated with the legendary Yellow Emperor.
    • 三苗国 - The *Sanmiao* kingdom, often associated with a historical rebellion against the rule of the Yellow Emperor.
    • 驩兠国 - The *Huan Dou* country, named after the figure *Huan Dou*.
    • 大人国 - The *Daren* country, a mythical place associated with giants.
    • 结胸国 - The *Jietong* country, a mythical place with a unique cultural or physical characteristic.
    • 厌光国 - The *Yanguang* country, a mythical place with a unique cultural or physical characteristic.
    • 灭防鸟 - The *Miefang* bird, a mythical creature often associated with death or destruction.
    • 竒肱民 - The *Qigong* people, a mythical group with a unique physical characteristic.
    • 飞车 - The *Feiche* (flying chariot), a mythical vehicle associated with immortality or supernatural abilities.
    • 玉门关 - The *Yumen Pass*, a historical pass in the western part of China.

    Records of Rare Things, Volume One
    　　Imperially Approved Complete Library of Four Treasuries
    　　Records of Rare Things, Volume Two
    　　Compiled by Zhang Hua of the Jin Dynasty
    　　Foreign Lands
    　　In the northwest of the Western Sea, there is the Xuanyuan (Yellow Emperor) country, situated at the edge of the remote mountains. Those who do not live long live for eight hundred years. In the fertile fields, the luan birds dance on their own. The people eat phoenix eggs and drink sweet dew.
    　　In the White People's country, there is the chenghuang (mythical creature) that looks like a fox with a horn on its back. Riding it grants a lifespan of three thousand years. The people of the Junzi (gentleman) country wear hats, caps, and swords, and use two tigers. They wear wild silk and practice yielding and respecting others, without contention. The land spans a thousand li and is rich in fragrant and beautiful grasses. The people suffer from strong winds, so they are not numerous. They value yielding, which is why it is called the Junzi country.
    　　The Sanmiao (Three Miao) country. In ancient times, Emperor Yao offered the throne to Shun, but the people of Sanmiao opposed him. The emperor then campaigned against them, and the people of Sanmiao rebelled and sailed into the South China Sea, forming the Sanmiao country.
    　　The Huan Dou country. Its people resemble immortals. They are descendants of Huan Dou, the imperial tutor of Emperor Yao. They often capture people from the islands, who have human faces and bird mouths, and are located 16,000 li south. They all resemble immortals.
    　　The Daren (Great Man) country. Its people gestate for thirty-six years before giving birth to white-haired children. These children grow up and can ride the clouds but cannot walk. They are like dragons, located 46,000 li from Shaoxing.
    　　The Yanguang country. Its people have light emanating from their mouths, and their shapes resemble black apes.
    　　The Jietong country has the Miefang bird and the Qigong people, who are skilled at making poles to kill a hundred birds. They can build flying chariots that travel with the wind. During the reign of King Tang, when the west wind blew, it carried their chariot to Yuzhou. King Tang destroyed the chariot and did not show it to the people. Ten years later, when the east wind blew, they rebuilt the chariot and returned it, and the country is 40,000 li from the Yumen Pass. [11]

    羽民国民有翼飞不逺多鸾鸟民食其卵去九嶷四万三千里
    　　穿胸国昔禹平天下防诸侯防稽之埜防风氏后到杀之夏德之盛二龙降庭禹使范成光御之行域外既周而还至南海经防风防风之神二臣以涂山之戮见禹便怒而射之迅风雷雨二龙升去二臣恐以刃自贯其心而死禹哀之乃拔其刃疗以不死之草是为穿胸民
    　　交趾民在穿胸东
    　　孟舒国民人首鸟身其先主为霅氏驯百禽夏后之世始食卵孟舒去之鳯凰随焉
    　　异人
    　　河图玉板云龙伯国人长三十丈生万八千嵗而死大秦国人长十丈中秦国人长一丈临洮人长二丈五尺
    　　禹致羣臣于防稽防风氏后至戮而杀之其骨专车长狄侨如身横九亩长五丈四尺或长十丈
    　　秦始皇二十六年有大人十二见于临洮长五丈足迹六尺东海之外大荒之中有大人国僬侥氏长三丈时含神雾
    　　日东北极人长九丈 [12]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [12]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 羽民国民有翼飞不远多鸾鸟民食其卵去九嶷四万三千里 - Describes a people with wings who cannot fly far and a people who eat the eggs of the phoenix-like bird, located 43,000 li from the Nine疑 Mountains.
    • 穿胸国昔禹平天下防诸侯防稽之埜防风氏后到杀之夏德之盛二龙降庭禹使范成光御之行域外既周而还至南海经防风防风之神二臣以涂山之戮见禹便怒而射之迅风雷雨二龙升去二臣恐以刃自贯其心而死禹哀之乃拔其刃疗以不死之草是为穿胸民 - Describes a story of Emperor Yu pacifying the world and punishing the late-arriving Fangfeng, leading to the creation of the穿胸 people.
    • 交趾民在穿胸东 - Refers to the people of Jiaozhi, a region in southern China, located east of穿胸国.
    • 孟舒国民人首鸟身其先主为霅氏驯百禽夏后之世始食卵孟舒去之鳯凰随焉 - Describes a people with bird-like bodies and human heads, whose ancestors were the Zhe clan, who tamed many birds, and who began eating eggs during the Xia dynasty.
    • 异人 - Refers to unusual or extraordinary people.
    • 河图玉板云龙伯国人长三十丈生万八千嵗而死大秦国人长十丈中秦国人长一丈临洮人长二丈五尺 - Describes the people of Longbo, who are thirty zhang tall and live for 18,000 years, and the heights of people from different regions.
    • 禹致羣臣于防稽防风氏后至戮而杀之其骨专车长狄侨如身横九亩长五丈四尺或长十丈 - Describes Emperor Yu summoning his ministers to Fangji and punishing the late-arriving Fangfeng, leading to the creation of the穿胸 people.
    • 秦始皇二十六年有大人十二见于临洮长五丈足迹六尺东海之外大荒之中有大人国僬侥氏长三丈时含神雾 - Describes twelve giants seen in Lintao during the 26th year of Qin Shi Huang’s reign and the land of giants and the Jiao people.
    • 日东北极人长九丈 - Describes people in the far northeast who are nine zhang tall.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 羽民 - A mythical people with wings, often associated with the legend of the phoenix.
    • 九嶷 - The Nine疑 Mountains, a sacred place in Chinese mythology.
    • 穿胸国 - A mythical country whose people have their chests pierced, originating from a story involving Emperor Yu.
    • 防风氏 - A mythical figure who was punished by Emperor Yu for arriving late to a meeting.
    • 夏德之盛 - Refers to the prosperity of the Xia dynasty, one of the earliest Chinese dynasties.
    • 二龙降庭 - A mythical event where two dragons appeared in Emperor Yu’s court.
    • 涂山之戮 - Refers to the punishment of Fangfeng at Mount Tushan.
    • 不死之草 - A mythical herb that grants immortality.
    • 交趾 - A historical region in southern China, now part of Vietnam.
    • 孟舒国 - A mythical country whose people have bird-like bodies and human heads.
    • 异人 - Refers to unusual or extraordinary people, often associated with mythical or legendary figures.
    • 大秦 - Refers to the Roman Empire, often mentioned in Chinese historical texts.
    • 临洮 - A historical city in Gansu Province, China.
    • 大人国 - A mythical land of giants.
    • 僬侥氏 - A mythical people who are short in stature, often associated with the land of dwarfs.

    Proper Nouns

    • 羽民 - A mythical people with wings.
    • 九嶷 - The Nine疑 Mountains.
    • 穿胸国 - A mythical country whose people have their chests pierced.
    • 防风氏 - A mythical figure.
    • 夏 - The Xia dynasty.

    Winged People and Their Myths

    The people of Yu Min have wings but cannot fly far. There are many people who eat the eggs of the phoenix-like bird, and they are located 43,000 li away from the Nine疑 Mountains.

    The people of Chuan Xiong Country once had Emperor Yu pacify the world and punish the late-arriving Fangfeng. During the flourishing times of the Xia dynasty, two dragons descended to Emperor Yu’s court. Emperor Yu sent Fan Chengguang to ride them and travel beyond the borders. After completing his journey, he returned to the South Sea, passing through Fangfeng. The two officials of Fangfeng, having witnessed the punishment of Fangfeng at Mount Tushan, became enraged and shot at Emperor Yu, causing a sudden wind and rainstorm. The two dragons ascended into the sky, and the two officials, fearing for their lives, stabbed themselves with their swords and died. Emperor Yu, moved by their plight, pulled out their swords and healed them with immortal grass. Thus, the people of Chuan Xiong were born.
