    The people of Jiaozhi are located to the east of Chuan Xiong Country.

    The people of Mengshu Country have bird-like bodies and human heads. Their ancestors were the Zhe clan, who tamed many birds. During the time of the Xia dynasty, they began eating eggs. When Mengshu left, the phoenix followed him.

    Unusual People

    According to the He Tu (River Map) and Yu Ban (Jade Tablet), the people of Longbo are thirty zhang tall and live for 18,000 years. The people of Da Qin are ten zhang tall, while the people of Zhong Qin are one zhang tall. The people of Lintao are two zhang and five chi tall.

    Emperor Yu summoned his ministers to Fangji and punished the late-arriving Fangfeng. The bones of Fangfeng were so large that they required a cart to transport them. The Qiao people are long-bodied, with bodies spanning nine mu and heights of five zhang and four chi, or ten zhang.

    In the 26th year of Qin Shi Huang’s reign, twelve giants were seen in Lintao, each five zhang tall with footprints six chi long. Beyond the East Sea, in the vast wilderness, there is a land of giants. The Jiao people are three zhang tall and live in a misty region.

    The people in the far northeast are nine zhang tall. [12]

    东方有螗螂沃焦防风氏长三丈短人身九寸逺夷之民雕题黒齿穿胸儋耳大足岐首
    　　子利国人一手二足拳反曲
    　　无启民居穴食土无男女死埋之其心不朽百年还化为人细民国其肝不朽百年而化为人皆穴居处二国同也
    　　防双民昔高阳氏有同产而为夫妇帝放之北埜相抱而死神鸟以不死草覆之七年男女皆活同颈二头四手是为防双民
    　　有一国亦在海中纯女无男又説得一布衣从海浮出其身如中国人衣两袖长二丈又得一破船随波出在海岸边有一人项中复有面生得与语不相通不食而死其地皆在沃沮东大海中
    　　南海外有鲛人水居如鱼不废织绩其眼能泣珠呕丝之埜其女子端跪据树而呕丝北海外也
    　　江陵有猛人能化为虎俗又云虎化为人好着紫葛衣足无踵
    　　日南有埜女羣行若丈夫状皛目裸袒无衣防
    　　异俗
    　　越之东有骇沐之国其长子生则解而食之谓之宜弟父死则其母而弃之言妻不可与同居【周日用曰既其母为妻则其为子亦合弃之矣】 [13]

    Here is the final translation of the source text:

    In the East, there are creatures called Tángláng Wòjiāo and Fángfēng, who are three zhang tall but have short human bodies measuring nine cun. The distant barbarians are tattooed on their foreheads, have black teeth, pierced chests, and large feet with split heads.

    The people of Zǐlì have one hand and two feet that are curved. The Wúqǐ people live in caves, eat earth, and have no distinction between men and women. When they die, they are buried, and their hearts remain uncorrupted for a hundred years before they transform into humans. The fine people of the state of Xì have livers that remain uncorrupted for a hundred years before they transform into humans. Both states live in caves and share similar customs.

    The Fángshuāng people are descendants of Gāoyáng, who were born as a couple and were exiled to the northern wilderness by the emperor. They embraced each other and died, and a divine bird covered them with immortal grass. After seven years, both men and women came back to life, with two heads and four hands, and are known as the Fángshuāng people.

    There is a country in the sea that consists entirely of women and no men. Another story tells of a clothed man who floated out of the sea, resembling a Chinese person with sleeves two zhang long. He also found a broken boat that drifted ashore. There was a man with another face on his neck who could not communicate and starved to death. These places are all located east of Wòjù in the great sea.

    In the South China Sea, there are mermaids who live in the water like fish and do not abandon weaving. Their eyes can weep pearls, and they spin silk. The mermaid girls sit upright, leaning against trees to spin silk, and are found in the North China Sea.

    In Jingzhou, there is a fierce person who can transform into a tiger. It is also said that tigers can transform into humans and wear purple bamboo clothing, with no heels on their feet.

    In the South, there are wild women who walk in groups, resembling men in appearance, with white eyes and naked bodies, without clothing.

    There are unusual customs in Yue's eastern region, where the country of Hài Mù has a practice of eating the eldest son upon birth, calling it "suitable for the younger brother." When the father dies, the mother is sacrificed and abandoned, as it is believed that a ghost wife cannot cohabit with a living person. [Zhou Rìyòng said: "If the mother is a ghost wife, then the ghost child should also be abandoned."]

    [13]

    楚之南有啖人之国其亲戚死朽其肉而弃之然后埋其骨乃为孝也
    　　秦之西有义渠国其亲戚死聚柴积而焚之熏之即烟上谓之登遐然后为孝此上以为政下以为俗中国未足为非也此事见墨子【周日用曰此事庶几佛国之法宜如是乎中国之徒不如是也】
    　　荆州极西南界至蜀诸民曰獠子媍人姙娠七月而产临水生儿便置水中浮则取养之沈便弃之然千百多浮既长皆拔去上齿牙各一以为身饰
    　　母丘俭遣王领追高句丽王宫尽沃沮东界问其耆老言国人常乘船捕鱼遭风吹数十日东得一岛上有人言语不相晓其俗常以七夕取童女沈海
    　　交州夷名曰俚子俚子弓长数尺箭长尺余以燋铜为镝涂毒药于镝中人即死不时敛藏即腹胀沸烂须臾肌肉都尽唯骨耳其俗誓不以此药法语人治之饮妇人月水及粪汁时有差者唯射猪犬则无恙以其食粪故也燋铜者故烧器其长老唯别燋铜声以物杵之徐听其声得燋毒者便凿取以为箭镝 [14]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [14]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 楚之南有啖人之国 - Literal phrase meaning 'to the south of Chu there is a country that eats people.'
    • 其亲戚死朽其肉而弃之然后埋其骨乃为孝也 - Describes a custom where the flesh of deceased relatives is left to rot before burial, and then burying their bones is considered filial piety.
    • 秦之西有义渠国 - Literal phrase meaning 'to the west of Qin there is the country of Yichu.'
    • 其亲戚死聚柴积而焚之熏之即烟上谓之登遐 - Describes a custom where the bodies of deceased relatives are burned after being piled on a heap of wood, and when the smoke rises, it is called 'ascending to the distant realms.'
    • 荆州极西南界至蜀诸民曰獠子媍人 - Literal phrase meaning 'to the extreme southwest of Jingzhou, among the people of Shu, there are those called Liaozi and Aomen.'
    • 姙娠七月而产 - Describes a custom where women give birth at seven months of pregnancy.
    • 临水生儿便置水中 - Means 'when a child is born, they are immediately placed in the water.'
    • 浮则取养之 - Means 'if they float, they are taken and raised.'
    • 沈便弃之 - Means 'if they sink, they are abandoned.'
    • 然千百多浮 - Means 'yet out of a thousand, most float.'
    • 既长皆拔去上齿牙各一 - Describes a custom where, as they grow older, they remove one upper tooth each.
    • 以为身饰 - Means 'as a decoration for their bodies.'
    • 母丘俭遣王领追高句丽王宫尽沃沮东界 - Means 'Mu Qiu Jian sent Wang Ling to pursue Goguryeo King Gungye to the eastern boundary of Wujue.'
    • 问其耆老言国人常乘船捕鱼 - Means 'he asked the elders about the local people who often sail to catch fish.'
    • 遭风吹数十日东得一岛上有人言语不相晓 - Means 'they were blown by the wind for several days and landed on an island where the people spoke a language they did not understand.'
    • 其俗常以七夕取童女沈海 - Means 'their custom is to capture young girls on the seventh day of the seventh month and throw them into the sea.'
    • 交州夷名曰俚子 - Literal phrase meaning 'the people of Jiaozhou are called Li Zi.'
    • 俚子弓长数尺箭长尺余 - Describes the long bows and arrows used by the Li Zi people.
    • 以燋铜为镝 - Means 'they use scorched copper as arrowheads.'
    • 涂毒药于镝中人即死 - Means 'they coat the arrowheads with poison, and anyone hit by them dies immediately.'
    • 不时敛藏即腹胀沸烂须臾肌肉都尽唯骨耳 - Means 'if not buried immediately, the body swells and boils, and within moments, the flesh is completely gone, leaving only the bones.'
    • 其俗誓不以此药法语人治之 - Means 'their custom is to swear not to reveal the method of making this poison to others.'
    • 饮妇人月水及粪汁时有差者 - Means 'drinking menstrual blood and feces sometimes cures them.'
    • 唯射猪犬则无恙 - Means 'only shooting pigs and dogs is harmless.'
    • 以其食粪故也 - Means 'because they eat feces.'
    • 燋铜者故烧器 - Means 'scorched copper is an old cooking vessel.'
    • 其长老唯别燋铜声以物杵之徐听其声 - Means 'the elders can distinguish the sound of scorched copper by striking it with something and listening carefully.'
    • 得燋毒者便凿取以为箭镝 - Means 'if they hear the sound of scorched copper, they take it and use it as arrowheads.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 楚 - Refers to the ancient state of Chu.
    • 啖人之国 - Refers to a cannibalistic country.
    • 秦 - Refers to the ancient state of Qin.
    • 义渠国 - Refers to the ancient state of Yichu.
    • 荆州 - Refers to the ancient state of Jingzhou.
    • 蜀 - Refers to the ancient state of Shu.
    • 獠子媍人 - Refers to the Liaozi and Aomen people, who were likely ethnic groups in the region.
    • 高句丽 - Refers to the ancient kingdom of Goguryeo.
    • 沃沮 - Refers to the ancient state of Wujue.
    • 七夕 - Refers to the seventh day of the seventh month in the lunar calendar.
    • 交州 - Refers to the ancient state of Jiaozhou.
    • 俚子 - Refers to the Li Zi people, who were likely an ethnic group in the region.

    Proper Nouns

    • 楚 - The ancient state of Chu.
    • 秦 - The ancient state of Qin.
    • 义渠国 - The ancient state of Yichu.
    • 荆州 - The ancient state of Jingzhou.
    • 蜀 - The ancient state of Shu.
    • 獠子媍人 - The Liaozi and Aomen people.
    • 高句丽 - The ancient kingdom of Goguryeo.
    • 沃沮 - The ancient state of Wujue.
    • 交州 - The ancient state of Jiaozhou.
    • 俚子 - The Li Zi people.
    • 母丘俭 - Mu Qiu Jian.

    To the south of Chu there is a country that eats people. When their relatives die, their flesh is left to rot before burial, and then burying their bones is considered filial piety.

    To the west of Qin there is the country of Yichu. When their relatives die, their bodies are burned after being piled on a heap of wood. When the smoke rises, it is called "ascending to the distant realms," and this is considered filial piety. This custom is practiced by the upper classes as a political statement and by the lower classes as a cultural tradition.

    To the extreme southwest of Jingzhou, among the people of Shu, there are those called Liaozi and Aomen. Women give birth at seven months of pregnancy and place their newborns in the water. If they float, they are taken and raised; if they sink, they are abandoned. Yet out of a thousand, most float. As they grow older, they remove one upper tooth each as a decoration for their bodies.

    Mu Qiu Jian sent Wang Ling to pursue Goguryeo King Gungye to the eastern boundary of Wujue. He asked the elders about the local people who often sail to catch fish. They were blown by the wind for several days and landed on an island where the people spoke a language they did not understand. Their custom is to capture young girls on the seventh day of the seventh month and throw them into the sea.

    The people of Jiaozhou are called Li Zi. The Li Zi people use long bows and arrows with scorched copper arrowheads coated with poison. Anyone hit by these arrows dies immediately. If not buried immediately, the body swells and boils, and within moments, the flesh is completely gone, leaving only the bones. Their custom is to swear not to reveal the method of making this poison to others. Drinking menstrual blood and feces sometimes cures them, and shooting pigs and dogs is harmless because they eat feces. Scorched copper is an old cooking vessel. The elders can distinguish the sound of scorched copper by striking it with something and listening carefully. If they hear the sound of scorched copper, they take it and use it as arrowheads. [14]

    景初中苍梧吏到京云广州西南接交州数郡桂林晋兴宁浦间人有病将死便有飞虫大如小麦或云有甲在舍上人气絶来食亡者虽复扑杀有斗斛而来者如风雨前后相寻续不可断截肌肉都尽唯余骨在便去尽贫家无相纒者或殡殓不时皆受此弊有物力者则以衣服布帛五六重裹亡者此虫恶梓木气即以板鄣防左右并以作器此虫便不敢近也入交界便无邻近郡亦有但微少耳
    　　异产
    　　汉武帝时弱水西国有人乘毛车以渡弱水来献香者帝谓是常香非中国之所乏不礼其使留久之帝幸上林苑西使至乘舆间并奏其香帝取之看大如鸾卵三枚与枣相似帝不悦以付外库后长安中大疫宫中皆疫病帝不举乐西使乞见请烧所贡香一枚以辟疫气帝不得已听之宫中病者即日并差长安中百里咸闻香气芳积九十余日香犹不歇帝乃厚礼发遣饯送
    　　一説汉制献香不满斤西使临去乃发香物如大豆者拭着宫门香气闻长安数十里经数月乃歇 [15]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [15]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 景初中 - The second year of the Jingchu era, a period in the Three Kingdoms era.
    • 蒼梧吏到京雲 - A phrase indicating a report from officials of Cangwu (a region in present-day Guangxi) to the capital.
    • 數郡 - Refers to multiple counties or regions.
    • 桂林、晉興、寧浦間 - Names of specific regions or counties in the area.
    • 有病將死 - Describes someone who is on the verge of death due to illness.
    • 飛蟲大如小麦 - Describes a flying insect as large as wheat grains.
    • 有甲在舍上 - Indicates the presence of armor-like objects on the roof.
    • 人氣絶來食亡者 - Describes the insects coming to eat the deceased after their breath has stopped.
    • 雖復扑殺 - Indicates repeated attempts to kill the insects.
    • 斗斛而来者如风雨 - Describes the insects coming in such large numbers as if in a storm.
    • 前后相寻续不可断截 - Indicates the continuous and unbroken nature of the insects' arrival.
    • 肌肉都尽唯余骨在 - Describes the complete consumption of flesh, leaving only bones.
    • 便去尽贫家无相纒者 - Indicates that the insects leave after consuming the flesh, but poor families have no means to protect themselves.
    • 殡殓不时皆受此弊 - Describes the disadvantage of not being able to properly bury the dead in a timely manner.
    • 物力者则以衣服布帛五六重裹亡者 - Describes the practice of wrapping the deceased in several layers of clothing and cloth to protect them from the insects.
    • 此虫恶梓木气即以板鄣防左右并以作器 - Indicates that the insects dislike the smell of paulownia wood and that boards made from it can be used to protect the deceased.
    • 入交界便无邻近郡亦有但微少耳 - Describes the situation in the region of Jiao, where the problem is less severe.
    • 异产 - Refers to unusual or exotic products.
    • 汉武帝时弱水西国有人乘毛车以渡弱水来献香者 - Describes a person from a country west of the weak water (a mythical river) bringing fragrant incense to the Han Emperor Wu.
    • 帝谓是常香非中国之所乏不礼其使留久之 - Indicates that the emperor initially dismissed the incense as common and did not treat the envoy respectfully, keeping him for a long time.
    • 帝幸上林苑西使至乘舆间并奏其香 - Describes the emperor visiting the Shanglin Park and the envoy presenting the incense.
    • 帝取之看大如鸾卵三枚与枣相似 - Describes the incense as large as the eggs of a phoenix and similar in size to dates.
    • 帝不悦以付外库 - Indicates that the emperor was displeased and had the incense stored in the treasury.
    • 后长安中大疫宫中皆疫病帝不举乐 - Describes a major epidemic in Chang'an, affecting the imperial palace, and the emperor not holding music performances.
    • 西使乞见请烧所贡香一枚以辟疫气 - Describes the envoy requesting to burn the incense to ward off the epidemic.
    • 帝不得已听之宫中病者即日并差长安中百里咸闻香气芳积九十余日香犹不歇 - Describes the emperor reluctantly agreeing, and the incense curing the sick in the palace and spreading a fragrance for over ninety days.
    • 帝乃厚礼发遣饯送 - Describes the emperor sending the envoy away with great respect and a farewell banquet.
    • 一説汉制献香不满斤西使临去乃发香物如大豆者拭着宫门香气闻长安数十里经数月乃歇 - Describes another account where the incense was not heavy enough and was placed at the palace gate, spreading fragrance for dozens of miles and lasting for several months.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 景初中 - Refers to the second year of the Jingchu era, a period in the Three Kingdoms era.
    • 蒼梧吏 - Refers to officials from Cangwu, a region in present-day Guangxi.
    • 桂林、晉興、寧浦 - Names of specific regions or counties in the area.
    • 弱水西国 - Refers to a mythical country west of the weak water (a mythical river).
    • 鸾卵 - Refers to the eggs of a phoenix, a mythical bird.
    • 上林苑 - Refers to the Shanglin Park, a royal garden in Chang'an.
    • 汉武帝 - Refers to Emperor Wu of Han, a prominent ruler of the Han dynasty.
    • 长安 - Refers to Chang'an, the capital of the Han dynasty.
    • 汉制 - Refers to the regulations of the Han dynasty.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The long sentence describes the phenomenon of insects attacking the dead in specific regions and the methods used to protect the deceased.
    • The long sentence describes the incident of the envoy bringing incense to the Han Emperor Wu and the subsequent events.
    • This sentence describes another account of the incense being placed at the palace gate and its effects.

    Proper Nouns

    • 景初中 - The second year of the Jingchu era.
    • 蒼梧 - A region in present-day Guangxi.
    • 桂林、晉興、寧浦 - Names of specific regions or counties.
    • 弱水西国 - A mythical country west of the weak water.
    • 鸾卵 - The eggs of a phoenix.
    • 上林苑 - The Shanglin Park.
    • 汉武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 长安 - Chang'an, the capital of the Han dynasty.
    • 汉制 - The regulations of the Han dynasty.

    During the second year of the Jingchu era, officials from Cangwu reported to the capital that in the regions of Guangzhou, southwest of the Jiaozhou, including Guilin, Jinxing, and Ningpu, when a person was on the verge of death due to illness, there would be flying insects as large as wheat grains, or even with armor-like objects on the roof. When the person's breath ceased, the insects would come to eat the deceased. Despite repeated attempts to kill them, the insects came in such large numbers as if in a storm, arriving continuously without interruption. They would consume all the flesh, leaving only bones behind, and then depart. Poor families, lacking means to protect themselves, were particularly affected, and those who could not bury the dead in a timely manner suffered from this problem. Those with resources would wrap the deceased in several layers of clothing and cloth to protect them. The insects disliked the smell of paulownia wood, so boards made from it could be used to shield the deceased, and items made from it could also be used to repel the insects. In the region of Jiao, the problem was less severe, but nearby counties still experienced it, albeit in smaller numbers.

    异产

    During the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, a person from a country west of the weak water (a mythical river) brought fragrant incense to the emperor, traveling by a vehicle covered in animal fur. The emperor initially dismissed the incense as common and not lacking in China, and did not treat the envoy respectfully, keeping him for a long time. Later, when the emperor visited the Shanglin Park, the envoy presented the incense. The emperor took it and found it to be as large as the eggs of a phoenix and similar in size to dates. Displeased, he had the incense stored in the treasury. Soon after, a major epidemic broke out in Chang'an, affecting the imperial palace, and the emperor ceased holding music performances. The envoy then requested to burn the incense to ward off the epidemic. Reluctantly, the emperor agreed, and the incense cured the sick in the palace. The fragrance spread for dozens of miles and lasted for over ninety days. The emperor then sent the envoy away with great respect and a farewell banquet.

    一説汉制献香不满斤西使临去乃发香物如大豆者拭着宫门香气闻长安数十里经数月乃歇

    Another account states that the incense was not heavy enough, and when the envoy was about to leave, he placed incense as small as soybeans at the palace gate. The fragrance spread for dozens of miles and lasted for several months before finally dissipating. [15]

    汉武帝时西海国有献胶五两者帝以付外库余胶半两西使佩以自随后从武帝射于甘泉宫帝弓断从者欲更张西使乃进乞以所送余胶续之上左右莫不怪西使乃以口濡胶为水注断两头相连注遂相着帝乃使力士各引其一头终不相离西使曰可以射终日不断帝大怪左右称竒因名曰续胶
    　　周书曰西域献火浣布昆吾氏献切玉刀火浣布污则烧之即洁刀切玉如脂布汉世有献者刀则未闻
    　　魏文帝黄初三年武都西都尉王褒献石胆二十斤四年献三斤
    　　临卭火井一所从广五尺深二三丈井在县南百里昔时人以竹木投以取火诸葛丞相徃视之后火转盛盆盖井上煮盐得盐入于家火即灭讫今不复燃也酒泉延寿县南山名火泉火出如炬
    　　徐公曰西域使王畅説石流黄出足弥山去高昌八百里有石流黄数十丈从广五六十亩有取流黄昼视孔中上状如烟而高数尺夜视皆如灯光明高尺余畅所亲见之也言时气不和皆徃保此山 [16]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [16]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為之一空 - Set phrase meaning completely emptied; fields were left barren.

    Proper Nouns

    • 紹興 - Era name of the Southern Song; rendered as Shaoxing.

    During the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, a state in the Western Sea region presented five liang of glue. The emperor stored it in the imperial treasury, and half a liang of leftover glue was kept by the Western envoy to carry with him. The envoy followed the emperor in shooting at the Ganquan Palace, and the emperor's bowstring broke. The attendants wanted to replace the bowstring, but the Western envoy offered to use the leftover glue to mend it. The emperor's attendants were all surprised, and the Western envoy then moistened the glue with his mouth, poured it over the broken ends of the string, and they stuck together. The emperor then had strong men pull each end, but they could not be separated. The Western envoy said it could withstand shooting for an entire day without breaking. The emperor was greatly surprised, and his attendants praised it, so it was named "Continuing Bowstring Glue."

    The Book of Zhou states that the Western region presented fire-washed cloth, and the Kunwu clan presented a jade-cutting knife. The fire-washed cloth, when soiled, can be cleaned by burning it, and the knife can cut jade as smoothly as butter. In the Han dynasty, there were those who presented fire-washed cloth, but no one presented the jade-cutting knife.

    In the third year of Huangchu, during the reign of Emperor Wen of Wei, Wang Bao, the Western Commandant of Wudu, presented twenty jin of stone胆 (likely a mineral) to the emperor. In the fourth year, he presented three jin.

    There is a well in Linqiong that is five chi wide and two or three zhang deep. It is located one hundred li south of the county. In the past, people used bamboo and wood to extract fire from it. Zhuge Liang, the Chancellor, visited the well, and after his visit, the fire became more intense. A basin was placed over the well to cook salt, and when the salt was taken home, the fire went out. The well no longer burns now. In Yan寿 County, Jiuquan, there is a fire spring in the southern mountain, where fire emerges like a torch.

    Xu Gong said that the Western envoy Wang Chang reported the presence of stone流黄 (likely sulfur) in a mountain near Gaochang. The mountain is described as having a large amount of sulfur, and during the day, it appears as smoke rising several feet, and at night, it looks like lights shining a few feet high. Xu Gong claims to have seen this personally and notes that when the weather is unfavorable, people seek refuge on this mountain. [16]

    博物志卷二
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　博物志卷三
    　　晋　张华　撰
    　　异兽
    　　汉武帝时大宛之北胡人有献一物大如狗然声能惊人鸡犬闻之皆走名曰猛兽帝见之怪其细小及出苑中欲使虎狼食之虎见此兽即低头着地帝为反观见虎如此谓欲下头作势起搏杀之而此兽见虎甚喜防唇摇尾径徃虎头上立因搦虎面虎乃闭目低头匍匐不敢动搦鼻下去下去之后虎尾下头起此兽顾之虎辄闭目
    　　后魏武帝伐冒顿经白狼山逢师子使人格之杀伤甚众王乃自率尝从军数百击之师子哮吼奋起左右咸惊王忽见一物从林中出如狸起上帝车轭师子将至此兽便跳起上师子头上师子即伏不敢起于是遂杀之得师子一还来至洛阳三千里鸡犬皆无鸣吠
    　　九真有神牛乃生谿上黒山时共鬭即海沸黄或出鬭岸上家牛皆怖人或遮则霹号曰神牛 [17]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [17]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 博物志 - Title of the text, part of the *Miscellaneous Records* (博物志), a classical Chinese text on various subjects.
    • 欽定四庫全書 - Refers to the *Four Treasuries* (四庫全書), a massive compilation of Chinese literature commissioned by the Qing dynasty.
    • 大宛之北胡人 - People from the northern regions of Dayuan (大宛), an ancient kingdom in Central Asia.
    • 猛獸 - A creature described as large as a dog but with a terrifying sound that causes other animals to flee.
    • 反觀 - Means 'to look back' or 'to reflect,' used here in the context of the emperor's reaction.
    • 搦虎面 - Means 'to grasp the tiger's face,' describing the action of the creature.
    • 霹 - Refers to thunder, used metaphorically to describe a loud noise.
    • 神牛 - A divine or supernatural bull.
    • 海沸 - Means 'the sea boiling,' used metaphorically to describe a violent struggle.
    • 飯牛牧羊 - Refers to feeding cattle and herding sheep, indicating ordinary labor.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 漢武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han, a prominent ruler of the Han dynasty known for his military campaigns and cultural achievements.
    • 大宛 - An ancient kingdom in Central Asia, known for its horses and trade routes.
    • 九真 - A historical region in present-day northern Vietnam, known for its unique cultural and natural phenomena.
    • 冒頓 - Modu Chanyu, a ruler of the Xiongnu, a nomadic confederation that posed a significant threat to the Han dynasty.
    • 白狼山 - A mountain in China, historically significant for its association with imperial expeditions.
    • 師子 - Refers to a lion, which was not native to China but was known through trade and cultural exchange.
    • 神牛 - A supernatural bull, often associated with divine or mythological contexts.

    Proper Nouns

    • 博物志 - Title of the text, indicating it is part of the *Miscellaneous Records*.
    • 欽定四庫全書 - The *Four Treasuries*, a massive compilation of Chinese literature.
    • 漢武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 大宛 - An ancient kingdom in Central Asia.
    • 後魏武帝 - Emperor Wu of the Northern Wei dynasty.
    • 冒頓 - Modu Chanyu, a ruler of the Xiongnu.
    • 白狼山 - A mountain in China.
    • 九真 - A historical region in present-day northern Vietnam.
    • 神牛 - A divine or supernatural bull.

    Miscellaneous Records, Volume Two
    　　Imperially Approved Collection of the Four Treasuries
    Miscellaneous Records, Volume Three
    Compiled by Zhang Hua of the Jin dynasty

    Strange Beasts

    During the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, people from the northern regions of Dayuan offered a creature that was as large as a dog but had a terrifying sound that caused chickens and dogs to flee. It was called the Mengshou (Fierce Beast). The emperor found it strange that it was so small and, when it was released into the imperial park, he intended to have tigers and wolves devour it. However, when the tiger saw this creature, it immediately lowered its head to the ground. The emperor, looking back, saw the tiger in this manner and thought it was preparing to attack, ready to pounce and kill it. But the creature, upon seeing the tiger, was very pleased, licking its lips and wagging its tail, and it ran directly onto the tiger's head, standing there. It then grabbed the tiger's face, and the tiger closed its eyes, lowered its head, and lay prostrate, unable to move. After the creature released its grip on the tiger's nose, the tiger's tail lowered, and its head raised. When the creature turned to look at the tiger, the tiger would close its eyes.

    Later, Emperor Wu of the Northern Wei attacked Modu Chanyu and passed through White Wolf Mountain, where he encountered a lion. He sent soldiers to kill it, resulting in many casualties. The emperor then personally led hundreds of his elite troops to attack the lion. The lion roared and leapt up, causing great alarm among the soldiers. Suddenly, the emperor saw something emerge from the forest, resembling a weasel, and it jumped onto the emperor's carriage yoke. As the lion approached, this creature leapt onto the lion's head, and the lion immediately submitted, cowering and refusing to rise. The emperor then killed the lion and captured one. Upon returning to Luoyang, the emperor reported that over three thousand li of distance, neither chickens nor dogs made a sound.

    In the region of Jiu Zhen, there was a divine bull born on Black Mountain. When it fought with other bulls, the sea would boil, and sometimes yellow smoke would rise from the shore. The local cattle would be terrified. If someone tried to block it, there would be thunderous noise, and it was called the Shen Niu (Divine Bull). [17]

    昔日南贡四各有雌雄其一雄死于九眞及至南海百有余日其雌涂土著身不饮食莝草长史问其所以闻之辄流涕矣
    　　越巂国有牛稍割取肉牛不死经日肉生如故
    　　大宛国有汗血马天马种汉魏西域时有献者
    　　文马赤鬛身色似若黄金即古之乗黄今谓之露犬也能飞食虎豹
    　　蜀山南高山上有物如猕猴长七尺能人行健走名曰猴玃一名化或曰猳玃同行道妇女有好者辄盗之以去人不得知行者或每过其旁皆以长绳相引乃得免此得男子气自死故取女也取去为室家其年少者终身不得还十年之后形皆之意亦迷惑不复思归有子者辄俱送还其家产子皆如人有不食养者其母辄死故无敢不养也及长与人不异皆以杨为姓故今蜀中西界多谓杨率皆猳玃化之子孙大约皆有玃爪者也
    　　小山有兽其形如鼓一足如蠡泽有委蛇状如毂长如辕见之者霸
    　　猩猩若黄狗人面能言
    　　异鸟
    　　崇丘山有鸟一足一翼一目相得而飞名曰防见则吉良乘之寿千嵗
    　　比翼鸟一青一赤在参嵎山 [18]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [18]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 昔 - In the past or formerly, indicating a reference to a previous time.
    • 南贡四 - Refers to four types of exotic or rare animals from the southern regions.
    • 雌雄 - Male and female, used to describe the sexes of animals.
    • 九眞 - A historical region in southern China, possibly referring to a specific area where the male animal died.
    • 南海 - South Sea, referring to the southern seas, likely the South China Sea.
    • 涂土著身 - Coating oneself with mud, a behavior that suggests mourning or distress.
    • 不饮食莝草 - Not eating hay or grass, indicating a refusal to eat, possibly due to grief or illness.
    • 长史 - Chief secretary or high-ranking official, likely referring to a person of authority asking about the situation.
    • 流涕 - Crying or shedding tears, indicating emotional distress.
    • 越巂国 - A historical region in southwestern China, possibly referring to a specific area where the unusual cow is found.
    • 稍割取肉 - Gradually cutting off meat, describing a method of taking meat from a living animal without killing it.
    • 大宛国 - A historical region in Central Asia, known for its horses, particularly the famous 'sweat-blood' horses.
    • 汗血马 - Sweat-blood horse, a legendary breed of horse known for its endurance and strength.
    • 天马种 - Heavenly horse breed, emphasizing the divine or exceptional nature of the horse.
    • 文马 - Patterned horse, referring to a horse with distinctive markings.
    • 赤鬛 - Red mane, describing the color of the horse's mane.
    • 乗黄 - Riding yellow, possibly referring to a mythical creature or a horse with golden or yellow markings.
    • 露犬 - Dew dog, a term that might refer to a mythical or legendary creature.
    • 蜀山南高山 - High mountains in the south of Shu, referring to a specific mountainous region in southwestern China.
    • 猕猴 - Macaque, a type of monkey.
    • 健走 - Strong walking or vigorous movement, describing the monkey's ability to move quickly and powerfully.
    • 猴玃 - Monkey ape, a term that might refer to a specific type of monkey or ape.
    • 化 - Transformation or change, suggesting a supernatural or magical element.
    • 猳玃 - Male monkey ape, a term that might refer to a specific type of monkey or ape.
    • 同行道妇女 - Women walking the same path, referring to women who are traveling or living in the same area.
    • 辄盗之以去 - Stealing them away, indicating that the monkeys abduct women.
    • 形皆之意 - Their appearance becomes similar, suggesting that the abducted women gradually resemble the monkeys.
    • 子孙 - Descendants, referring to the offspring of the monkeys.
    • 玃爪 - Ape claws, indicating that the descendants of the abducted women have physical traits similar to those of the monkeys.
    • 小山 - Small hill, referring to a specific location.
    • 鼓 - Drum, describing the shape of the animal.
    • 蠡 - Snail, describing the shape of the animal's foot.
    • 泽 - Swamp or marsh, referring to a wetland area.
    • 委蛇 - Coiling snake, describing the shape of the animal.
    • 毂 - Spoke, describing the shape of the animal.
    • 辕 - Axle, describing the length of the animal.
    • 霸 - Dominance or supremacy, suggesting that seeing this animal brings about a state of dominance or control.
    • 猩猩 - Orangutan, a type of ape.
    • 黄狗 - Yellow dog, describing the color of the orangutan.
    • 崇丘山 - Tall hill, referring to a specific mountain.
    • 防 - Fang, a mythical creature with one foot, one wing, and one eye.
    • 比翼鸟 - Birds with one wing, referring to a pair of birds that fly together with one wing each.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 南贡四 - Refers to four types of exotic or rare animals from the southern regions, possibly including the ones mentioned later in the text.
    • 九眞 - A historical region in southern China, known for its unique flora and fauna.
    • 南海 - Refers to the South China Sea, an important maritime region in Chinese history.
    • 越巂国 - A historical region in southwestern China, known for its unusual natural phenomena and mythical creatures.
    • 大宛国 - A historical region in Central Asia, famous for its horses, particularly the 'sweat-blood' horses.
    • 蜀山南高山 - Refers to the high mountains in the south of Sichuan, a region known for its rich biodiversity and cultural legends.
    • 崇丘山 - A specific mountain in the region, possibly known for its unique wildlife and mythical creatures.

    Translation

    In the past, there were four types of animals from the south, each with male and female counterparts. One male died in the region of Jiu Zhen, and by the time they reached the South Sea, after more than a hundred days, the female coated herself with mud and did not eat hay or grass. The chief secretary asked about the reason, and upon hearing it, he shed tears.

    In the region of Yuezhou, there is a cow that can have its meat cut off without dying, and the meat regrows as before.

    In the region of Dayuan, there are sweat-blood horses, heavenly horse breeds, which during the Han and Wei dynasties were presented to the court.

    A patterned horse with a red mane and a body like gold is the ancient riding yellow, now called a dew dog, and can fly and eat tigers and leopards.

    In the high mountains of the south of Shu, there is a creature like a macaque, seven feet tall, that can walk like a human and run strongly. It is called a monkey ape, also known as a male monkey ape. Women walking the same path, if they are attractive, are stolen away by these creatures, and no one knows what happens to them. Travelers often pass by and tie themselves with long ropes to avoid being taken. These creatures take only women who have a strong male presence, so they take women. Those taken are made into wives, and young ones never return. After ten years, their appearance becomes similar, and they become confused and no longer think of returning home. If they have children, they are returned to their families, and the children are treated like humans. If they do not feed and care for their children, their mothers die, so no one dares not to care for them. When they grow up, they are no different from humans and are named Yang, so in the western part of Shu, many people are called Yang, all descendants of the monkey apes, and most have ape-like claws.

    On a small hill, there is an animal shaped like a drum with a single foot like a snail. In the swamp, there is a coiling snake shaped like a spoke, as long as an axle. Seeing this animal brings about dominance.

    An orangutan looks like a yellow dog with a human face and can speak.

    There are strange birds.

    On the tall hill, there is a bird with one foot, one wing, and one eye, which flies when paired. It is called Fang, and seeing it brings good fortune. Riding it grants a lifespan of a thousand years.

    A pair of birds with one wing each, one blue and one red, are found in the region of San Yu Mountain. [18]

    有鸟如乌文首白喙赤足曰精衞故精衞常取西山之木石以填东海
    　　越地深山有鸟如鸠青色名曰冶鸟穿大树作巢如升器其户口径数寸周饰以土垩赤白相次状如射侯伐木见此树即避之去或夜防人不见鸟鸟亦知人不见巳也鸣曰咄咄上去明日便宜急上树去咄咄下去明日便宜急下树去若使去但言笑而不已者可往伐也若有恶乃犯其树者则虎通夕来守人不知者即害人此鸟白日见其形鸟也夜听其鸣人也时欢乐便作人悲喜形长三尺涧中取石蠏就人火间炙之不可犯也越人谓此鸟为越祝之祖异虫
    　　南方有落头虫其头能飞其种人常有所祭祀其虫即至故因取之焉其飞因晚便去以耳为翼将晓还复着体呉时徃徃得此虫也
    　　江南山谿水中射工虫甲也长一二寸口中有弩形气射人影随所着处发疮不治则杀人今蠼螋虫溺人影亦随所着处生疮【卢氏曰以鸡防草捣涂经日即愈周日用曰万物皆有所相感愚闻以霹木击鸟影其鸟应时落地虽未尝试以是推必有之】 [19]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [19]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 有鸟如乌文首白喙赤足曰精衞 - Describes a bird like a crow with a speckled head, white beak, and red feet, called Jingwei.
    • 故精衞常取西山之木石以填东海 - Means 'Therefore, Jingwei often takes wood and stones from the Western Mountain to fill the Eastern Sea,' which is a metaphor for perseverance and determination.
    • 越地深山有鸟如鸠青色名曰冶鸟 - Describes a bird in the deep mountains of Yue that looks like a pigeon and is blue, named Yetao.
    • 穿大树作巢如升器其户口径数寸周饰以土垩赤白相次状如射侯 - Describes the bird's nest as being made in a large tree, shaped like a winnowing basket, with an entrance several inches wide, decorated with red and white earth, resembling a target.
    • 伐木见此树即避之去 - Means 'When cutting wood and seeing this tree, one should immediately avoid it.'
    • 或夜防人不见鸟鸟亦知人不见巳也鸣曰咄咄上去明日便宜急上树去咄咄下去明日便宜急下树去 - Describes the bird's behavior at night, warning people to go up or down the tree.
    • 若使去但言笑而不已者可往伐也 - Means 'If the bird continues to talk and laugh without stopping, one can go to cut the tree.'
    • 若有恶乃犯其树者则虎通夕来守人不知者即害人 - Describes the consequences if someone harms the tree, leading to a tiger guarding the area and potentially harming people.
    • 此鸟白日见其形鸟也夜听其鸣人也 - Describes the bird's behavior during the day and night, appearing as a bird during the day and sounding like a human at night.
    • 时欢乐便作人悲喜形长三尺涧中取石蠏就人火间炙之不可犯也 - Describes the bird's behavior when it is happy, making sounds that resemble human emotions, and its size and habitat.
    • 越人谓此鸟为越祝之祖异虫 - Means 'The people of Yue call this bird the ancestor of the Yue shaman and an unusual creature.'
    • 南方有落头虫其头能飞其种人常有所祭祀其虫即至故因取之焉 - Describes a creature in the south whose head can fly, and its people often sacrifice to it, causing the creature to appear.
    • 其飞因晚便去以耳为翼将晓还复着体 - Describes the creature's flight pattern, leaving at dusk and returning at dawn.
    • 吴时往往得此虫也 - Means 'During the Wu period, this creature was often found.'
    • 江南山谿水中射工虫甲也长一二寸口中有弩形气射人影随所着处发疮不治则杀人 - Describes a creature in the mountains and rivers of Jiangnan that is about one to two inches long, with a mouth shaped like a crossbow, and can shoot a deadly ray that causes sores.
    • 今蠼螋虫溺人影亦随所着处生疮 - Means 'Nowadays, the urine of the centipede-like creature can also cause sores on the shadow it touches.'
    • 卢氏曰以鸡防草捣涂经日即愈周日用曰万物皆有所相感愚闻以霹木击鸟影其鸟应时落地虽未尝试以是推必有之 - Provides additional commentary on the effectiveness of certain remedies and the principle of sympathetic magic.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 精衞 - A legendary bird from Chinese mythology, known for its perseverance in trying to fill the sea with stones and wood.
    • 越地 - Refers to the region of Yue, which is in southern China.
    • 越祝 - Refers to the shamans or priests of the Yue people.
    • 吴时 - Refers to the period of the Wu state, a historical period in ancient China.
    • 江南山谿 - Refers to the mountains and rivers in the southern part of China.
    • 射工虫 - A mythical creature from Chinese folklore, known for its deadly ray.

    Proper Nouns

    • 精衞 - The name of the legendary bird.
    • 越地 - The region of Yue.
    • 越祝 - The shamans or priests of the Yue people.
    • 吴时 - The period of the Wu state.
    • 江南山谿 - The mountains and rivers in the southern part of China.
    • 射工虫 - The name of the mythical creature.

    There is a bird like a crow with a speckled head, a white beak, and red feet, called Jingwei. Therefore, Jingwei often takes wood and stones from the Western Mountain to fill the Eastern Sea. In the deep mountains of Yue, there is a bird like a pigeon, blue in color, named Yetao. It makes its nest in a large tree, shaped like a winnowing basket, with an entrance several inches wide, decorated with red and white earth, resembling a target. When cutting wood and seeing this tree, one should immediately avoid it. Sometimes at night, when people do not see the bird, it also knows that people are not around and will make sounds, saying "Dudu up, go up the tree tomorrow, it's best to hurry." "Dudu down, go down the tree tomorrow, it's best to hurry." If the bird continues to talk and laugh without stopping, one can go to cut the tree. If someone harms the tree, a tiger will come to guard the area all night, and if people do not know, they will be harmed. This bird appears as a bird during the day and sounds like a human at night. When it is happy, it makes sounds that resemble human emotions, and its body is about three feet long. It lives in the streams and takes stone crabs, cooking them over people's fires, and should not be disturbed. The people of Yue call this bird the ancestor of the Yue shaman and an unusual creature. In the south, there is a creature whose head can fly, and its people often sacrifice to it, causing the creature to appear. It leaves at dusk and returns at dawn, using its ears as wings. During the Wu period, this creature was often found. In the mountains and rivers of Jiangnan, there is a creature about one to two inches long, with a mouth shaped like a crossbow, and can shoot a deadly ray that causes sores. Nowadays, the urine of the centipede-like creature can also cause sores on the shadow it touches. [Lu Shi said that by pounding chicken grass and applying it, the sores will heal after several days. Zhou Shi said that all things are interconnected by sympathy, and I have heard that striking a bird's shadow with a thunderwood will cause the bird to fall to the ground at the time. Although I have not tried it, based on this principle, it must be true.] [19]

    蝮蛇秋月毒盛无所蜇螫啮草木以泄其气草木即死人樵采设为草木所伤刺者亦杀人毒甚于蝮齧谓之蛇迹也
    　　华山有蛇名肥遗六足四翼见则天下大旱
    　　常山之蛇名率然有两头触其一头头至触其中则两头俱至孙武以喻善用兵者
    　　异鱼
    　　南海有鳄鱼状似鼍斩其头而干之去齿而更生如此者三乃止
    　　东海中有牛体鱼其形状如牛剥其皮悬之潮水至则毛起潮水去则毛伏
    　　东海蛟错鱼生子子惊还入母肠寻复出
    　　呉王江行食鲙有余弃于中流化为鱼今鱼中有名呉王鲙余者长数寸大者如筯犹有鲙形
    　　广陵陈登食脍作病华佗下之脍头皆成虫尾犹是脍东海有物状如凝血从广数尺方员名曰鲊鱼无头目处所内无脏众虾附之随其东西人煮食之
    　　异草木
    　　太原晋阳以北生屏风草
    　　海上有草焉名蒒其实食之如大麦七月稔俗名曰自然谷或曰禹余粮【蒒音师】 [20]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [20]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 蝮蛇秋月毒盛无所蜇螫 - Describes a venomous snake whose poison is particularly potent during autumn, and it has nowhere to sting or bite.
    • 啮草木以泄其气 - The snake bites or gnaws at grass and trees to release its poison.
    • 草木即死 - The grass and trees die immediately.
    • 人樵采设为草木所伤 - People who cut wood are injured by the grass and trees.
    • 刺者亦杀人 - Those who are pricked by the grass and trees also die.
    • 毒甚于蝮齧谓之蛇迹也 - The poison is even more potent than that of the snake's bite, and it is called 'snake trail'.
    • 华山有蛇名肥遗六足四翼见则天下大旱 - There is a snake on Mount Hua named Fei Yi with six feet and four wings. When it appears, there will be a great drought in the world.
    • 常山之蛇名率然有两头触其一头头至触其中则两头俱至孙武以喻善用兵者 - The snake on Mount Chang is called Shu Ran and has two heads. When one head is touched, both heads come together. Sun Wu used this to illustrate good military strategy.
    • 南海有鳄鱼状似鼍斩其头而干之去齿而更生如此者三乃止 - There are crocodiles in the South Sea that look like alligators. Cutting off their heads and drying them, removing their teeth, and letting them regenerate this process three times will stop them.
    • 东海中有牛体鱼其形状如牛剥其皮悬之潮水至则毛起潮水去则毛伏 - In the East Sea, there is a fish with a cow-like body. Its shape is like a cow. If you peel its skin and hang it, the hair will stand up when the tide comes and lie down when the tide goes out.
    • 东海蛟错鱼生子子惊还入母肠寻复出 - In the East Sea, there is a dragonfish that gives birth to its offspring. The offspring are startled and return to the mother's intestines, then come out again.
    • 呉王江行食鲙有余弃于中流化为鱼今鱼中有名呉王鲙余者长数寸大者如筯犹有鲙形 - King Wu was traveling on the river and had leftover fish. He threw it into the middle of the river, and it turned into a fish. Today, there is a fish called 'King Wu's Leftover Fish' that is several inches long, some as big as chopsticks, still resembling the shape of the leftover fish.
    • 广陵陈登食脍作病华佗下之脍头皆成虫尾犹是脍东海有物状如凝血从广数尺方员名曰鲊鱼无头目处所内无脏众虾附之随其东西人煮食之 - Chen Deng from Guangling got sick after eating raw fish. Hua Tuo treated him, and the raw fish turned into worms, but the tails were still fish. In the East Sea, there is something that looks like congealed blood, several feet wide and round, called 'Zha Fish'. It has no head or eyes, and inside it is empty. Many shrimp attach to it, following it east and west. People cook and eat it.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 蝮蛇 - A venomous snake found in China.
    • 华山 - One of the Five Great Mountains in China, known for its natural beauty and historical significance.
    • 常山 - Another mountain in China, often associated with strategic military positions.
    • 孙武 - Also known as Sun Tzu, a famous Chinese military strategist and author of 'The Art of War'.
    • 呉王 - King Wu, a historical figure in ancient China.
    • 广陵 - A city in present-day Jiangsu Province, known for its historical and cultural significance.
    • 华佗 - A renowned physician in ancient China, known for his surgical skills and herbal medicine.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This long sentence describes the behavior and effects of a venomous snake. It must be read as a single, continuous description.
    • This sentence introduces a mythical snake on Mount Hua. The clause structure is straightforward but requires careful translation to maintain the mythological tone.
    • This sentence describes a snake on Mount Chang and its use in military strategy. The clause structure is complex and requires careful segmentation to ensure clarity.
    • This section introduces various unusual fish, each with its own unique characteristics. Each sentence must be read as a separate description.
    • This sentence describes a crocodile in the South Sea and a method to stop it. The clause structure is complex and requires careful segmentation.
    • This sentence describes a fish in the East Sea with a cow-like body. The clause structure is complex and requires careful segmentation.
    • This sentence describes a dragonfish in the East Sea and its reproductive behavior. The clause structure is straightforward but requires careful translation to maintain the mythological tone.
    • This sentence describes a legend about King Wu and a fish. The clause structure is complex and requires careful segmentation.
    • This sentence describes a medical case involving Chen Deng and a description of a fish. The clause structure is complex and requires careful segmentation.

    Proper Nouns

    • 蝮蛇 - Venomous snake.
    • 华山 - Mount Hua.
    • 常山 - Mount Chang.
    • 孙武 - Sun Tzu.
    • 呉王 - King Wu.
    • 广陵 - Guangling.
    • 华佗 - Hua Tuo.
    • 肥遗 - Fei Yi.
    • 率然 - Shu Ran.
    • 鼍 - Alligator.
    • 牛体鱼 - Cow-like fish.
    • 蛟错鱼 - Dragonfish.
    • 呉王鲙余 - King Wu's Leftover Fish.
    • 鲊鱼 - Zha Fish.

    A Venomous Snake

    During the autumn moon, the poison of the venomous snake is particularly potent, and it has nowhere to sting or bite. It gnaws at grass and trees to release its poison, causing the grass and trees to die immediately. People who cut wood are injured by the grass and trees, and those who are pricked by the grass and trees also die. The poison is even more potent than that of the snake's bite, and it is called "snake trail."

    Mount Hua has a snake named Fei Yi, which has six feet and four wings. When it appears, there will be a great drought in the world.

    The snake on Mount Chang is called Shu Ran and has two heads. When one head is touched, both heads come together. Sun Wu used this to illustrate good military strategy.

    Unusual Fish

    In the South Sea, there are crocodiles that look like alligators. Cutting off their heads and drying them, removing their teeth, and letting them regenerate this process three times will stop them.

    In the East Sea, there is a fish with a cow-like body. Its shape is like a cow. If you peel its skin and hang it, the hair will stand up when the tide comes and lie down when the tide goes out.

    In the East Sea, there is a dragonfish that gives birth to its offspring. The offspring are startled and return to the mother's intestines, then come out again.

    King Wu was traveling on the river and had leftover fish. He threw it into the middle of the river, and it turned into a fish. Today, there is a fish called "King Wu's Leftover Fish" that is several inches long, some as big as chopsticks, still resembling the shape of the leftover fish.

    Chen Deng from Guangling got sick after eating raw fish. Hua Tuo treated him, and the raw fish turned into worms, but the tails were still fish. In the East Sea, there is something that looks like congealed blood, several feet wide and round, called "Zha Fish." It has no head or eyes, and inside it is empty. Many shrimp attach to it, following it east and west. People cook and eat it.

    Unusual Grass and Trees

    North of Jin Yang in Tai Yuan, there grows a type of grass called "screening grass."

    There is a plant in the sea called "Shi," and its fruit tastes like barley. It ripens in July and is commonly known as "natural grain" or "Yu Yu Liang." [Shi sounds like "Shi."] [20]

    尧时有屈轶草生于庭佞人入朝则屈而指之又名指佞草
    　　右詹山草帝女所化其叶郁茂其华黄实如豆服者媚于人
    　　止些山多竹长千仞鳯食其实去九疑万八千里江南诸山郡中大树断倒者经春夏生菌谓之椹食之有味而每毒杀人云此物徃徃自生毒或云蛇所着之物枫树生者啖之令人笑不得止治之饮土浆即愈

    　　博物志卷三
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　博物志卷四
    　　晋　张华　撰
    　　物性
    　　九窍者胎化八窍者卵生鼈皆此咸卵生影伏白鹢雄雌相视则孕或曰雄鸣上风则雌孕
    　　兔防毫望月而孕口中吐子旧有此説余目所未见也大腰无雄鼍也无雄与蛇通气则孕细腰无雌蜂也无雌则负别虫于空木中七日而化盖取桑即阜螽子呪而成子诗云螟蛉有子蜾蠃之是也
    　　三化先孕而后交不交者亦产子子后为皆无眉目易伤采亦薄
    　　鸟雌雄不可别翼右掩左雄左掩右雌二足而翼谓之禽四足而毛谓之兽
    　　鹊巢门户背太嵗向太乙智也 [21]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [21]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 屈轶草 - Mythical grass that bends toward evil people.
    • 指佞草 - Another name for the same grass, emphasizing its ability to point out evil people.
    • 詹山草 - Mythical grass from the mountain of Zhan, associated with a divine transformation.
    • 九窍者胎化八窍者卵生 - Biological classification where beings with nine orifices are born from eggs, and those with eight are born from eggs as well.
    • 影伏白鹢雄雌相视则孕 - Description of a white egret that conceives when its male and female counterparts look at each other.
    • 兔防毫望月而孕口中吐子 - Description of a rabbit that conceives by looking at the moon and then gives birth through its mouth.
    • 大腰无雄 - Description of a creature with a large waist but no male counterpart.
    • 龟鼍之类 - Refers to turtles and crocodiles, indicating a group of animals.
    • 细腰无雌 - Description of a creature with a small waist but no female counterpart.
    • 蜂之类 - Refers to bees, indicating a group of insects.
    • 二足而翼谓之禽四足而毛谓之兽 - Classification of animals based on the number of legs and the presence of wings or fur.
    • 鹊巢门户背太岁向太乙 - Description of a magpie's nest, which is built facing away from the direction of the 'Tai Sui' and toward the 'Tai Yi'.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 尧时 - Refers to the time of Emperor Yao, a legendary ruler in Chinese history.
    • 詹山 - A mythical mountain, possibly referring to a specific location in ancient Chinese mythology.
    • 白鹢 - A type of white egret, often associated with auspiciousness in Chinese culture.
    • 兔防毫 - A mythical rabbit, often associated with the moon in Chinese folklore.
    • 龟鼍 - Turtles and crocodiles, often associated with longevity and water in Chinese culture.
    • 细腰无雌 - A description of a creature that is often associated with bees in Chinese folklore.
    • 鹊 - Magpie.
    • 太岁 - A feng shui term referring to the annual position of Jupiter.
    • 太乙 - A feng shui term referring to the northern pole star.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The sentence describing a mythical grass that bends toward evil people ('尧时有屈轶草生于庭佞人入朝则屈而指之又名指佞草') requires careful translation to maintain the mythological context.
    • The sentence describing a mythical grass from the mountain of Zhan, which is said to have been transformed by a goddess ('右詹山草帝女所化其叶郁茂其华黄实如豆服者媚于人'), requires careful translation to maintain the mythological context.
    • The sentence describing a mountain with many tall bamboo trees, where phoenixes eat the fruit, and a fungus that grows on fallen trees, which can be toxic ('止些山多竹长千仞凤食其实去九疑万八千里江南诸山郡中大树断倒者经春夏生菌谓之椹食之有味而每毒杀人云此物往往自生毒或云蛇所着之物枫树生者啖之令人笑不得止治之饮土浆即愈'), requires careful translation to maintain the mythological and biological context.

    Proper Nouns

    • 尧 - Emperor Yao, a legendary ruler in Chinese history.
    • 詹山 - A mythical mountain.
    • 白鹢 - A type of white egret.
    • 兔防毫 - A mythical rabbit.
    • 龟鼍 - Turtles and crocodiles.
    • 细腰无雌 - A description of a creature, often associated with bees.
    • 鹊 - Magpie.
    • 太岁 - A feng shui term referring to the annual position of Jupiter.
    • 太乙 - A feng shui term referring to the northern pole star.

    During the reign of Emperor Yao, there was a mythical grass called Quyi, which grew in the court. When wicked people entered the court, the grass would bend and point towards them. It was also known as the Grass that Points to Wicked People.

    To the right of the mountain of Zhan, there was a mythical grass said to have been transformed by a goddess. Its leaves were lush and green, and its flowers were yellow, resembling beans. Those who consumed it would become charming to others.

    On this mountain, there were many tall bamboo trees, some reaching a thousand feet in height. Phoenixes fed on the fruits of these trees, and the fruits were said to be a thousand eight hundred miles away from the Nine疑 Mountains. In the southern regions, large trees that had fallen would grow mushrooms during the summer and autumn seasons. These mushrooms were called "Shen," and while they were delicious, they were also highly toxic and often killed those who ate them. It was said that these mushrooms often grew poisonous on their own, or perhaps they were the result of snakes' influence. Those that grew on maple trees would make people laugh uncontrollably, and the only way to stop this was to drink water from the ground.

    In terms of biological classification, beings with nine orifices are born from eggs, and those with eight are also born from eggs. Turtles and crocodiles are examples of such beings, all born from eggs. A white egret conceives when its male and female counterparts look at each other, or some say when the male calls out in the wind, the female becomes pregnant.

    A rabbit that looks at the moon becomes pregnant and gives birth through its mouth. This is an old saying, but I have not seen it myself. Large-bodied creatures without males, like turtles and crocodiles, can conceive by exchanging breath with snakes. Small-bodied creatures without females, like bees, can conceive by carrying other insects in hollow trees for seven days, after which they transform into their own kind. This is similar to the verse in the Book of Songs: "The mantis has a child, the wasp adopts it."

    Insects undergo three transformations: they conceive first and then mate. Even if they do not mate, they can still produce offspring, which later become insects. These insects have no eyebrows or eyes and are easily injured. Their appearance is also quite unremarkable.

    Birds cannot be distinguished by gender based on their wings alone. If the right wing covers the left, it is a male; if the left wing covers the right, it is a female. Creatures with two feet and wings are called birds, while those with four feet and fur are called beasts.

    The nests of magpies are built facing away from the direction of the "Tai Sui" (a feng shui term) and toward the "Tai Yi" (another feng shui term). This is a sign of wisdom. [21]

    鸐雉长毛雨雪惜其尾栖高树杪不敢下食徃徃饿死闻魏景初中时人所説
    　　鹳水鸟也伏卵时则不鸣卵冷取礜石用绕卵以时助燥气
    　　山鸡有美毛自爱其色终日映水目则溺死
    　　三千嵗游于莲叶巢于卷耳之上
    　　屠解其肌肉唯肠连其头而经日不死犹能齧物鸟徃食之则为所得渔者或以取鸟遇神虵复续
    　　蛴螬以背行快于用足
    　　周官云貉不渡汶水鸜鹆不渡济水鲁国无鸜鹆来巢记异也
    　　橘渡江北化为枳今之江东甚有枳橘
    　　百足一名马蚿中断成两段各行而去
    　　物理
    　　凡月晕随灰画之随所画而阙【淮南子云未详其法】
    　　麒麟鬭而日蚀鲸鱼死而彗星出婴儿号而母乳出咡丝而商絶
    　　庄子云地三年种蜀黍其后七年多蛇
    　　积艾草三年后烧津液下流成鈆锡已试有验
    　　煎麻油水气尽无烟不复沸则还冷可以手搅之得水则焰起散卒不灭此亦试之有验
    　　庭州灞水以金银铁器盛之皆漏唯瓠叶则不漏龙肉以醢渍之则文章生 [22]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [22]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 鸐雉长毛雨雪惜其尾栖高树杪不敢下食徃徃饿死 - Describes a bird (鸐雉) that, due to its long tail, is reluctant to descend from high trees in the rain and snow, often leading to starvation.
    • 魏景初中时人所説 - Refers to a saying from the early years of the Wei Jing era, indicating a historical reference.
    • 鹳水鸟也伏卵时则不鸣卵冷取礜石用绕卵以时助燥气 - Describes the behavior of a water bird (鹳) during nesting, where it does not make sounds when the eggs are cold and uses a mineral (礜石) to help keep them warm.
    • 山鸡有美毛自爱其色终日映水目则溺死 - Describes a mountain chicken (山鸡) with beautiful feathers that loves its appearance so much that it spends all day reflecting in water, eventually drowning due to its vanity.
    • 三千嵗游于莲叶巢于卷耳之上 - Describes a turtle () that lives for three thousand years, swimming among lotus leaves and nesting on the tips of *aristolochia* plants.
    • 屠解其肌肉唯肠连其头而经日不死犹能齧物鸟徃食之则为所得渔者或以取鸟遇神虵复续 - Describes a method of killing a turtle (屠) by cutting its muscles, leaving only the intestines connected to its head, which allows it to remain alive for days and still bite. Birds that eat it can be caught by fishermen, and if they encounter a divine snake, they can be revived.
    • 蛴螬以背行快于用足 - Describes a grub (蛴螬) that moves faster using its back than its feet.
    • 周官云貉不渡汶水鸜鹆不渡济水鲁国无鸜鹆来巢记异也 - Refers to a saying from the *Zhouguan* (周官) that mentions the貉 (a type of wild dog) not crossing the Wei River and the 鸜鹆 (a type of bird) not crossing the Ji River, noting that the absence of 鸜鹆 in Lu (鲁) is unusual.
    • 橘渡江北化为枳今之江东甚有枳橘 - Describes how oranges (橘) change into a different species (枳) when they cross the Yangtze River, and how this is common in the eastern part of the river.
    • 百足一名马蚿中断成两段各行而去 - Describes a centipede (百足) that can split into two segments and move independently.
    • 凡月晕随灰画之随所画而阙 - Describes a phenomenon where the moon's halo follows the outline of a drawing made with ash, leaving gaps where the drawing is incomplete.
    • 麒麟鬭而日蚀鲸鱼死而彗星出婴儿号而母乳出咡丝而商絶 - Describes various natural phenomena and their supposed causes, such as a fight between unicorns causing a solar eclipse, the death of a whale causing a comet to appear, a baby crying causing its mother's milk to flow, and a string being pulled causing a musical instrument to break.
    • 庄子云地三年种蜀黍其后七年多蛇 - Refers to a statement from *Zhuangzi* (庄子) that planting millet for three years leads to an increase in snakes after seven years.
    • 积艾草三年后烧津液下流成鈆锡已试有验 - Describes a method of burning mugwort (艾草) for three years, where the distilled liquid turns into lead and tin, and this has been tested and proven effective.
    • 煎麻油水气尽无烟不复沸则还冷可以手搅之得水则焰起散卒不灭此亦试之有验 - Describes a method of heating sesame oil until all the water evaporates, the oil stops smoking and boiling, and then cooling it to a point where it can be stirred with hands. If water is added, the oil will ignite and continue to burn, and this method has been tested and proven effective.
    • 庭州灞水以金银铁器盛之皆漏唯瓠叶则不漏龙肉以醢渍之则文章生 - Describes a phenomenon where water from the Bashi River (灞水) in Tingzhou (庭州) leaks through gold, silver, and iron containers but not through gourd leaves. It also describes how dragon meat, when soaked in vinegar (醢), produces patterns.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 魏景初中时人所説 - Refers to a specific historical period and a saying from that time, indicating the cultural context of the text.
    • 周官云 - Refers to the *Zhouguan* (周官), an ancient Chinese text that deals with state organization and rituals.
    • 庄子云 - Refers to *Zhuangzi* (庄子), a foundational text of Daoism that often contains philosophical and fantastical elements.
    • 橘渡江北化为枳 - Refers to a well-known Chinese saying about the transformation of oranges into a different species when they cross the Yangtze River, symbolizing the idea that things change when they move to a new environment.
    • 百足一名马蚿 - Refers to a centipede, which is known in Chinese culture for its ability to split into two segments and move independently.

    Final Translation

    The 鸐雉, a bird with long tail feathers, fears to descend from high trees in the rain and snow, valuing its tail so much that it often starves to death. This is a saying from the early years of the Wei Jing era.

    The 鹳 is a water bird. During the nesting period, it does not make sounds when the eggs are cold and uses a mineral called 礜石 to keep the eggs warm by wrapping it around them, thus aiding the warmth.

    The 山鸡 has beautiful feathers and loves its appearance so much that it spends all day reflecting in water, eventually drowning due to its vanity.

    The 龟 lives for three thousand years, swimming among lotus leaves and nesting on the tips of aristolochia plants.

    The method of killing a 龟 involves cutting its muscles, leaving only the intestines connected to its head. This allows the turtle to remain alive for days and still bite. Birds that eat it can be caught by fishermen, and if they encounter a divine snake, they can be revived.

    The 蛴螬 (grub) moves faster using its back than its feet.

    According to the Zhouguan, the 貉 (a type of wild dog) does not cross the Wei River, and the 鸲鹆 (a type of bird) does not cross the Ji River. The absence of 鸲鹆 in the state of Lu is unusual.

    The 橘 (orange) changes into a different species called 枳 when it crosses the Yangtze River, and this is common in the eastern part of the river.

    The 百足 (centipede), also known as 马蚿, can split into two segments and move independently.

    Physical phenomena include the moon's halo following the outline of a drawing made with ash, leaving gaps where the drawing is incomplete.

    The 麒麟 (unicorn) fight causes a solar eclipse, the death of a 鲸鱼 (whale) causes a comet to appear, a baby crying causes its mother's milk to flow, and pulling a string causes a musical instrument to break.

    According to Zhuangzi, planting 蜀黍 (millet) for three years leads to an increase in snakes after seven years.

    Burning 艾草 (mugwort) for three years, the distilled liquid turns into lead and tin, and this has been tested and proven effective.

    Heating 麻油 (sesame oil) until all the water evaporates, the oil stops smoking and boiling, and then cooling it to a point where it can be stirred with hands. If water is added, the oil will ignite and continue to burn, and this method has been tested and proven effective.

    In Tingzhou, the water from the 灞水 (Bashi River) leaks through gold, silver, and iron containers but not through gourd leaves. Dragon meat, when soaked in vinegar, produces patterns. [22]

    积油满万石则自然生火武帝泰始中武库火积油所致也
    　　物
    　　烧铅锡成胡粉犹也烧丹朱成水银则不物同异用者
    　　石　魏文帝所记诸物相似乱眞者
    　　碔砆怪石似美玉　　蛇牀乱蘼芜
    　　荠苨乱人参　　　　杜衡乱细辛
    　　雄黄似石流黄　　　鳊鱼相乱以有大小相异
    　　敌木乱门冬　　　　百部似门冬
    　　房葵似狼毒　　　　钩吻草与荇华相似拔揳与萆薢相似一名狗脊
    　　药物
    　　乌头天雄附子一物春秋冬夏采各异也
    　　逺志苗曰小草根曰逺志
    　　芎防苗曰江蓠根曰芎防
    　　菊有二种苗花如一唯味小异苦者不中食
    　　埜葛食之杀人家葛种之三年不后旅生亦不可食神仙传云松栢脂入地千年化为茯苓茯苓化为琥珀琥珀一名江珠今泰山出茯苓而无琥珀益州永昌出琥珀而无茯苓或云烧蜂巢所作未详此二説
    　　地黄蓝首断心分根复种皆生女萝寄生兔丝兔丝寄生木上生根不着地 [23]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [23]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 积油满万石则自然生火 - Literal expression meaning 'When oil accumulates to ten thousand stones, it naturally ignites.' Refers to the phenomenon where a large amount of oil can spontaneously combust.
    • 武帝泰始中 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Wu of Han during the Tai Shi period, a specific time frame in Chinese history.
    • 物类 - General term for 'kinds of things' or 'categories of objects.'
    • 烧铅锡成胡粉 - Refers to the process of burning lead and tin to produce 'hu powder,' a type of cosmetic or pigment.
    • 犹类也 - Means 'it is still of the same kind' or 'it remains similar.'
    • 石流黄 - Refers to 'stone sulfur' or 'sulfur ore.'
    • 蛇床乱蘼芜 - Refers to the confusion between snake bed and sweet flag, two plants that look similar.
    • 荠苨乱人参 - Refers to the confusion between wild carrot and ginseng, two plants that are often mistaken for each other.
    • 杜衡乱细辛 - Refers to the confusion between Du Huang and fine cinnamon, two herbs that are often confused.
    • 雄黄似石流黄 - Refers to the similarity between orpiment (a mineral) and sulfur ore.
    • 鳊鱼相乱以有大小相异 - Refers to the confusion between different sizes of bream fish.
    • 敌木乱门冬 - Refers to the confusion between enemy wood and wintergreen, two plants that are often mistaken for each other.
    • 百部似门冬 - Refers to the similarity between Baimu and wintergreen.
    • 房葵似狼毒 - Refers to the similarity between Fanggui and wolfsbane, two plants that are often mistaken for each other.
    • 钩吻草与荇华相似 - Refers to the similarity between Huoguancao (a type of herb) and water caltrop, two plants that look alike.
    • 拔揳与萆薢相似 - Refers to the similarity between Baixie and Bixie, two plants that are often mistaken for each other.
    • 一名狗脊 - Refers to another name for Bixie, which is also known as 'dog's spine.'
    • 乌头天雄附子 - Refers to three different types of aconite, a toxic plant used in traditional medicine.
    • 春秋冬夏采各异也 - Means 'collected at different times of the year,' indicating that the different parts of the aconite plant are harvested in different seasons.
    • 远志苗曰小草根曰远志 - Refers to the different names for the herb *Polygala tenuifolia*: 'small grass' for the leaves and 'far-reaching intention' for the root.
    • 芎防苗曰江蓠根曰芎防 - Refers to the different names for the herb *Ligusticum chuanxiong*: 'river moss' for the leaves and 'chuanxiong' for the root.
    • 菊有二种苗花如一唯味小异苦者不中食 - Refers to the two types of chrysanthemum, which look similar but differ in taste, with the bitter one being inedible.
    • 野葛食之杀人家葛种之三年不生后旅生亦不可食 - Refers to the poisonous wild kudzu, which can be fatal if eaten, and the cultivated kudzu, which takes three years to grow and is also inedible when it does.
    • 神仙传云松柏脂入地千年化为茯苓茯苓化为琥珀 - Refers to the legend that pine resin buried for a thousand years turns into *fu ling* (poria), which then turns into amber.
    • 琥珀一名江珠今泰山出茯苓而无琥珀益州永昌出琥珀而无茯苓 - Refers to the fact that amber is called 'river pearl' and is found in Yizhou (Yunnan), while *fu ling* is found in Taishan (Shandong) but not amber.
    • 或云烧蜂巢所作未详此二说 - Refers to the uncertainty about whether amber is formed by burning beehives or through the natural process described in the previous sentence.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 武帝泰始中 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Wu of Han during the Tai Shi period, a specific time frame in Chinese history.
    • 武库火 - Refers to a fire in the imperial arsenal, which was a significant event during the reign of Emperor Wu.
    • 远志 - Refers to the herb *Polygala tenuifolia*, which is used in traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 芎防 - Refers to the herb *Ligusticum chuanxiong*, which is used in traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 乌头天雄附子 - Refers to three different types of aconite, a toxic plant used in traditional medicine.
    • 茯苓 - Refers to *fu ling* (poria), a medicinal fungus used in traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 琥珀 - Refers to amber, a fossilized tree resin used in traditional Chinese medicine and as a decorative material.
    • 神仙传 - Refers to the *Shenxian Zhuan* (Biographies of Immortals), a collection of stories about legendary figures in Chinese mythology.

    Proper Nouns

    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 泰始 - The Tai Shi period, a specific time frame in the reign of Emperor Wu.
    • 武库 - The imperial arsenal.
    • 远志 - The herb *Polygala tenuifolia*.
    • 芎防 - The herb *Ligusticum chuanxiong*.
    • 乌头天雄附子 - Three types of aconite.
    • 茯苓 - *Fu ling* (poria).
    • 琥珀 - Amber.
    • 神仙传 - The *Shenxian Zhuan* (Biographies of Immortals).
    • 泰山 - Mount Tai, a famous mountain in Shandong.
    • 益州永昌 - Yizhou (Yunnan), a region in southwestern China.

    When oil accumulates to ten thousand stones, it naturally ignites. During the Tai Shi period of Emperor Wu, a fire broke out in the imperial arsenal, caused by the accumulated oil.

    Categories of Things

    Burning lead and tin to produce hu powder is still of the same kind. Burning cinnabar to produce mercury is not of the same kind, even though the materials are similar but the uses differ.

    Stones
    According to Cao Pi, the emperor of Wei, there are many things that look similar but are not genuine.

    • Wufu stones, strange stones that resemble fine jade.
    • Snake bed and sweet flag, often confused for one another.
    • Wild carrot and ginseng, often mistaken for each other.
    • Du Huang and fine cinnamon, often confused.
    • Orpiment, which resembles sulfur ore.
    • Bream fish, which are often confused due to their varying sizes.
    • Enemy wood and wintergreen, often mistaken for each other.
    • Baimu and wintergreen, which look similar.
    • Fanggui and wolfsbane, which resemble each other.
    • Huoguancao (a type of herb) and water caltrop, which look alike.
    • Baixie and Bixie, which are often confused, with Bixie also known as "dog's spine."

    Medicinal Herbs

    Aconite, tianxiong, and fuzi are all parts of the same plant, but they are harvested at different times of the year: spring, summer, autumn, and winter.

    Far志
    The leaves of the herb Polygala tenuifolia are called "small grass," and the root is called "far-reaching intention."

    Chuanxiong
    The leaves of the herb Ligusticum chuanxiong are called "river moss," and the root is called "chuanxiong."

    Chrysanthemum
    There are two types of chrysanthemum. Their leaves and flowers look the same, but their tastes differ slightly, with the bitter one being inedible.

    Wild Kudzu
    Eating wild kudzu is fatal, while cultivated kudzu takes three years to grow and, when it does, is also inedible. The Shenxian Zhuan (Biographies of Immortals) states that pine resin buried for a thousand years turns into fu ling (poria), which then turns into amber. Amber is also called "river pearl." Today, fu ling is found in Mount Tai but not amber, while amber is found in Yizhou (Yunnan) but not fu ling. Some say that amber is formed by burning beehives, but this is unclear.

    Dihuang
    The root of the herb Rehmannia glutinosa is called "dihuang." When the root is cut and replanted, it grows new shoots and produces tangkuei (dendrobium), which is a parasitic plant. Tangkuei grows on trees and has roots that do not touch the ground. [23]

    槿花朝生夕死
    　　药论
    　　神农经曰上药养命谓五石之练形六芝之延年也中药养性合欢蠲忿萱草忘忧下药治病谓大黄除实当归止痛夫命之所以延性之所以利病之所以止当其药应以病也违其药失其应即怨天尤人説神矣
    　　神农经曰药物有大毒不可入口鼻耳目者即杀人凡六物焉【一曰钩吻似黄精不相连根苗独生者是也二曰鸱状如雌鸡生中山三曰隂命赤色着木悬其子出海中四曰内童状如鵞亦生海中五曰鸩羽如雀黒头赤喙六曰防生海中雄曰防雌曰也】
    　　神农经曰药种有五毒一曰狼毒占斯解之二曰巴豆霍汁解之三曰黎卢汤解之四曰天雄乌头大豆解之五曰班猫戎盐解之毒菜害小儿乳汁解多食饮二升
    　　食忌
    　　人啖豆三年则身重行止难
    　　啖榆则眠不欲觉
    　　啖麦稼令人有力健行
    　　饮眞茶令人少眠
    　　人常食小豆令人肌肥麤燥
    　　食鷰麦令人骨节断解
    　　人食鷰肉不可入水为蛟龙所吞 [24]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [24]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 槿花朝生夕死 - Classical idiom describing the short-lived nature of the mallow flower, symbolizing fleeting beauty or transient life.
    • 藥論 - Discourse or treatise on medicine.
    • 神農經曰 - Reference to the *Shennong Bencao Jing*, an ancient Chinese pharmacopoeia attributed to Shennong.
    • 上藥養命 - Refers to 'upper-grade medicines' that nourish life, typically associated with longevity and vitality.
    • 五石之練形 - Refers to the use of five minerals or stones to refine or transform the body.
    • 六芝之延年 - Refers to the use of six types of mushrooms (灵芝) to extend life.
    • 中药养性 - Refers to 'middle-grade medicines' that cultivate or regulate the mind and emotions.
    • 合欢蠲忿 - Refers to the use of *hehuan* to alleviate anger or resentment.
    • 萱草忘忧 - Refers to the use of *xuancao* to dispel worries or sadness.
    • 下药治病 - Refers to 'lower-grade medicines' that treat specific illnesses.
    • 大黄除实 - Refers to the use of *dahuang* to purge or detoxify.
    • 当归止痛 - Refers to the use of *danggui* to relieve pain.
    • 夫命之所以延 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Why does life extend?'
    • 性之所以利 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Why is the mind or nature beneficial?'
    • 病之所以止 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Why does illness cease?'
    • 当其药应以病也 - Refers to the proper use of medicine in response to illness.
    • 违其药失其应即怨天尤人説神矣 - Refers to the tendency to blame heaven or others when medicine fails, and to resort to superstition.
    • 药种有五毒 - Refers to the five toxic plants or substances.
    • 狼毒 - Toxic herb known as *langdu*.
    • 占斯解之 - Refers to the use of *zhanshi* to counteract the poison.
    • 巴豆 - Toxic herb known as *badou*.
    • 霍汁解之 - Refers to the use of *huoju* to counteract the poison.
    • 黎卢 - Toxic herb known as *lilu*.
    • 汤解之 - Refers to the use of *tang* to counteract the poison.
    • 天雄 - Toxic herb known as *tianxiong*.
    • 乌头 - Toxic herb known as *wutou*.
    • 大豆解之 - Refers to the use of *doudou* to counteract the poison.
    • 班猫 - Toxic herb known as *banmao*.
    • 戎盐解之 - Refers to the use of *rongyan* to counteract the poison.
    • 毒菜害小儿 - Refers to the harmful effects of toxic plants on children.
    • 乳汁解多食饮二升 - Refers to the use of *ruju* to counteract the effects of overeating toxic plants.
    • 人啖豆三年则身重行止难 - Refers to the long-term effects of consuming beans for three years, leading to heaviness and difficulty in movement.
    • 啖榆则眠不欲觉 - Refers to the effects of eating *yu* wood, causing drowsiness and difficulty waking.
    • 啖麦稼令人有力健行 - Refers to the effects of eating *mai* crops, providing strength and agility.
    • 饮眞茶令人少眠 - Refers to the effects of drinking *zhentea*, reducing sleep.
    • 人常食小豆令人肌肥麤燥 - Refers to the effects of regularly eating *xiaodou*, causing the skin to become thick and dry.
    • 食鷰麦令人骨节断解 - Refers to the effects of eating *yimai*, causing bones to become brittle and break easily.
    • 人食鷰肉不可入水为蛟龙所吞 - Refers to the danger of eating *yirou*, as it may attract water spirits or dragons.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 神农经 - The *Shennong Bencao Jing* is one of the oldest Chinese texts on herbal medicine, attributed to Shennong, a mythical figure credited with discovering the medicinal properties of plants.
    • 五石 - Refers to five minerals or stones used in traditional Chinese medicine for alchemical purposes.
    • 六芝 - Refers to six types of mushrooms, particularly *lingzhi*, which are highly valued for their purported health benefits.
    • 合欢 - A plant known for its calming and soothing properties.
    • 萱草 - A plant known for its ability to alleviate sadness and stress.
    • 大黄 - A herb used for its laxative and detoxifying properties.
    • 当归 - A herb used for its blood-nourishing and pain-relieving properties.
    • 狼毒 - A toxic herb used in traditional medicine for its strong effects.
    • 巴豆 - A toxic herb used for its purgative properties.
    • 黎卢 - A toxic herb used in traditional medicine.
    • 天雄 - A toxic herb used for its warming and invigorating properties.
    • 乌头 - A toxic herb used for its analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties.
    • 班猫 - A toxic herb used in traditional medicine.
    • 戎盐 - A type of salt used in traditional medicine.
    • 毒菜 - Refers to toxic plants that can harm children.
    • 乳汁 - Refers to breast milk, which is used in traditional medicine to counteract certain poisons.
    • 小豆 - Refers to small beans, which are used in traditional medicine.
    • 鷰麦 - Refers to a type of grain that can cause bone brittleness.
    • 鷰肉 - Refers to a type of meat that is considered dangerous to consume.

    Proper Nouns

    • 神农 - Mythical figure credited with discovering the medicinal properties of plants.
    • 合欢 - Plant known for its calming and soothing properties.
    • 萱草 - Plant known for its ability to alleviate sadness and stress.
    • 大黄 - Herb used for its laxative and detoxifying properties.
    • 当归 - Herb used for its blood-nourishing and pain-relieving properties.
    • 狼毒 - Toxic herb used in traditional medicine for its strong effects.
    • 巴豆 - Toxic herb used for its purgative properties.
    • 黎卢 - Toxic herb used in traditional medicine.
    • 天雄 - Toxic herb used for its warming and invigorating properties.
    • 乌头 - Toxic herb used for its analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties.
    • 班猫 - Toxic herb used in traditional medicine.
    • 戎盐 - Type of salt used in traditional medicine.
    • 乳汁 - Breast milk, used in traditional medicine to counteract certain poisons.
    • 小豆 - Small beans, used in traditional medicine.
    • 鷰麦 - Type of grain that can cause bone brittleness.
    • 鷰肉 - Type of meat considered dangerous to consume.

    Mallow Blossoms Bloom and Wither in a Single Day
    　　Discourse on Medicines
    　　The Shennong Bencao Jing states that upper-grade medicines nourish life, referring to the refinement of the body through the use of five minerals and the extension of life through the six types of mushrooms. Middle-grade medicines cultivate the mind, alleviating anger with hehuan and dispelling worries with xuancao. Lower-grade medicines treat illnesses, using dahuang to purge and danggui to relieve pain. Why does life extend? Why is the mind beneficial? Why does illness cease? When medicine is properly applied in response to illness, but when it is misused and fails, people blame heaven, others, and resort to superstition.
    　　The Shennong Bencao Jing states that there are six highly toxic substances that, if ingested or inhaled through the nose, eyes, or ears, can kill. These are: [1] gouwen, which resembles huangjing but grows as a single root without connected stems; [2] chi, which resembles a hen and is found in the mountains of Zhongshan; [3] yinming, which is red and attaches to trees, with its seeds hanging from the tree and originating from the sea; [4] neitong, which resembles a goose and is also found in the sea; [5] zhen, which has feathers like a sparrow with a black head and red beak; [6] jiefang, which lives in the sea, with the male called fang and the female called jie.
    　　The Shennong Bencao Jing states that there are five toxic plants: [1] langdu, which is counteracted by zhanshi; [2] badou, which is counteracted by huoju; [3] lilu, which is counteracted by tang; [4] tianxiong and wutou, which are counteracted by doudou; [5] banmao, which is counteracted by rongyan. Toxic plants harm children, and ruju can counteract their effects if consumed in excess.
    　　Taboos of Food
    　　Eating beans for three years leads to heaviness and difficulty in movement.
    　　Eating yu wood causes drowsiness and difficulty waking.
    　　Eating mai crops provides strength and agility.
    　　Drinking zhentea reduces sleep.
    　　Regularly eating xiaodou causes the skin to become thick and dry.
    　　Eating yimai causes bones to become brittle and break easily.
    　　Eating yirou is dangerous, as it may attract water spirits or dragons. [24]

    人食冬葵为狗所齧疮不差或致死
    　　马食谷则足重不能行
    　　鴈食粟则翼重不能飞
    　　药术
    　　胡粉白石灰等以水和之涂髩须不白涂讫着油单裹令温煗候欲燥未燥间洗之汤则不得着晩晩则多折用暖汤洗讫泽涂之欲染当洗髩须有腻不着药临染时亦当拭须燥温之
    　　陈葵子微火炒令爆咤散着熟地遍蹋之朝种暮生逺不过经宿耳
    　　陈葵子秋种覆盖令经冬不死春有子也【周日用曰愚闻熟地植生菜兰将石流黄筛于其上以盆覆之即时可待又以变白牡丹为五色皆须沃其根以紫草汁则变紫红花汁则变红并未试于理可为此出尔雅注】
    　　烧马蹄羊角成灰春夏散着湿地生罗勒
    　　蠏漆相合成为神仙药服食方云
    　　戏术
    　　削氷令圆举以向日以艾于后承其影则得火
    　　取火法如用珠取火多有説者此未试
    　　神农本草云鸡卵可作琥珀其法取伏卵毈黄白浑杂者煮及尚软随意刻作物件以苦酒渍数宿既坚内着粉中佳者乃乱眞矣此世所恒用作无不成者 [25]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [25]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為之一空 - Set phrase meaning completely emptied; fields were left barren.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 冬葵 - A type of vegetable, often used in traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 胡粉 - A type of cinnabar or vermilion powder, often used in cosmetics.
    • 白石灰 - White lime, used in traditional Chinese medicine and construction.
    • 羅勒 - Basil, a herb used in cooking and traditional medicine.
    • 神農本草 - A classical text on herbal medicine, attributed to Shennong, the legendary founder of Chinese agriculture and medicine.
    • 琥珀 - Amber, a fossilized resin, often used in jewelry and traditional medicine.

    Proper Nouns

    • 紹興 - Era name of the Southern Song; rendered as Shaoxing.

    People who eat winter melon may be bitten by dogs, resulting in wounds that do not heal or even lead to death.
    Horses that eat grain may have heavy hooves and be unable to walk.
    Geese that eat millet may have heavy wings and be unable to fly.

    Medicinal Techniques
    Mix cinnabar, white lime, and other substances with water, apply it to the hair and beard to prevent them from turning white. After applying, wrap it in oil paper to keep it warm. Wash it when it is almost dry but not yet dry. Do not use hot water, as frequent use of warm water to wash and then apply a moisturizer is recommended. When dyeing, wash the hair and beard thoroughly. If there is grease, do not apply the medicine. When dyeing, also wipe the beard dry and warm it.

    Old winter melon seeds are lightly roasted until they pop, then spread on fertile land and trample it. Plant in the morning and it will grow by evening, but it does not grow far, only within a night.
    Plant old winter melon seeds in autumn, cover them so they do not die through winter, and in spring, they will produce seeds.

    Burn horse hooves and sheep horns to ashes, sprinkle the ashes on wet land in spring and summer to grow basil.
    Crab shell and lacquer combined together become an elixir of immortality. The recipe for consumption is said to be...

    Tricks
