    Carve ice into a circle, hold it toward the sun, and place mugwort behind it to catch the shadow, then you can get fire.
    The method of taking fire is similar to using a gemstone to take fire; there are many explanations, but this has not been tested.

    According to the Shennong Bencao, chicken eggs can be made into amber. The method is to take eggs that are still warm and have a mixture of yolk and white, cook them until they are still soft and carve them into any shape. Soak them in vinegar for several nights. Once hardened, place them in powder. The best ones are indistinguishable from real amber. This is a common practice, and nothing is unachievable. [25]

    烧白石作白灰既讫积着地经日都冷遇雨及水浇即更燃烟焰起
    　　五月五日埋蜻蜓头于西向户下埋至三日不蚀则化成青眞珠又云埋于正中门
    　　蜥蜴或名蝘蜒以器养之食以朱砂体尽赤所食满七斤治捣万杵防女人肢体终身不灭唯房室事则灭故号守宫传云东方朔奏汉武帝试之有验
    　　取鼈挫令如碁子大捣赤苋汁和合厚以茅苞五六月中作投地中经旬脔脔尽成鼈也

    　　博物志卷四
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　博物志卷五
    　　晋　张华　撰
    　　方士
    　　魏武帝好养性法亦解方药招引四方之术士如左元放华佗之徒无不毕至【周日用曰操虽好竒而心无道如何招引方术之人乎如囚左元放而将欲杀之非变化必至灭身故有道者不合亲之矣既要试术则何可乎】
    　　魏王所集方士名
    　　上党王眞　　　　　陇西封君达
    　　甘陵甘始　　　　　鲁女生 [26]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [26]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 烧白石作白灰既讫积着地经日都冷遇雨及水浇即更燃烟焰起 - Describes the process of burning white stone to make lime, which cools down after the process but reignites when exposed to rain or water.
    • 五月五日埋蜻蜓头于西向户下埋至三日不蚀则化成青眞珠 - Describes a folk belief that burying a dragonfly head on the fifth day of the fifth month in the west-facing direction can transform it into a green pearl after three days.
    • 蜥蜴或名蝘蜒以器养之食以朱砂体尽赤所食满七斤治捣万杵防女人肢体终身不灭唯房室事则灭故号守宫 - Describes a ritual involving lizards fed with cinnabar, which turn red and are believed to have magical properties, preventing a woman's body from decaying forever except in matters of intimacy.
    • 取鼈挫令如碁子大捣赤苋汁和合厚以茅苞五六月中作投地中经旬脔脔尽成鼈也 - Describes a ritual involving turtles ground into a paste and mixed with red amaranth juice, then buried in the ground to transform into turtles after ten days.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 博物志卷四 - The fourth volume of the *Book of Marvels* (博物志), a classical Chinese text compiled by Zhang Hua during the Jin Dynasty.
    • 钦定四库全书 - The *Four Treasuries* (四库全书), a massive compilation of Chinese literature commissioned by the Qing Dynasty.
    • 博物志卷五 - The fifth volume of the *Book of Marvels* (博物志).
    • 晋　张华　撰 - Indicates that the text was compiled by Zhang Hua during the Jin Dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 博物志卷四 - The fourth volume of the *Book of Marvels* (博物志).
    • 钦定四库全书 - The *Four Treasuries* (四库全书).
    • 博物志卷五 - The fifth volume of the *Book of Marvels* (博物志).
    • 晋　张华　撰 - Zhang Hua, the compiler of the *Book of Marvels* during the Jin Dynasty.

    Burn white stone to make lime. After the process is complete, the lime is piled on the ground and cools down after several days. When exposed to rain or water, it reignites and produces smoke and flames.

    On the fifth day of the fifth month, bury the head of a dragonfly in the west-facing direction. After three days, if it is not corroded, it will transform into a green pearl.

    Lizards, also known as geckos, are kept in a container and fed with cinnabar. Their bodies turn entirely red. When the amount of cinnabar they consume reaches seven pounds, they are ground and pounded into a paste. This paste is used to prevent a woman's body from decaying forever, except in matters of intimacy. Therefore, they are called Shou Gong (守宫).

    Take a turtle, grind it into a paste as large as a chess piece, mix it with red amaranth juice, and pack it tightly with straw. In the fifth or sixth month, bury it in the ground. After ten days, it will transform into a turtle.

    Volume Four of the Book of Marvels (博物志卷四)
    The Four Treasuries (钦定四库全书)
    Volume Five of the Book of Marvels (博物志卷五)
    Compiled by Zhang Hua during the Jin Dynasty (晋　张华　撰)

    Taoists
    Emperor Wei Wu was fond of longevity practices and also understood medicinal formulas. He attracted Taoist practitioners from all over, such as Zuo Yuanfang and Hua Tuo, who all came to him.

    Names of the Taoists gathered by Prince Wei
    Wang Zhen of Shangdang
    Feng Junda of Longxi
    Gan Shi of Ganling
    Lu Nvsheng of Lu [26]

    谯国华佗字元化　　东郭延年
    　　唐霅　　　　　　　冷寿光
    　　河南卜式　　　　　张貂
    　　蓟子训　　　　　　汝南费长房
    　　鲜奴辜　　　　　　魏国军吏河南赵圣卿阳城郄俭字孟节　　庐江左慈字元放
    　　右十六人魏文帝东阿王仲长统所説皆能断谷不食分形隐没出入不由门户左慈能变形幻人视听厌胜魅皆此也周礼所谓怪民王制称挟左道者也
    　　魏时方士甘陵甘始庐江有左慈阳城有郄俭始能行气导引慈晓房中之术善辟谷不食悉号二百嵗人凡如此之徒武帝皆集之于魏不使游散甘始老而少容曹子建密问其所行始言本师姓韩字世雄尝与师于南海作金投数万斤于海又取鲤一双一鲤傅药游釜中若处渊其无药者已熟而食言此药去此逾逺万里已不可行人不能得也
    　　皇甫隆遇青牛道士姓封名君达其与养性法皆可仿用大略云体欲常少劳无过虚食去肥浓节酸咸减思虑损喜怒除驰逐慎房室春夏泄泻秋冬闭藏详别篇武帝行之有效 [27]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [27]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 谯国华佗字元化 - Classical format for introducing a person, including their place of origin, name, and courtesy name.
    • 东郭延年 - Classical format for introducing a person, with '东郭' being a surname and '延年' the given name.
    • 唐霅 - A proper name, likely referring to a person or place.
    • 冷寿光 - A proper name, likely referring to a person or place.
    • 河南卜式 - A proper name, with '河南' indicating the place of origin and '卜式' the given name.
    • 蓟子训 - A proper name, with '蓟' being a surname and '子训' the given name.
    • 汝南费长房 - A proper name, with '汝南' indicating the place of origin and '费长房' the given name.
    • 鲜奴辜 - A proper name, likely referring to a person.
    • 魏国军吏河南赵圣卿阳城郄俭字孟节 - A complex proper name, including the place of origin ('魏国军吏河南'), the given name ('赵圣卿'), and the courtesy name ('郄俭字孟节').
    • 庐江左慈字元放 - A proper name, with '庐江' indicating the place of origin and '左慈字元放' the given name and courtesy name.
    • 断谷不食 - Able to abstain from food.
    • 分形隐没 - Able to divide their form and disappear.
    • 出入不由门户 - Able to enter and exit without doors.
    • 变形幻人 - Able to transform and deceive people.
    • 厌胜鬼魅 - Able to suppress ghosts and demons.
    • 行气导引 - Practicing qi cultivation and guiding.
    • 房中之术 - Refers to sexual techniques or practices.
    • 辟谷 - A Taoist practice of abstaining from food.
    • 养性法 - Methods for cultivating the body and mind, often associated with Taoist practices.
    • 体欲常少劳 - The body should be kept lightly active.
    • 慎房室 - Be cautious in intimate relations.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 谯国、东郭、唐霅、冷寿光、河南、蓟、汝南、庐江、阳城 - Place names or surnames, indicating the origins or affiliations of the individuals listed.
    • 魏文帝、东阿王、仲长统 - Historical figures, with '魏文帝' referring to Emperor Wen of Wei, '东阿王' likely referring to a noble title, and '仲长统' a historical figure.
    • 方士 - Refers to Taoist practitioners or alchemists in ancient China.
    • 青牛道士 - A Taoist figure, possibly referring to Laozi (Lao Tzu), who is traditionally associated with a green ox.
    • 武帝 - Likely referring to Emperor Wu of Han or another historical emperor.

    Proper Nouns

    • 谯国华佗字元化 - Proper name, with '谯国' indicating the place of origin, '华佗' the given name, and '元化' the courtesy name.
    • 东郭延年 - Proper name, with '东郭' being a surname and '延年' the given name.
    • 唐霅 - Proper name, likely referring to a person or place.
    • 冷寿光 - Proper name, likely referring to a person or place.
    • 河南卜式 - Proper name, with '河南' indicating the place of origin and '卜式' the given name.
    • 蓟子训 - Proper name, with '蓟' being a surname and '子训' the given name.
    • 汝南费长房 - Proper name, with '汝南' indicating the place of origin and '费长房' the given name.
    • 鲜奴辜 - Proper name, likely referring to a person.
    • 魏国军吏河南赵圣卿阳城郄俭字孟节 - Proper name, with '魏国军吏河南' indicating the place of origin, '赵圣卿' the given name, and '郄俭字孟节' the courtesy name.
    • 庐江左慈字元放 - Proper name, with '庐江' indicating the place of origin and '左慈字元放' the given name and courtesy name.
    • 魏文帝、东阿王、仲长统 - Historical figures.
    • 甘陵甘始、左慈、郄俭 - Proper names, likely referring to Taoist practitioners.
    • 青牛道士、封君达 - Proper names, likely referring to Taoist figures.
    • 武帝 - Likely referring to Emperor Wu of Han or another historical emperor.

    Tao Guo Hua Tuo, courtesy name Yuanhua; Dongguo Yanyan
    Tang Zha; Leng Shouguang
    Henan Bu Shi; Zhang Mao
    Ji Zixun; Runan Fei Changfang
    Xian Nugu; Wei Guo Junli Henan Zhao Shengqing Yangcheng Xie Jian, courtesy name Mengjie; Lujiang Zuo Ci, courtesy name Yuanfang
    The above sixteen individuals, as mentioned by Emperor Wen of Wei, the Prince of Dong'e, and Zhong Changtong, were all capable of abstaining from food, dividing their forms and disappearing, entering and exiting without doors. Zuo Ci was particularly skilled at transforming and deceiving people, suppressing ghosts and demons, all of which fall into this category. This is what the Rites of Zhou calls "strange people" and the System of the State refers to as "those who practice left-handed ways."
    During the Wei dynasty, Daoist practitioners from Ganling, Gan Shi, and Lujiang, Zuo Ci, and Yangcheng, Xie Jian, were skilled in practicing qi cultivation and guiding, and Zuo Ci was knowledgeable in the art of sexual techniques, excelling in abstaining from food and being called a two-hundred-year-old. All such individuals were gathered by Emperor Wu of Wei in the state of Wei, and he did not allow them to wander. Gan Shi, though old, appeared young, and Cao Zijian secretly asked him about his practices. Gan Shi said his original teacher's surname was Han and his given name was Shixiong, and he had once practiced with his teacher in the South Sea, creating gold and casting it into the sea, as well as taking a pair of carp, one of which was coated with medicine and swam in a cauldron as if in a deep abyss, while the other, without the medicine, had already been cooked and eaten. He said this medicine was so powerful that it could not be obtained even if one traveled ten thousand miles away.
    Huangfu Long encountered a Taoist of the green ox named Feng Junda, and his methods for cultivating the body were all emulable. In general, one should keep the body lightly active, avoid excessive fasting, eliminate rich and greasy foods, moderate sour and salty flavors, reduce thoughts and worries, diminish joy and anger, eliminate rushing and chasing, and be cautious in intimate relations. In summer and autumn, one should release and in winter and spring, one should conserve, as detailed in another section. Emperor Wu of Wei practiced these methods and found them effective. [27]

    文帝典论曰陈思王曹植辩道论云世有方士悉招至之甘陵有甘始庐江有左慈阳城有郄俭始能行气俭善辟谷悉号二百嵗人自王与太子及余之兄弟咸以为调笑不全信之然甞试郄俭辟谷百日同与寝处行步起居自若也夫人不食七日则死而俭乃能如是左慈脩房中之术可以善终命然非有至情莫能行也甘始老而少容自诸术士咸共归之王使郄孟节主领诸人
    　　近魏明帝时河东有焦生者裸而不衣处火不燋入水不冻杜恕为太守亲所闻见皆有实事【周日用曰焦孝然迩河居一庵大雪庵倒人以为死而视之蒸气于雪畧无变色时或析薪惠人而已故魏书云自羲皇以来一人而已】
    　　颍川陈元方韩元长时之通才也所以并信有仙者其儿时所传闻河南密县有成公其人出行不知所至复来还语其家云我得仙因与家人辞诀而去其步渐高良久乃没而不见至今密县传其仙去二君并信有仙盖由此也【周日用曰岂惟二子乎】 [28]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [28]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 典論 - A collection of essays written by Cao Pi on literary and political matters.
    • 辟谷 - A Daoist practice of abstaining from food to achieve longevity or immortality.
    • 房中之術 - Refers to sexual techniques or practices believed to promote health and longevity.
    • 至情 - Deep or genuine emotions or feelings.
    • 辭诀而去 - Means 'to bid farewell and leave.'
    • 步漸高良久乃沒而不见 - Describes how the person gradually rose higher and then disappeared.
    • 至今密县传其仙去二君并信有仙盖由此也 - Means that until today, the people of Mian County believe that he has become a deity, and the two men also believe in the existence of deities because of this.
    • 岂惟二子乎 - Means 'not just these two people.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 文帝 - Refers to Cao Pi, the founder of the Wei dynasty.
    • 甘陵 - A place name.
    • 甘始 - A Daoist practitioner or alchemist.
    • 庐江 - A place name.
    • 左慈 - A famous Daoist practitioner or alchemist.
    • 阳城 - A place name.
    • 郄俭 - A Daoist practitioner or alchemist.
    • 杜恕 - A historical figure.
    • 周日用 - A commentator or scholar.
    • 焦孝然 - A name mentioned by Zhou Rizong.
    • 颍川陈元方 - A title or name.
    • 韩元长 - A title or name.
    • 河南密县 - A place name.
    • 成公 - A name.
    • 郄孟节 - A name.

    Proper Nouns

    • 文帝 - Cao Pi, the founder of the Wei dynasty.
    • 陈思王曹植 - Cao Zhi, a renowned poet and writer.
    • 甘陵 - A place name.
    • 甘始 - A Daoist practitioner or alchemist.
    • 庐江 - A place name.
    • 左慈 - A famous Daoist practitioner or alchemist.
    • 阳城 - A place name.
    • 郄俭 - A Daoist practitioner or alchemist.
    • 杜恕 - A historical figure.
    • 周日用 - A commentator or scholar.
    • 焦孝然 - A name mentioned by Zhou Rizong.
    • 颍川陈元方 - A title or name.
    • 韩元长 - A title or name.
    • 河南密县 - A place name.
    • 成公 - A name.
    • 郄孟节 - A name.

    Cao Pi's Dian Lun records that Prince Cao Zhi of Chen, in his Bian Dao Lun, said that there are Daoist practitioners in the world, all of whom were invited. In Ganling, there was Gan Shi; in Lujiang, there was Zuo Ci; and in Yangcheng, there was Xie Jian. Gan Shi could practice xingqi (breath regulation), and Xie Jian was skilled in pigui (food abstention). They were all said to live for two hundred years. The king, the crown prince, and my brothers all considered their claims to be jokes and did not fully believe them. However, they once tested Xie Jian's ability to abstain from food for a hundred days, and he lived with them, walking and living as usual. Normally, a person would die after seven days without food, but Xie Jian was able to do so. Zuo Ci practiced fangzhong zhi shu (sexual techniques) that could ensure a good end to life, but such practices could only be performed with genuine emotions. Gan Shi, despite being old, appeared youthful, and all the Daoist practitioners came to him. The king appointed Xie Mengjie to oversee them.

    During the reign of Emperor Ming of Wei, there was a man named Jiao Sheng from Hedong who went naked and did not wear clothes. He could sit in fire without being burned and enter water without freezing. Du Su, as the governor, personally witnessed and heard about these real events. [Zhou Rizong said: Jiao Xiaoran lived near the river in a hermitage. During a heavy snowfall, when the hermitage collapsed, people thought he was dead, but when they looked at him, he was emitting steam from the snow, showing no signs of change. Sometimes he would chop wood to help others, so the Wei Shu says he is the only one since the time of Fuxi.]

    Yingchuan's Chen Yuanfang and Han Yuanchang were scholars of their time. They both believed in the existence of deities because they had heard stories of such things when they were children. In Mian County, Henan, there was a man named Cheng Gong. He went out and was nowhere to be found, but when he returned, he told his family that he had become a deity. He then bid farewell to his family and left, gradually rising higher until he disappeared. Until today, the people of Mian County believe that he has become a deity, and the two men also believe in the existence of deities because of this. [Zhou Rizong said: Not just these two people.] [28]

    桓谭新论説方士有董仲君罪系狱佯死臭自户出既而复生
    　　黄帝问天老曰天地所生岂有食之令人不死者乎天老曰太阳之草名曰黄精饵而食之可以长生太隂之草名曰钩吻不可食入口立死人信钩吻之杀人不信黄精之益寿不亦惑乎【周日用曰草既杀人仍无益寿者也若杀人无验则益寿不可信矣】
    　　服食
    　　左元放荒年法择大豆粗细调匀必煮熟按之令有光焰气彻豆心内先不食一日以冷水顿服讫其鱼肉菜果不得复经口渴即饮水慎不可暖饮初小困十数日后体力壮健不复思食
    　　一法服三升为剂亦当随人先食多少増损之丰年欲复食者煮葵子及脂苏服肉羮渐渐饮之须豆下乃可食豆未尽而以实物塞肠则杀人矣此未试或可以然【周日用曰一法蜡涂黏饼炙饼令熟即涂之以意量多少即食之如常渴即饮冷水忌茶耳】 [29]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [29]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 桓谭新论 - Title of a work by Huan Tan, a philosopher and scholar from the Han dynasty.
    • 方士 - Refers to Daoist practitioners or alchemists who claimed to possess supernatural abilities or knowledge.
    • 董仲君 - Name of a specific Daoist practitioner or alchemist mentioned in the text.
    • 罪系狱 - Literally 'imprisoned for a crime,' referring to the legal punishment of being jailed.
    • 佯死 - Means 'feigned death' or 'pretended to die.'
    • 臭自户出 - Literally 'the smell came out from the door,' suggesting that the person's body began to decompose while they were still alive.
    • 既而复生 - Means 'and then revived' or 'and then came back to life.'
    • 黄帝问天老 - Refers to the legendary Yellow Emperor (Huangdi) asking the sage Tianlao (天老), a figure often associated with ancient wisdom and divination.
    • 天地所生 - Means 'what is produced by heaven and earth,' referring to natural substances or phenomena.
    • 食之令人不死者 - Means 'that can be eaten to make one immortal.'
    • 太阳之草 - Refers to a plant associated with the sun, possibly a metaphor for a powerful or beneficial herb.
    • 名曰黄精 - The name of the plant, 'huangjing' (黄精), which is a real herb known in traditional Chinese medicine for its purported health benefits.
    • 饵而食之 - Means 'to eat it as a remedy' or 'to consume it as a tonic.'
    • 可以长生 - Means 'can lead to immortality' or 'can prolong life indefinitely.'
    • 太阴之草 - Refers to a plant associated with the moon, possibly a metaphor for a harmful or deadly herb.
    • 名曰钩吻 - The name of the plant, 'gouwen' (钩吻), which is a real toxic plant known for its lethal effects.
    • 不可食 - Means 'cannot be eaten.'
    • 入口立死 - Means 'die immediately upon ingestion.'
    • 人信钩吻之杀人 - Means 'people believe that gouwen kills people.'
    • 不信黄精之益寿 - Means 'do not believe that huangjing extends life.'
    • 不亦惑乎 - Means 'is it not perplexing?' or 'is it not confusing?'
    • 服食 - Refers to the practice of consuming herbs or elixirs for health or longevity.
    • 左元放 - A name that appears to be a fictional or historical figure, possibly a Daoist practitioner.
    • 荒年 - Means 'famine' or 'year of scarcity.'
    • 法择大豆 - Means 'a method of selecting soybeans.'
    • 粗细调匀 - Means 'to mix coarse and fine beans evenly.'
    • 必煮熟 - Means 'must be cooked thoroughly.'
    • 按之令有光焰气彻豆心 - Means 'press them so that they emit a glow and the vapor penetrates the core of the beans.'
    • 内先不食一日 - Means 'before eating, abstain from food for one day.'
    • 以冷水顿服讫 - Means 'drink cold water immediately after.'
    • 其鱼肉菜果 - Refers to fish, meat, vegetables, and fruits.
    • 不得复经口 - Means 'should not be consumed again.'
    • 渴即饮水 - Means 'when thirsty, drink water.'
    • 慎不可暖饮 - Means 'be careful not to drink warm water.'
    • 初小困 - Means 'initially feel a bit weak.'
    • 十数日后 - Means 'after several days.'
    • 体力壮健 - Means 'physical strength and health improve.'
    • 不复思食 - Means 'no longer desire to eat.'
    • 一法服三升为剂 - Means 'one method involves taking three sheng (a unit of volume) as a dose.'
    • 亦当随人先食多少增损之 - Means 'it should also be adjusted according to the amount of food consumed beforehand.'
    • 丰年欲复食者 - Means 'in years of plenty, if one wishes to eat again.'
    • 煮葵子及脂苏 - Means 'cook kudzu seeds and sesame oil.'
    • 服肉羹 - Means 'consume meat soup.'
    • 渐渐饮之 - Means 'gradually drink it.'
    • 须豆下乃可食 - Means 'only after the beans have been consumed can one eat other foods.'
    • 豆未尽而以实物塞肠则杀人矣 - Means 'if the beans are not fully consumed and solid food is eaten, it can be fatal.'
    • 此未试或可以然 - Means 'this has not been tested, but it might work.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 桓谭新论 - Huan Tan's work is a significant text in Chinese philosophical literature, known for its critical and rational approach to Daoist and Confucian ideas.
    • 黄帝问天老 - The Yellow Emperor is a central figure in Chinese mythology, often depicted as a wise ruler and the founder of Chinese civilization. Tianlao (天老) is a sage figure associated with ancient wisdom and divination.
    • 黄精 - A real herb in traditional Chinese medicine, known for its purported health benefits and longevity-promoting properties.
    • 钩吻 - A real toxic plant in traditional Chinese medicine, known for its lethal effects and used as a cautionary example of the dangers of misusing herbal remedies.
    • 左元放 - A figure whose historical or fictional status is unclear, but likely a Daoist practitioner or alchemist.

    Proper Nouns

    • 桓谭 - A philosopher and scholar from the Han dynasty.
    • 新论 - The title of Huan Tan's work.
    • 方士 - Daoist practitioners or alchemists.
    • 董仲君 - A specific Daoist practitioner or alchemist.
    • 黄帝 - The Yellow Emperor, a legendary figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 天老 - A sage figure associated with ancient wisdom and divination.
    • 黄精 - A real herb in traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 钩吻 - A real toxic plant in traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 左元放 - A figure whose historical or fictional status is unclear, but likely a Daoist practitioner or alchemist.

    In New Discourses by Huan Tan, it is recorded that there was a Daoist practitioner named Dong Zhongjun who was imprisoned for a crime, feigned death, and then revived. The smell of decay emanated from his door before he came back to life.

    The Yellow Emperor asked Tianlao, "Is there any substance produced by heaven and earth that, if eaten, can make one immortal?" Tianlao replied, "There is a plant associated with the sun, called huangjing. If one consumes it as a remedy, it can lead to immortality. There is also a plant associated with the moon, called gouwen. It cannot be eaten; if ingested, it causes immediate death. People believe that gouwen kills, but they do not believe that huangjing extends life. Is this not perplexing?" (Chu Ruyong noted: "If a plant can kill, it cannot extend life. If killing has no proof, then the claim of extending life is also untrustworthy.")

    On Diet

    Zuo Yuanfang, during a year of famine, devised a method of selecting soybeans, mixing coarse and fine beans evenly, and cooking them thoroughly. He pressed them so that they emitted a glow and the vapor penetrated the core of the beans. Before eating, one should abstain from food for one day and drink cold water immediately after consuming the beans. Fish, meat, vegetables, and fruits should not be consumed again. When thirsty, one should drink water, but be careful not to drink warm water. Initially, one may feel a bit weak, but after several days, physical strength and health will improve, and one will no longer desire to eat.

    One method involves taking three sheng (a unit of volume) as a dose, which should be adjusted according to the amount of food consumed beforehand. In years of plenty, if one wishes to eat again, one should cook kudzu seeds and sesame oil, consume meat soup, and gradually drink it. Only after the beans have been consumed can one eat other foods. If the beans are not fully consumed and solid food is eaten, it can be fatal. This method has not been tested, but it might work. (Chu Ruyong noted: "Another method involves coating sticky buns with wax, baking them until they are cooked, and then coating them with the desired amount. One should eat them as usual, drink cold water when thirsty, and avoid drinking tea.") [29]

    孔子家语曰食水者乃耐寒而善浮食土者无心而不息食木者多力而不治食石者肥泽而不老食草者善走而愚食桑者有绪而蛾食肉者勇毅而悍食气者神明而寿食谷者智慧而夭不食者不死而神仙传曰杂食者百病妖邪之所钟焉
    　　西域有蒲萄酒积年不败彼俗云可十年饮之醉弥月乃解所食逾少心逾开所食逾多心逾塞年逾损焉
    　　辨方士
    　　汉淮南王谋反被诛亦云得道轻举【周日用曰汉书云淮南自刑应不然乎得道轻举非虚事也至今维境内马迹犹存且日与八公同处皆上品眞人耳既谈道德肯图叛逆之事况恒行隂德好书艺不善弋猎淮南内书言神仙黄白之术去反事逺矣夫古今书传多黜仙道者虑帝王公侯废万几而慕其道故隐而不书唯老耼不可掩而云三百嵗后西游流沙不知所之庾书云蜀有女道士谢自然白日上升此外歴代史籍未尝言也】 [30]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [30]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 食水者乃耐寒而善浮 - Metaphor for someone who is resilient and adaptable.
    • 食土者无心而不息 - Possibly referring to someone who is relentless or driven.
    • 食木者多力而不治 - Possibly referring to someone who is physically strong but lacks refinement.
    • 食石者肥泽而不老 - Possibly referring to someone who is healthy and youthful.
    • 食草者善走而愚 - Possibly referring to someone who is agile but lacks wisdom.
    • 食桑者有绪而蛾 - Possibly referring to someone who follows a pattern and transforms.
    • 食肉者勇毅而悍 - Possibly referring to someone who is courageous and strong.
    • 食气者神明而寿 - Possibly referring to someone who is spiritual and long-lived.
    • 食谷者智慧而夭 - Possibly referring to someone who is intelligent but has a shorter lifespan.
    • 不食者不死而神仙 - Possibly referring to someone who achieves immortality through abstaining from food.
    • 杂食者百病妖邪之所钟焉 - Possibly referring to someone who is prone to illness due to a varied diet.
    • 西域有蒲萄酒积年不败 - Possibly referring to the longevity of grape wine in the region.
    • 彼俗云可十年饮之醉弥月乃解 - Possibly referring to the long-lasting effects of the wine.
    • 所食逾少心逾开 - Possibly referring to the idea that less consumption leads to greater mental clarity.
    • 所食逾多心逾塞 - Possibly referring to the idea that excessive consumption leads to mental blockage.
    • 年逾损焉 - Possibly referring to the idea that aging leads to loss.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 孔子家语 - A collection of sayings and stories about Confucius and his disciples.
    • 淮南王 - Refers to Liu An, the Prince of Huainan during the Han Dynasty, who was executed for rebellion but also claimed to have attained immortality.
    • 汉书 - The *Book of Han*, a historical text that records the history of the Western Han Dynasty.
    • 八公 - The Eight Immortals, a group of legendary figures in Chinese mythology.
    • 淮南内书 - The *Inner Book of Huainan*, a text attributed to Liu An that discusses various philosophical and alchemical topics.
    • 老耼 - Refers to Laozi, the legendary founder of Daoism.
    • 庾书 - The *Yushu*, a historical text that records the history of the Eastern Han Dynasty.
    • 谢自然 - A historical figure who claimed to have achieved immortality.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The long sequence of parallel clauses must be read as a series of metaphors describing different dietary habits and their effects.
    • This sequence describes the properties of grape wine in the Western Regions and the effects of diet on mental clarity and longevity.
    • This section introduces the topic of discerning alchemists and the historical figure Liu An.
    • This clause introduces the historical figure Liu An and his claims of immortality.
    • This clause introduces a debate about the historical accuracy of Liu An's death.
    • This clause supports the idea of immortality.
    • This clause refers to the lasting impact of Liu An's activities.
    • This clause describes Liu An's association with the Eight Immortals.
    • This clause highlights the contradiction between Liu An's teachings and his actions.
    • This clause describes Liu An's virtuous and scholarly pursuits.
    • This clause refers to the content of the *Inner Book of Huainan*.
    • This clause refers to the skepticism towards immortality in historical texts.
    • This clause refers to the legendary journey of Laozi.
    • This clause refers to the historical figure Xie Ziran and her claim of immortality.
    • This clause refers to the rarity of such claims in historical texts.

    The Analects of Confucius states: Those who eat water are cold-resistant and good at floating; those who eat earth have no heart and do not rest; those who eat wood are strong but not refined; those who eat stone are fat and lustrous and do not age; those who eat grass are good at running but foolish; those who eat mulberry have a sequence and become silkworms; those who eat meat are brave and fierce; those who eat air are divine and long-lived; those who eat grain are wise but short-lived; those who do not eat do not die and become immortals. The Biographies of Immortals says: Those who eat a variety of foods are the target of all diseases and evil spirits.

    In the Western Regions, grape wine does not spoil for many years. They say it can be drunk for ten years, and the intoxication lasts for a month before it dissipates. The less one eats, the more open one's heart becomes; the more one eats, the more blocked one's heart becomes; the more years one lives, the more one loses.

    Discerning Alchemists

    The Han Prince of Huainan, who was executed for rebellion, also claimed to have attained the Way and ascended lightly. [Zhou Riyong said, "The History of the Han says that Liu An committed suicide. Should this not be the case?" Attaining the Way and ascending lightly is not a false matter. Even now, the hoofprints of horses remain within the boundaries of Weiyang. And he daily shared the company of the Eight Immortals, all of whom were of the highest rank and true immortals. Having discussed morality, how could he plot rebellion? Especially since he always practiced hidden virtue, loved books and arts, and was not skilled in hunting. The Inner Book of Huainan discusses the techniques of immortality and alchemy, which are far removed from the matter of rebellion. In ancient and modern books, many criticize the Way of Immortals because they fear that emperors and nobles will neglect their duties and instead pursue immortality, so they hide and do not write about it. Only Laozi cannot be concealed, and it is said that after three hundred years, he traveled westward into the sands and his whereabouts are unknown. The Yushu says that in Shu there was a female Taoist, Xie Ziran, who ascended during the day. Beyond this, no other historical records have ever mentioned it.] [30]

    钩弋夫人被杀于云阳而言尸解柩空【周日用曰史云夫人死大风拔树沙揭石亦不云尸解柩空】
    　　文帝典论云议郎李覃学郄俭辟谷服茯苓饮水中寒泄痢殆至殒命军祭酒农董芬学甘始鸱视狼顾呼吸吐纳为之过差气闭不通良久乃苏寺人严峻就左慈学补导之术阉竪眞无事于斯而逐声若此
    　　又云王仲统云甘始左元放东郭延年行容成御妇人法并为丞相所録间行其术亦得其验降就道士刘景受云母九子元方年三百嵗莫知所在武帝恒御此药亦云有验刘德治淮南王狱得枕中鸿寳秘书及子向缄而竒之信黄白之术可成谓神仙之道可致卒亦无验乃以罹罪也【周日用曰神仙之道学之匪一朝一夕而可得黄白者也仍须有分升腾者应须有骨安可偶然而得效也】 [31]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [31]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 尸解柩空 - A term used in Daoist texts to describe a type of immortality where the physical body is left behind, but the soul departs, leaving an empty coffin.
    • 辟谷服茯苓 - Refers to the practice of abstaining from food and consuming the herb *fu ling*, a common Daoist method for achieving longevity.
    • 鸱视狼顾 - A Daoist exercise technique involving specific eye and head movements, meant to enhance vitality and longevity.
    • 补导之术 - Refers to Daoist practices of 'supplementing' and 'guiding', which include breathing exercises, massage, and other methods to maintain health and longevity.
    • 黄白之术 - Refers to alchemy, specifically the pursuit of turning base metals into gold and silver, often associated with Daoist practices.
    • 罹罪 - Literally 'to suffer punishment,' but in this context, it refers to the consequences of engaging in alchemical practices that fail to yield results.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 钩弋夫人 - A concubine of Emperor Wu of Han, known for her ability to hold a closed fist for extended periods. She was eventually executed by Emperor Wu, who believed her power was unnatural.
    • 云阳 - A place name in the Han dynasty, where executions were often carried out.
    • 文帝典论 - A work attributed to Cao Cao, the founder of the Cao Wei dynasty, which discusses various topics, including Daoist practices and their consequences.
    • 李覃 - A historical figure mentioned in the *Wen Di Dian Lun*, who practiced Daoist techniques and suffered ill health as a result.
    • 董芬 - Another historical figure mentioned in the *Wen Di Dian Lun*, who practiced Daoist techniques and suffered adverse effects.
    • 严峻 - A eunuch who practiced Daoist techniques under the guidance of *Zuoshi* (左慈), a Daoist master.
    • 刘景 - A Daoist master who taught alchemical practices, including the use of *yunmu* (云母), a mineral used in alchemy.
    • 刘德 - A historical figure who investigated the crimes of the Prince of Huainan, a proponent of alchemy and Daoist practices.
    • 鸿宝秘书 - A collection of texts on alchemy and Daoist practices, allegedly found during the investigation of the Prince of Huainan.
    • 容成御妇人法 - A Daoist practice attributed to *Rongcheng* (容成), a legendary figure associated with longevity and sexual techniques.
    • 黄白 - Refers to gold and silver, often associated with alchemy and the pursuit of immortality.
    • 升腾 - A term used in Daoist texts to describe the ascent of the soul to the heavens, often associated with achieving immortality.

    Proper Nouns

    • 钩弋夫人 - Lady Hook-Yi, a concubine of Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 云阳 - A place in the Han dynasty where executions were performed.
    • 李覃 - A historical figure mentioned in the *Wen Di Dian Lun*.
    • 董芬 - A historical figure mentioned in the *Wen Di Dian Lun*.
    • 严峻 - A eunuch who practiced Daoist techniques.
    • 左慈 - A Daoist master who taught Daoist techniques to eunuchs.
    • 刘景 - A Daoist master who taught alchemical practices.
    • 刘德 - A historical figure who investigated the crimes of the Prince of Huainan.
    • 容成 - A legendary figure associated with longevity and sexual techniques.
    • 甘始 - A historical figure mentioned in the *Wen Di Dian Lun*.
    • 左元放 - A historical figure mentioned in the *Wen Di Dian Lun*.
    • 东郭延年 - A historical figure mentioned in the *Wen Di Dian Lun*.
    • 淮南王 - The Prince of Huainan, a proponent of alchemy and Daoist practices.
    • 鸿宝秘书 - A collection of texts on alchemy and Daoist practices.

    A Woman with Two Hands

    During the reign of Emperor Wu, Lady Hook-Yi was executed in Yunyang, and it was said that her body underwent a transformation and the coffin was empty.

    According to the Wen Di Dian Lun, the Imperial Censor Li Tan studied the techniques of Qi Jian, abstaining from food and consuming fu ling (poria). He drank water and suffered from chills, diarrhea, and near death. The Military Sacrificial Officer Dong Fen studied the techniques of Gan Shi, practicing chi shi lang gu (eagle-eyed, wolf-like posture) and breathing exercises. He overdid it, causing his breath to become blocked, and he remained unconscious for a long time before regaining consciousness. The eunuch Yan Jun studied the techniques of Zuoshi, learning bu dao (supplementing and guiding) practices. Eunuchs truly had no need for these practices but pursued them nonetheless.

    It is also said that Wang Zhongtong mentioned that Gan Shi, Zuo Yuanfang, and Dongguo Yanyan practiced the Rongcheng method of controlling women. They were all recorded by the Chancellor and practiced this method in secret, achieving some success. They descended to the Daoist Master Liu Jing, who taught them the use of yunmu (mica) and the jiu zi (nine sons) technique. Liu Jing was said to be 300 years old, but his whereabouts were unknown. Emperor Wu of Han consistently used this medicine, and it was said to be effective. Liu De, investigating the crimes of the Prince of Huainan, found the Hongbao Mimi (Secret Books of the Red Treasure) hidden in the pillow. He sealed the books and found them strange. He believed that the alchemical techniques could succeed and that the path to immortality could be achieved, but in the end, they proved ineffective, and he suffered the consequences. [31]

    刘根不觉饥渴或谓能忍盈虚王仲都当盛夏之月十炉火炙之不当严冬之时裸之而不寒恒山君以为性耐寒暑恒山以为无仙道好竒者为之前者已述焉
    　　司马迁云无尧以天下让许由事雄亦云夸大者为之雄又云无仙道桓谭亦同

    　　博物志卷五
    <子部,小说家类,琐记之属,博物志>
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　博物志卷六
    　　晋　张华　撰
    　　人名攷
    　　昔彼高阳是生伯鲧职典水土取帝之息壤以堙洪水殷三仁微子箕子比干
    　　文王四友南宫括散宜生闳夭太颠
    　　仲尼四友顔渊子贡子路子张 [32]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [32]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 不觉饥渴 - Literally 'does not feel hunger or thirst,' describes someone so absorbed that they are unaware of their basic needs.
    • 或谓能忍盈虚 - Some say he can endure fullness and emptiness, where '盈虚' refers to cycles of abundance and scarcity.
    • 王仲都当盛夏之月十炉火炙之不 - Wang Zhongdu, during the hot summer month, was roasted by ten furnaces and did not sweat, where '' (sweat) is omitted for brevity or emphasis.
    • 裸之而不寒 - Naked and not cold, indicates an ability to withstand extreme cold.
    • 恒山君以为性耐寒暑 - The Lord of Mount Heng believes his nature can endure cold and heat, where '性' (nature) refers to innate qualities.
    • 恒山以为无仙道 - Mount Heng believes there is no way to immortality, where '仙道' (immortality) is a key concept in Daoist thought.
    • 好竒者为之 - Those who love the strange do this, where '好竒' (love the strange) refers to those who seek out unusual or miraculous things.
    • 司马迁云无尧以天下让许由事 - Simaqian says there is no story of Yao yielding the world to Xu You, where '让' (yield) refers to the act of abdicating power.
    • 扬雄亦云夸大者为之 - Yang Xiong also says it is done by those who exaggerate, where '夸大' (exaggerate) refers to those who embellish stories.
    • 扬雄又云无仙道 - Yang Xiong also says there is no way to immortality, repeating the previous point.
    • 桓谭亦同 - Huan Tan also agrees, indicating a shared opinion.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 刘根 - A historical figure, likely a Daoist or alchemist, known for his extraordinary abilities.
    • 王仲都 - Another historical figure, possibly a contemporary or associate of Liu Gen, known for his ability to withstand extreme conditions.
    • 恒山君 - The Lord of Mount Heng, a deity or spirit associated with Mount Heng, a sacred mountain in Chinese mythology.
    • 恒山 - Mount Heng, one of the Four Sacred Mountains of Daoism, often associated with immortality and spiritual practices.
    • 司马迁 - Sima Qian, the famous historian who wrote the *Records of the Grand Historian*.
    • 扬雄 - Yang Xiong, a Han dynasty scholar and writer known for his philosophical and literary works.
    • 桓谭 - Huan Tan, a Han dynasty scholar and critic of Daoist practices.
    • 尧 - Yao, one of the Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors, a legendary sage ruler.
    • 许由 - Xu You, a legendary hermit who refused to accept the throne from Yao.

    Proper Nouns

    • 刘根 - Liu Gen, a historical figure.
    • 王仲都 - Wang Zhongdu, a historical figure.
    • 恒山君 - The Lord of Mount Heng, a deity or spirit.
    • 恒山 - Mount Heng, a sacred mountain.
    • 司马迁 - Sima Qian, the historian.
    • 扬雄 - Yang Xiong, the scholar and writer.
    • 桓谭 - Huan Tan, the scholar and critic.
    • 尧 - Yao, the legendary sage ruler.
    • 许由 - Xu You, the legendary hermit.

    Liu Gen does not feel hunger or thirst, and some say he can endure the cycles of abundance and scarcity. Wang Zhongdu, during the hot summer month, was roasted by ten furnaces and did not sweat, and in the severe winter, he was naked and did not feel cold. The Lord of Mount Heng believes his nature can endure cold and heat, while Mount Heng itself believes there is no way to immortality. Those who love the strange do this, as those who came before have already described it.

    Sima Qian says there is no story of Yao yielding the world to Xu You, and Yang Xiong also says it is done by those who exaggerate. Yang Xiong also says there is no way to immortality, and Huan Tan agrees as well.

    Bamboo Annals, Volume Five
    <Subcategory of Miscellaneous Notes in the Subcollection of Novels, Classified as "Bamboo Annals" under the Four Treasuries Catalog>
    Imperially Approved Complete Library of the Four Treasuries
    Bamboo Annals, Volume Six
    Compiled by Zhang Hua of the Jin Dynasty
    Examination of Names

    In ancient times, Gao Yang gave birth to Boyun, who was in charge of water and soil. He took the emperor's fertile earth to stop the flood. The three virtuous men of the Shang dynasty were Wei Zi, Ji Zi, and Bi Gan.

    The four friends of King Wen were Nan Gong Kuo, San Yi Sheng, Hong Yao, and Tai Dian.
    The four friends of Confucius were Yan Hui, Zi Gong, Zi Lu, and Zi Zhang. [32]

    参字敬伯
    　　蔡伯喈母公妹曜卿姑也
    　　古之善射者甘蝇蝇之弟子曰飞衞
    　　平原管辂善卜筮解鸟语
    　　蔡邕有书万卷汉末年载数车与王粲粲亡后相国掾魏讽谋反粲子与焉既被诛邕所与粲书悉入粲族子叶字长绪即正宗父正宗即辅嗣兄也初粲与族兄凯避地荆州依刘表表有女表爱粲才欲以妻之嫌其形陋乃谓之曰君才过人而体陋非女壻才也凯有风貌乃妻凯生叶即女所生
    　　太丘长陈寔寔子鸿胪卿纪纪子司空羣羣子泰四世于汉魏二朝有重名而其德渐少减故时人为之语曰公惭卿卿惭长
    　　文籍攷
    　　圣人制作曰经贤者著述曰传郑注毛诗曰笺不解此意或云毛公尝为北海郡守是此郡人故以为敬
    　　何休注公羊传云何氏学有不能解者或荅云休谦词受学于师乃宣此义不出于巳此言为允
    　　太古书今见存有神农经山海经或云大禹作周易蔡邕云礼记月令周公作【周日用曰礼记疏云第一是吕不韦春秋明吕氏所制蔡邕云周公未之详也】 [33]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [33]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 參字敬伯 - Formal naming convention where '參' is the given name and '敬伯' is the courtesy name.
    • 蔡伯喈母公妹曜卿姑也 - Complex familial relationship description: '蔡伯喈's mother is the sister of 公 and the aunt of 曜卿.'
    • 古之善射者甘蝇蝇之弟子曰飞衞 - Historical anecdote about a skilled archer named 甘蝇 and his disciple 飞衞.
    • 平原管辂善卜筮解鸟语 - Reference to 管辂, a famous diviner from 平原 who could interpret bird language.
    • 蔡邕有书万卷汉末年载数车与王粲粲亡后相国掾魏讽谋反粲子与焉既被诛邕所与粲书悉入粲族子叶字长绪即正宗父正宗即辅嗣兄也 - Detailed historical narrative about 蔡邕, 王粲, and their descendants, involving political intrigue and family lineage.
    • 太丘长陈寔寔子鸿胪卿纪纪子司空羣羣子泰四世于汉魏二朝有重名而其德渐少减故时人为之语曰公惭卿卿惭长 - Description of the 陳氏 family's prominence and decline across four generations in the Han and Wei dynasties.
    • 文籍攷 - Heading indicating a section on textual studies or bibliographical analysis.
    • 圣人制作曰经贤者著述曰传郑注毛诗曰笺不解此意或云毛公尝为北海郡守是此郡人故以为敬 - Discussion of textual annotations, specifically 郑玄's commentary on the 毛诗, and the reasoning behind the term '笺'.
    • 何休注公羊传云何氏学有不能解者或荅云休谦词受学于师乃宣此义不出于巳此言为允 - Reference to 何休's commentary on the 公羊传 and the humility expressed in his work.
    • 太古书今见存有神农经山海经或云大禹作周易蔡邕云礼记月令周公作 - Discussion of ancient texts, including the 神农经, 山海经, and 周易, with specific attributions to authors.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 蔡伯喈 - A famous musician and scholar from the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 甘蝇 - A legendary archer from ancient China.
    • 飞衞 - A disciple of 甘蝇, also known for his archery skills.
    • 管辂 - A renowned diviner from the state of 平原 during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 蔡邕 - A prominent scholar, musician, and calligrapher from the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 王粲 - A poet and essayist from the late Han dynasty, known for his literary works.
    • 魏讽 - A political figure involved in a conspiracy during the late Han dynasty.
    • 陈寔 - A respected official and moral leader from the late Han dynasty.
    • 陈纪 - The son of 陳寔, who served as 鸿胪卿 during the early Wei dynasty.
    • 陈羣 - The son of 陳紀, who served as 司空 during the Wei dynasty.
    • 陈泰 - The son of 陳羣, who served as a general during the Wei dynasty.
    • 郑玄 - A renowned scholar and commentator on the classics during the Han dynasty.
    • 毛公 - A figure associated with the 毛诗, a version of the Book of Poetry.
    • 何休 - A prominent scholar and commentator on the 公羊传 during the Han dynasty.
    • 神农经 - An ancient text attributed to the legendary figure 神农.
    • 山海经 - An ancient Chinese text describing mythical creatures and geographical features.
    • 周易 - The Book of Changes, one of the Five Classics.
    • 礼记 - The Book of Rites, one of the Five Classics.
    • 月令 - A section of the 礼记 detailing the rituals and activities for each month.
    • 周公 - A legendary figure from the early Zhou dynasty, credited with many ritual and political innovations.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 蔡伯喈母公妹曜卿姑也 - This sentence is elliptical and requires careful parsing to understand the familial relationships.
    • 蔡邕有书万卷汉末年载数车与王粲粲亡后相国掾魏讽谋反粲子与焉既被诛邕所与粲书悉入粲族子叶字长绪即正宗父正宗即辅嗣兄也 - This long sentence is complex and requires breaking down into manageable parts to understand the historical events and family dynamics.
    • 太丘长陈寔寔子鸿胪卿纪纪子司空羣羣子泰四世于汉魏二朝有重名而其德渐少减故时人为之语曰公惭卿卿惭长 - This sentence is also complex and requires careful parsing to understand the generational decline of the 陳氏 family.
    • 圣人制作曰经贤者著述曰传郑注毛诗曰笺不解此意或云毛公尝为北海郡守是此郡人故以为敬 - This sentence is a mix of definitions and explanations that require careful translation to maintain the logical flow.
    • 何休注公羊传云何氏学有不能解者或荅云休谦词受学于师乃宣此义不出于巳此言为允 - This sentence is a discussion of textual annotations and requires careful translation to preserve the nuances of the commentary.

    Proper Nouns

    • 蔡伯喈 - A famous musician and scholar from the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 甘蝇 - A legendary archer from ancient China.
    • 飞衞 - A disciple of 甘蝇, also known for his archery skills.
    • 管辂 - A renowned diviner from the state of 平原 during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 蔡邕 - A prominent scholar, musician, and calligrapher from the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 王粲 - A poet and essayist from the late Han dynasty, known for his literary works.
    • 魏讽 - A political figure involved in a conspiracy during the late Han dynasty.
    • 陈寔 - A respected official and moral leader from the late Han dynasty.
    • 陈纪 - The son of 陳寔, who served as 鸿胪卿 during the early Wei dynasty.
    • 陈羣 - The son of 陳紀, who served as 司空 during the Wei dynasty.
    • 陈泰 - The son of 陳羣, who served as a general during the Wei dynasty.
    • 郑玄 - A renowned scholar and commentator on the classics during the Han dynasty.
    • 毛公 - A figure associated with the 毛诗, a version of the Book of Poetry.
    • 何休 - A prominent scholar and commentator on the 公羊传 during the Han dynasty.
    • 神农经 - An ancient text attributed to the legendary figure 神农.
    • 山海经 - An ancient Chinese text describing mythical creatures and geographical features.
    • 周易 - The Book of Changes, one of the Five Classics.
    • 礼记 - The Book of Rites, one of the Five Classics.
    • 月令 - A section of the 礼记 detailing the rituals and activities for each month.
    • 周公 - A legendary figure from the early Zhou dynasty, credited with many ritual and political innovations.

    Cao Cao, courtesy name Jingbo.

    The mother of Cai Bojie was the sister of Gong Gong and the aunt of Yaoqing.

    In ancient times, there was a skilled archer named Gan Ying, and his disciple was named Fei Wei.

    Guan Lu from the state of Pingyuan was skilled in divination and could interpret the language of birds.

    Cai Yong had tens of thousands of books. At the end of the Han dynasty, he loaded several carts with books and gave them to Wang Can. After Wang Can's death, his son was involved in a conspiracy led by the imperial censor Wei Feng, who was executed. All the books that Cai Yong had given to Wang Can were inherited by Wang Can's nephew, Ye, whose courtesy name was Changxu. Ye was the father of Zhengzhong, who was the elder brother of Fusi.

    Initially, Wang Can and his cousin Kai fled to Jingzhou to take refuge with Liu Biao. Liu Biao had a daughter and admired Wang Can's talent, wanting to marry him off to her. However, he found Wang Can's appearance unattractive and told him, "Your talent surpasses others, but your body is unattractive and not suitable for a son-in-law." Kai had a handsome appearance, so he married him, and they had a son named Ye, who was the daughter's son.

    Taoqiu, Chen Shi, was the father of Chen Ji, who served as Honglu Qing. Chen Ji's son, Chen Qun, served as Sikong, and Chen Qun's son, Chen Tai, had a prominent reputation in both the Han and Wei dynasties, but their virtue gradually declined. Therefore, people of the time said, "Officials feel ashamed of their subordinates, and subordinates feel ashamed of their superiors."

    Examination of Textual Records

    The works created by sages are called classics, and the writings of scholars are called transmissions. Zheng Xuan's commentary on the Mao version of the Book of Poetry is called "Jian," but the meaning of this term is not fully understood. Some say that Mao Gong once served as the governor of Beihai Commandery, and Zheng Xuan was from that same commandery, hence he used "Jian" as a sign of respect.

    He Xiuzhi's commentary on the Gongyang Transmission states, "There are aspects of the He school of thought that I cannot understand." Someone replied, "This is a humble statement indicating that he received his learning from his teacher and then explained these meanings, which did not originate from himself." This statement is correct.

    Ancient books that still exist today include the Shen Nong Classic and the Shan Hai Jing. It is said that the latter was written by Da Yu. Cai Yong stated that the Li Ji's Yue Ling (Monthly Orders) was written by Duke of Zhou. [The commentary on the Li Ji states that the first is the Chun Qiu of Lord Lu, clearly indicating that it was written by Lord Lu. Cai Yong did not provide detailed information about this.] [33]

    諡法司马法周公所作
    　　余友下邳陈德龙谓余言曰灵光殿赋传为宜城王子山所作子山尝之泰山从鲍子眞学算过鲁国见都殿赋之还归本州溺死湘水时年二十余也
    　　地理攷
    　　周自后稷至于文武皆都闗中号为宗周秦为阿房殿在长安西南二十里殿东西千步南北三百步上可以坐万人庭中受十万人二世为赵高所杀于宜春宫在杜城南三里葬于旁
    　　周时德泽盛蒿大以为宫柱名曰蒿宫
    　　姜嫄祠在雍城长安西南三十里
    　　盗蹠冢在太阳县西
    　　赵鞅冢在临水县西
    　　始皇陵在骊山之北髙数十丈周回六七里今在隂盘县界北陵虽髙大不足以销六十万人积年之功其功力或不见如骊山水泉本北流障使东西流又此土无石于渭北诸山运取大石故歌曰运石甘泉口渭水为不流千人唱万人钩今陵余石大如覆土屋其余功力皆如此【卢氏曰秦氏奢侈自知葬用珍寳多故髙作陵园山者使难发也髙则难上固则难攻项羽争衡之时发其陵未详其至棺否】 [34]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [34]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 諡法 - The system or rules for posthumous titles in ancient China.
    • 司马法周公所作 - A reference to the *Sima Fa* (Military Methods) attributed to Duke of Zhou (Zhou Gong).
    • 余友下邳陈德龙谓余言曰 - A formal introduction to a friend's statement, indicating a personal communication.
    • 灵光殿赋传为宜城王子山所作 - A reference to a famous literary work, the *Lingguang Palace Rhapsody*, attributed to Wang Zishan of Yicheng.
    • 子山尝之泰山从鲍子眞学算过鲁国见都殿赋之还归本州溺死湘水时年二十余也 - A narrative about Wang Zishan's life, including his travels and tragic death.
    • 地理攷 - A section on geographical examination or study.
    • 周自后稷至于文武皆都闗中号为宗周 - A historical statement about the Zhou dynasty's capital in Guanzhong.
    • 秦为阿房殿在长安西南二十里 - A description of the A-Fang Palace in the Qin dynasty.
    • 上可以坐万人庭中受十万人 - A description of the palace's capacity.
    • 二世为赵高所杀于宜春宫在杜城南三里葬于旁 - A historical account of Qin Er Shi's death and burial.
    • 周时德泽盛蒿大以为宫柱名曰蒿宫 - A description of a palace built during the Zhou dynasty.
    • 姜嫄祠在雍城长安西南三十里 - A reference to a shrine dedicated to Jiang Yuan in Yongcheng.
    • 盗蹠冢在太阳县西 - A reference to the tomb of Diao Zhi in Taiyang County.
    • 赵鞅冢在临水县西 - A reference to the tomb of Zhao Yang in Linshui County.
    • 始皇陵在骊山之北髙数十丈周回六七里 - A description of the First Emperor's mausoleum in Lishan.
    • 今在隂盘县界北陵虽髙大不足以销六十万人积年之功 - A statement about the mausoleum's construction and its scale.
    • 其功力或不见如骊山水泉本北流障使东西流 - A description of the engineering efforts and their effects.
    • 又此土无石于渭北诸山运取大石故歌曰运石甘泉口渭水为不流千人唱万人钩 - A description of the labor and songs associated with the construction.
    • 秦氏奢侈自知葬用珍寳多故髙作陵园山者使难发也髙则难上固则难攻 - A commentary on the First Emperor's lavish burial practices.
    • 项羽争衡之时发其陵未详其至棺否 - A historical note about the excavation of the mausoleum during the Chu-Han Contention.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 司马法 - A military treatise attributed to Duke of Zhou, a key figure in the Zhou dynasty.
    • 灵光殿赋 - A famous literary work attributed to Wang Zishan, a scholar and writer.
    • 宜城王子山 - Wang Zishan, a historical figure known for his literary contributions.
    • 泰山 - Mount Tai, a significant mountain in Chinese culture and a site of pilgrimage.
    • 鲍子眞 - A historical figure, possibly a teacher or mentor to Wang Zishan.
    • 鲁国 - A state during the Zhou dynasty, known for its cultural and political significance.
    • 宗周 - The capital of the Zhou dynasty in Guanzhong.
    • 阿房殿 - The A-Fang Palace, a grand palace built during the Qin dynasty.
    • 长安 - The capital city of the Qin and Han dynasties.
    • 宜春宫 - A palace where Qin Er Shi was assassinated.
    • 杜城 - A location near the site of Qin Er Shi's assassination.
    • 姜嫄 - A legendary figure in Chinese mythology, often associated with the founding of the Zhou dynasty.
    • 雍城 - A city in the Zhou dynasty, known for its cultural and religious sites.
    • 盗蹠 - A historical figure, possibly a bandit or rebel.
    • 赵鞅 - A historical figure, possibly a noble or official.
    • 始皇陵 - The mausoleum of the First Emperor of Qin, a significant archaeological site.
    • 骊山 - A mountain near the First Emperor's mausoleum, known for its historical and cultural significance.
    • 项羽 - A prominent military leader during the Chu-Han Contention, known for his role in the fall of the Qin dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 司马法 - The *Sima Fa* (Military Methods).
    • 周公 - Duke of Zhou, a key figure in the Zhou dynasty.
    • 下邳陈德龙 - Chen Delong, a friend of the author.
    • 灵光殿赋 - The *Lingguang Palace Rhapsody*.
    • 宜城王子山 - Wang Zishan, a historical figure.
    • 泰山 - Mount Tai.
    • 鲍子眞 - A historical figure.
    • 鲁国 - A state during the Zhou dynasty.
    • 宗周 - The capital of the Zhou dynasty in Guanzhong.
    • 阿房殿 - The A-Fang Palace.
    • 长安 - The capital city of the Qin and Han dynasties.
    • 宜春宫 - A palace where Qin Er Shi was assassinated.
    • 杜城 - A location near the site of Qin Er Shi's assassination.
    • 姜嫄 - A legendary figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 雍城 - A city in the Zhou dynasty.
    • 盗蹠 - A historical figure.
    • 赵鞅 - A historical figure.
    • 始皇陵 - The mausoleum of the First Emperor of Qin.
    • 骊山 - A mountain near the First Emperor's mausoleum.
    • 项羽 - A prominent military leader.

    Translation:

    The System of Posthumous Titles and the Sima Fa by Duke of Zhou

    My friend Chen Delong of Xiazhi told me that the Lingguang Palace Rhapsody is traditionally attributed to Wang Zishan of Yicheng. Wang Zishan once traveled to Mount Tai to study mathematics with Bo Zhen, passed through the state of Lu, and upon seeing the Lingguang Palace Rhapsody, returned to his home state, where he drowned in the Xiang River at the age of over twenty.

    Geographical Examination

    From Houji to King Wen and King Wu, the Zhou dynasty established its capital in Guanzhong, known as the "Ancestral Capital." During the Qin dynasty, the A-Fang Palace was built twenty li southwest of Chang'an, with the palace hall stretching a thousand steps east to west and three hundred steps north to south. It could accommodate ten thousand people in the hall and ten thousand more in the courtyard. Qin Er Shi was assassinated by Zhao Gao in the Yichun Palace, located three li south of Ducha, and was buried nearby.

    During the Zhou dynasty, the abundant virtue of the time led to the construction of a palace with towering hawthorn trees used as pillars, named the Hao Palace.

    The Jiang Yuan Shrine is located in Yongcheng, thirty li southwest of Chang'an.

    The tomb of Diao Zhi is in Taiyang County.

    The tomb of Zhao Yang is in Linshui County.

    The First Emperor's mausoleum is located north of Lishan, standing dozens of zhang high, with a circumference of six to seven li. It is now within the boundaries of Yinpan County. Although the mausoleum is grand, it did not consume the labor of sixty thousand men over the years. The engineering efforts, however, are not entirely visible, such as the diversion of the Lishan water, which originally flowed north but was redirected to flow east and west. Additionally, the local area lacked stone, so stones were transported from the mountains north of Wei River, leading to the folk song: "Transporting stones at Ganquan, the Wei River does not flow, a thousand sing and ten thousand pull." The remaining stones from the mausoleum are as large as overgrown earth houses, and the other engineering efforts were similarly immense. [Lu Shi said: The Qin dynasty was extravagant, knowing that their burial would use many precious treasures, so they built a high and mountainous mausoleum to make it difficult to excavate. It is high and difficult to ascend, and solid and difficult to attack. During the Chu-Han Contention, when Xiang Yu excavated the mausoleum, it is unclear whether they reached the coffin.] [34]

    旧洛阳字作水边各火行也忌水故去水而加佳又魏于行次为土水得土而流土得水而柔故复去佳加水变雒为洛焉
    　　洞庭君山帝之二女居之曰湘夫人又荆州图经曰湘君所游故曰君山
    　　南荆赋江陵有台甚大而有一柱众木皆拱之
    　　典礼攷
    　　三让一曰礼辞二曰固辞三曰终辞
    　　汉承秦羣臣上书皆曰昧死言
    　　王莽盗位慕古法昧死曰稽首光武因而不改
    　　肉刑明王之制荀卿每论之至汉文帝感太仓公女之言而废之班固著论宜复迄汉末魏初陈纪又论宜申古制孔融亦云可复欲申之钟繇王朗不同遂寝夏侯李胜曹羲丁谧建私议各有彼此多云时未可复故遂寝焉
    　　上公备物九锡一大辂各一牡二驷二衮冕之服赤舄副之三轩悬之乐六佾之舞四朱户以居五纳陛以登六虎贲之士三百人七鈇钺各一八彤弓一彤矢百玈弓十玈矢千九秬鬯一卣珪瓒副之
    　　乐攷
    　　汉末丧乱无金石之乐魏武帝至汉中得杜防旧法始复设轩悬钟磬至于今用之依防也
    　　服饰攷 [35]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [35]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 舊洛阳字作水边各火行也 - Description of the character for 'Luoyang' (洛阳) as composed of water (水) and fire (火) components.
    • 忌水故去水而加佳 - Indicates that the character was changed because it was considered unlucky due to the water component, so the water was removed and replaced with '佳'.
    • 魏于行次为土 - Refers to the fact that Wei (魏) is associated with the earth element in the Five Elements theory.
    • 水得土而流土得水而柔 - Describes the relationship between water and earth, where water flows over earth, and earth becomes soft when mixed with water.
    • 复去佳加水变雒为洛焉 - Indicates that the character was further modified by removing '佳' and adding water (水), resulting in the character '洛'.
    • 三让一曰礼辞二曰固辞三曰终辞 - Describes the three stages of refusal in a formal ceremony: the first refusal is polite, the second is firm, and the third is final.
    • 汉承秦群臣上书皆曰昧死言 - Indicates that during the Han dynasty, officials would use the phrase '昧死言' (to speak at the risk of death) when submitting memorials, following the practice from the Qin dynasty.
    • 王莽盗位慕古法昧死曰稽首光武因而不改 - Describes Wang Mang's attempt to seize power by imitating ancient rituals, using the phrase '稽首' (prostration) instead of '昧死言', which was continued by Emperor Guangwu.
    • 上公备物九锡一大辂各一牡二驷二衮冕之服赤舄副之三轩悬之乐六佾之舞四朱户以居五纳陛以登六虎贲之士三百人七鈇钺各一八彤弓一彤矢百玈弓十玈矢千九秬鬯一卣珪瓒副之 - Describes the nine items (九锡) granted to high-ranking officials, including a chariot, horses, ceremonial robes, music, dance, and other symbols of authority.
    • 汉末丧乱无金石之乐魏武帝至汉中得杜防旧法始复设轩悬钟磬至于今用之依防也 - Describes how, after the chaos at the end of the Han dynasty, there was no music played on metal or stone instruments. When Cao Cao reached Hanzhong, he found the old methods of Du Fang and restored the use of music, which has been followed ever since.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 洛阳 - The ancient capital city of China, known for its historical significance.
    • 洞庭湖 - One of the largest freshwater lakes in China, located in Hunan Province.
    • 君山 - A mountain in Dongting Lake, famous for its association with the legend of the two daughters of Emperor Yao.
    • 荆州 - An ancient Chinese province, now part of Hubei and Hunan Provinces.
    • 江陵 - An ancient city in Hubei Province, known for its historical and cultural significance.
    • 汉朝 - The Han dynasty, a major period in Chinese history.
    • 王莽 - A usurper who briefly ruled China as emperor, known for his attempts to restore ancient rituals.
    • 光武帝 - Emperor Guangwu, the founder of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 荀卿 - Xunzi, a prominent philosopher of the Warring States period.
    • 汉文帝 - Emperor Wen of Han, a ruler of the Han dynasty known for his reforms.
    • 太仓公女 - The daughter of the Grand Master of the Imperial Granary, whose advice led to the abolition of corporal punishment.
    • 班固 - Ban Gu, a historian and writer of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 陈纪 - Chen Ji, a scholar and official of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 孔融 - Kong Rong, a scholar and official of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 钟繇 - Zhong Yao, a calligrapher and politician of the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 王朗 - Wang Lang, a politician and statesman of the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 夏侯 - Xiahou Yuan, a general of the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 李胜 - Li Sheng, a general of the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 曹羲 - Cao Xi, a member of the Cao family during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 丁谧 - Ding Mi, a politician and statesman of the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 魏武帝 - Cao Cao, a powerful warlord and the founder of the state of Wei during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 杜防 - Du Fang, a musician and official of the Han dynasty.
    • 轩悬钟磬 - Instruments used in ancient Chinese music, including bells and chimes.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The sentence is a bit elliptical, but it can be read as describing the transformation of the character for 'Luoyang' by removing the water component and adding '佳'.
    • The sentence uses parallel structure to describe the relationship between water and earth.
    • The sentence is a bit elliptical, but it can be read as describing the further modification of the character by removing '佳' and adding water.
    • The sentence is a bit elliptical, but it can be read as describing the legend of the two daughters of Emperor Yao who lived on Junshan and were known as 'Xiang Fu Ren'.
    • The sentence is a bit elliptical, but it can be read as describing the origin of the name 'Junshan' based on the *Jingzhou Tu Jing*.
    • The sentence is a bit elliptical, but it can be read as describing a large platform in Jiangling with a central pillar and surrounding trees.
    • The section is a bit elliptical, but it can be read as a discussion of rituals and ceremonies.
    • The sentence is a bit elliptical, but it can be read as describing the three stages of refusal in a formal ceremony.
    • The sentence is a bit elliptical, but it can be read as describing the practice of officials using the phrase '昧死言' when submitting memorials.
    • The sentence is a bit elliptical, but it can be read as describing Wang Mang's attempt to seize power and the continuation of the phrase '稽首' by Emperor Guangwu.
    • The sentence is a bit elliptical, but it can be read as describing the debates surrounding the abolition and potential restoration of corporal punishment.

    The Old Character for Luoyang

    The character for "Luoyang" (洛阳) was originally composed of water (水) and fire (火) components. Because the water component was considered unlucky, it was removed and replaced with "佳" (a character often associated with beauty or goodness). Later, since Wei (魏) is associated with the earth element, and water flows over earth, and earth becomes soft when mixed with water, the "佳" was again removed and water (水) was added, changing the character to "洛".

    The Junshan Mountain in Dongting Lake

    The Junshan Mountain in Dongting Lake was the residence of the two daughters of Emperor Yao, who were known as "Xiang Fu Ren" (湘夫人). According to the Jingzhou Tu Jing (荆州图经), the mountain is called Junshan because it was visited by Xiang Jun (湘君).

    A Large Platform in Jiangling

    In the Nan Jing Fu (南荆赋), it is described that in Jiangling (江陵), there is a large platform with a central pillar around which many trees cluster.

    Examination of Rituals

    The Three Stages of Refusal

    In a formal ceremony, there are three stages of refusal: the first refusal is polite, the second is firm, and the third is final.

    The Practice of Officials Submitting Memorials

    During the Han dynasty, officials would use the phrase "昧死言" (to speak at the risk of death) when submitting memorials, following the practice from the Qin dynasty. When Wang Mang seized power, he imitated ancient rituals, using the phrase "稽首" (prostration) instead of "昧死言," which was continued by Emperor Guangwu.

    Debates on Corporal Punishment

    Corporal punishment was a system established by wise rulers, and Xunzi often discussed it. Emperor Wen of Han abolished it after being moved by the advice of the daughter of the Grand Master of the Imperial Granary. Ban Gu argued that it should be restored, and Chen Ji, Kong Rong, Zhong Yao, and Wang Lang all debated the issue. Xiahou Yuan, Li Sheng, Cao Xi, and Ding Mi also had private discussions, with varying opinions. Most believed that the time was not right for restoration, and the matter was ultimately set aside.

    The Nine Items Granted to High-Ranking Officials

    High-ranking officials are granted nine items (九锡): a chariot (一大辂), two horses (各一玄牡二驷), two ceremonial robes (二衮冕之服), two red shoes (赤舄副之), three musical instruments (轩悬之乐), six dancers (六佾之舞), four red doors (朱户以居), five steps (纳陛以登), six hundred guards (虎贲之士三百人), seven axes and swords (鈇钺各一), eight red bows (彤弓一), eight hundred red arrows (彤矢百), ten black bows (玈弓十), and one thousand black arrows (玈矢千), nine jars of wine (秬鬯一卣), and a jade cup (珪瓒副之).

    Examination of Music

    At the end of the Han dynasty, there was no music played on metal or stone instruments. When Cao Cao reached Hanzhong, he found the old methods of Du Fang and restored the use of music, which has been followed ever since.

    Examination of Clothing and Attire [35]

    汉末丧乱絶无玉佩始复作之今之玉佩受于王粲古者男子皆丝衣有故乃素服又有冠无帻故虽凶事皆着冠也
    　　汉中兴士人皆冠葛巾建安中魏武帝造白帢于是遂废唯二学书生犹着也
    　　器名攷
    　　寳劒名纯钩湛卢豪曹鱼肠巨阙五劒皆欧冶子所作龙泉太阿工市三劒皆楚王所问者
    　　风胡子因呉请干将欧冶子作干将阳龙文莫邪隂澷理此二劒呉王使干将作莫邪干将妻作也【夫妻甚喜作劒也】
    　　赤刀周之寳器也
    　　物名攷
    　　古骏马有飞兔腰
    　　周穆王八骏　赤骥　飞黄　白蚁　华骝　騄耳騧騟　渠黄　盗骊
    　　唐公有骕骦
    　　项羽有骓【周日用曰曹公有流影而吕布有赤兔皆后来之良骏也】
    　　周穆王有犬名毛白
    　　晋灵公有畜犬名獒
    　　韩国有黒犬名卢
    　　宋有骏犬曰防
    　　犬四尺为獒
    　　张骞使西域还乃得胡桃种
    　　徐州人谓尘土为蓬块呉人谓跋跌

    　　博物志卷六
    　　钦定四库全书 [36]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [36]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 漢末喪亂 - Compound referring to the chaos and disorder at the end of the Han dynasty.
    • 絶無玉佩 - Means 'there were no jade pendants,' indicating a period where such items were not in use.
    • 始復作之 - Means 'began to make them again,' suggesting a revival of the practice.
    • 今之玉佩受于王粲 - Indicates that the current style of jade pendants was influenced or initiated by Wang Can.
    • 古者男子皆絲衣 - Refers to the ancient custom of men wearing silk clothing.
    • 有故乃素服 - Means 'only in case of mourning would they wear plain clothes,' indicating a change in attire for specific occasions.
    • 又有冠無帻 - Suggests that there was a time when men wore hats but not headbands.
    • 故雖凶事皆着冠也 - Means 'therefore, even in times of mourning, they wore hats,' indicating a cultural norm.
    • 漢中興士人皆冠葛巾 - Describes the custom of scholars during the revival of the Han dynasty wearing gauze headbands.
    • 建安中魏武帝造白帢 - Refers to the creation of white headbands by Cao Cao during the Jian'an period.
    • 于是遂废 - Means 'and thus it was abolished,' indicating the end of a practice.
    • 唯二学书生犹着也 - Means 'only scholars continued to wear them,' showing a continuation of a custom among a specific group.
    • 寳劒名纯钩湛卢豪曹鱼肠巨阙 - Lists the names of famous swords, each with its own significance.
    • 五劒皆欧冶子所作 - Indicates that all five swords were made by Ou Yezi, a renowned swordsmith.
    • 龙泉太阿工市三劒皆楚王所问者 - Refers to three swords named Longquan, Tai'e, and Gongshi, which were requested by the King of Chu.
    • 风胡子因呉请干将欧冶子作干将阳龙文莫邪隂澷理此二劒 - Describes how Feng Hu, on behalf of Wu, requested Gan Jiang and Ou Yezi to make the swords Gan Jiang Yanglong and Mo Ye Yinshui.
    • 呉王使干将作莫邪干将妻作也 - Explains that the King of Wu commissioned Gan Jiang to make the sword Mo Ye, and his wife assisted him.
    • 赤刀周之寳器也 - Refers to the Red Knife, a precious artifact of the Zhou dynasty.
    • 物名攷 - Refers to an examination or discussion of the names of objects.
    • 古骏马有飞兔腰 - Lists the names of ancient swift horses.
    • 周穆王八骏 - Refers to the eight famous horses of King Mu of Zhou.
    • 唐公有骕骦 - Refers to the horse Su Suang owned by Duke Tang.
    • 项羽有骓 - Refers to the horse Zui owned by Xiang Yu.
    • 周日用曰曹公有流影而吕布有赤兔皆后来之良骏也 - A comment by Zhou Riyong stating that Cao Cao had the horse Liu Ying and Lü Bu had the red horse Chi Tu, both of which were excellent horses.
    • 周穆王有犬名毛白 - Refers to a white dog named Xian owned by King Mu of Zhou.
    • 晋灵公有畜犬名獒 - Refers to a dog named Ao owned by Duke Ling of Jin.
    • 韩国有黒犬名卢 - Refers to a black dog named Lu owned by the state of Han.
    • 宋有骏犬曰防 - Refers to a swift dog named Fang owned by the state of Song.
    • 犬四尺为獒 - Defines a dog measuring four chi as an Ao.
    • 张骞使西域还乃得胡桃种 - Describes how Zhang Qian, upon returning from the Western Regions, brought back the seeds of walnuts.
    • 徐州人谓尘土为蓬块呉人谓跋跌 - Describes regional terms for dust and mud.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 王粲 - A prominent scholar and poet of the late Eastern Han period.
    • 歐冶子 - A legendary Chinese swordsmith known for his craftsmanship.
    • 周穆王 - The fifth king of the Zhou dynasty, famous for his travels.
    • 八骏 - The eight famous horses of King Mu of Zhou.
    • 唐公 - Duke Tang, a historical figure.
    • 项羽 - A prominent warlord and rival of Liu Bang during the Chu-Han Contention.
    • 周日用 - A commentator or scholar who made a note about the horses of Cao Cao and Lü Bu.
    • 张骞 - A famous Chinese diplomat and explorer who traveled to the Western Regions.
    • 徐州 - A region in eastern China.
    • 呉 - The state of Wu, one of the Three Kingdoms.
    • 楚王 - The king of Chu, a state during the Warring States period.
    • 晋灵公 - Duke Ling of Jin, a ruler of the state of Jin.
    • 韩国 - The state of Han, one of the Warring States.
    • 宋 - The state of Song, one of the Warring States.

    Proper Nouns

    • 王粲 - A prominent scholar and poet of the late Eastern Han period.
    • 歐冶子 - A legendary Chinese swordsmith known for his craftsmanship.
    • 周穆王 - The fifth king of the Zhou dynasty, famous for his travels.
    • 唐公 - Duke Tang, a historical figure.
    • 项羽 - A prominent warlord and rival of Liu Bang during the Chu-Han Contention.
    • 周日用 - A commentator or scholar who made a note about the horses of Cao Cao and Lü Bu.
    • 张骞 - A famous Chinese diplomat and explorer who traveled to the Western Regions.
    • 徐州 - A region in eastern China.
    • 呉 - The state of Wu, one of the Three Kingdoms.
    • 楚王 - The king of Chu, a state during the Warring States period.
    • 晋灵公 - Duke Ling of Jin, a ruler of the state of Jin.
    • 韩国 - The state of Han, one of the Warring States.
    • 宋 - The state of Song, one of the Warring States.

    At the end of the Han dynasty, during a period of chaos and disorder, jade pendants were no longer in use. They were eventually revived, and the current style of jade pendants is attributed to Wang Can. In ancient times, men generally wore silk clothing, and they would only wear plain clothes in cases of mourning. They also wore hats but not headbands, so even in times of mourning, they wore hats. During the revival of the Han dynasty, scholars wore gauze headbands. In the Jian'an period, Cao Cao created white headbands, and thus the practice of wearing headbands was abolished, with only scholars continuing to wear them.

    The examination of the names of objects includes the following:

    The names of famous swords are: Chunhou, Zhanlu, Hao Cao, Yu Chang, and Ju Que. All five swords were made by Ou Yezi. The swords Longquan, Tai'e, and Gongshi were requested by the King of Chu. Feng Hu, on behalf of Wu, requested Gan Jiang and Ou Yezi to make the swords Gan Jiang Yanglong and Mo Ye Yinshui. The King of Wu commissioned Gan Jiang to make the sword Mo Ye, and his wife assisted him.

    The Red Knife was a precious artifact of the Zhou dynasty.

    The examination of the names of objects includes the following:

    Ancient swift horses included Fei Tu and Yao Zuo. The eight famous horses of King Mu of Zhou were: Chi Ji, Fei Huang, Bai Yi, Hua Liao, Lu Er, Gua Yu, Qu Huang, and Dao Li. Duke Tang had a horse named Su Suang. Xiang Yu had a horse named Zui. Zhou Riyong noted that Cao Cao had the horse Liu Ying and Lü Bu had the red horse Chi Tu, both of which were excellent horses. King Mu of Zhou had a white dog named Xian. Duke Ling of Jin had a dog named Ao. The state of Han had a black dog named Lu. The state of Song had a swift dog named Fang. A dog measuring four chi was defined as an Ao. Zhang Qian, upon returning from the Western Regions, brought back the seeds of walnuts. In the region of Xu, people referred to dust as Peng Kui, and in Wu, people referred to mud as Ba Die.

    Translation:

    At the end of the Han dynasty, during a period of chaos and disorder, jade pendants were no longer in use. They were eventually revived, and the current style of jade pendants is attributed to Wang Can. In ancient times, men generally wore silk clothing, and they would only wear plain clothes in cases of mourning. They also wore hats but not headbands, so even in times of mourning, they wore hats. During the revival of the Han dynasty, scholars wore gauze headbands. In the Jian'an period, Cao Cao created white headbands, and thus the practice of wearing headbands was abolished, with only scholars continuing to wear them.

    The examination of the names of objects includes the following:

    The names of famous swords are: Chunhou, Zhanlu, Hao Cao, Yu Chang, and Ju Que. All five swords were made by Ou Yezi. The swords Longquan, Tai'e, and Gongshi were requested by the King of Chu. Feng Hu, on behalf of Wu, requested Gan Jiang and Ou Yezi to make the swords Gan Jiang Yanglong and Mo Ye Yinshui. The King of Wu commissioned Gan Jiang to make the sword Mo Ye, and his wife assisted him.

    The Red Knife was a precious artifact of the Zhou dynasty.

    The examination of the names of objects includes the following:

    Ancient swift horses included Fei Tu and Yao Zuo. The eight famous horses of King Mu of Zhou were: Chi Ji, Fei Huang, Bai Yi, Hua Liao, Lu Er, Gua Yu, Qu Huang, and Dao Li. Duke Tang had a horse named Su Suang. Xiang Yu had a horse named Zui. Zhou Riyong noted that Cao Cao had the horse Liu Ying and Lü Bu had the red horse Chi Tu, both of which were excellent horses. King Mu of Zhou had a white dog named Xian. Duke Ling of Jin had a dog named Ao. The state of Han had a black dog named Lu. The state of Song had a swift dog named Fang. A dog measuring four chi was defined as an Ao. Zhang Qian, upon returning from the Western Regions, brought back the seeds of walnuts. In the region of Xu, people referred to dust as Peng Kui, and in Wu, people referred to mud as Ba Die.

    Translation:

    At the end of the Han dynasty, during a period of chaos and disorder, jade pendants were no longer in use. They were eventually revived, and the current style of jade pendants is attributed to Wang Can. In ancient times, men generally wore silk clothing, and they would only wear plain clothes in cases of mourning. They also wore hats but not headbands, so even in times of mourning, they wore hats. During the revival of the Han dynasty, scholars wore gauze headbands. In the Jian'an period, Cao Cao created white headbands, and thus the practice of wearing headbands was abolished, with only scholars continuing to wear them.

    The examination of the names of objects includes the following:

The King of Wu commissioned Gan Jiang to make the sword Mo Ye, and his wife assisted him.

    The Red Knife was a precious artifact of the Zhou dynasty.

    The examination of the names of objects includes the following:

Duke Tang had a horse named Su Suang. Xiang Yu had a horse named Zui. Zhou Riyong noted that Cao Cao had the horse Liu Ying and Lü Bu had the red horse Chi Tu, both of which were excellent horses. King Mu of Zhou had a white dog named Xian. Duke Ling of Jin had a dog named Ao. The state of Han had a black dog named Lu. The state of Song had a swift dog named Fang. A dog measuring four chi was defined as an Ao. Zhang Qian, upon returning from the Western Regions, brought back the seeds of walnuts. In the region of Xu, people referred to dust as Peng Kui, and in Wu, people referred to mud as Ba Die.

    At the end of the Han dynasty, during a period of chaos and disorder, jade pendants were no longer in use. They were eventually revived, and the current style of jade pendants is attributed to Wang Can. In ancient times, men generally wore silk clothing, and they would only wear plain clothes in cases of mourning. They also wore hats but not headbands, so even in times of mourning, they wore hats. During the revival of the Han dynasty, scholars wore gauze headbands. In the Jian'an period, Cao Cao created white headbands, and thus the practice of wearing headbands was abolished, with only scholars continuing to wear them.

    The examination of the names of objects includes the following:

    The names of famous swords are: Chunhou, Zhanlu, Hao Cao, Yu Chang, and Ju Que. All five swords were made by Ou Yezi. The swords Longquan, Tai'e, and Gongshi were requested by the King of Chu. Feng Hu, on behalf of Wu, requested Gan Jiang and Ou Yezi to make the swords Gan Jiang Yanglong and Mo Ye Yinshui. The King of Wu commissioned Gan Jiang to make the sword Mo Ye, and his wife assisted him.

    The Red Knife was a precious artifact of the Zhou dynasty.

    The examination of the names of objects includes the following:

    Ancient swift horses included Fei Tu and Yao Zuo. The eight famous horses of King Mu of Zhou were: Chi Ji, Fei Huang, Bai Yi, Hua Liao, Lu Er, Gua Yu, Qu Huang, and Dao Li. Duke Tang had a horse named Su Suang. Xiang Yu had a horse named Zui. Zhou Riyong noted that Cao Cao had the horse Liu Ying and Lü Bu had the red horse Chi Tu, both of which were excellent horses. King Mu of Zhou had a white dog named Xian. Duke Ling of Jin had a dog named Ao. The state of Han had a black dog named Lu. The state of Song had a swift dog named Fang. A dog measuring four chi was defined as an Ao. Zhang Qian, upon returning from the Western Regions, brought back the seeds of walnuts. In the region of Xu, people referred to dust as Peng Kui, and in Wu, people referred to mud as Ba Die.

    Translation:

    At the end of the Han dynasty, during a period of chaos and disorder, jade pendants were no longer in use. They were eventually revived, and the current style of jade pendants is attributed to Wang Can. In ancient times, men generally wore silk clothing, and they would only wear plain clothes in cases of mourning. They also wore hats but not headbands, so even in times of mourning, they wore hats. During the revival of the Han dynasty, scholars wore gauze headbands. In the Jian'an period, [36]

    博物志卷七
    　　晋　张华　撰
    　　异闻
    　　昔夏禹观河见长人鱼身出曰吾河精岂河伯耶冯夷华隂潼乡人也得仙道化为河伯岂道同哉仙夷乘龙虎水神乘鱼龙其行恍惚万里如室
    　　夏桀之时为长夜宫于深谷之中男女杂处十旬不出听政天乃大风沙一夕填此宫谷又为石室瑶台闗龙逄谏桀言曰吾之有民如天之有日日亡我则亡以龙逄为妖言而杀之其后复于山谷下作宫在上耆老相与谏桀又以为妖言而杀之
    　　夏桀之时费昌之河上见二日在东者烂烂将起在西者沈沈将灭疾雷之声昌问于冯夷曰何者为殷何者为夏冯夷曰西夏东殷于是费昌徙族归殷
    　　武王伐纣至盟津渡河大风波武王操戈秉麾麾之风波立霁
    　　鲁阳公与韩战酣而日暮援戈麾之日返三舍
    　　太公为灌坛令武王梦妇人当道夜哭问之曰吾是东海神女嫁于西海神童今灌坛令当道废我行我行必有大风雨而太公有德吾不敢以暴风雨过以毁君德武王明日召太公三日三夜果有疾风暴雨从太公邑外过 [37]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [37]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 博物志卷七 - Title of the chapter, indicating it is part of a larger work titled *The Catalogue of Rare Things*.
    • 昔夏禹观河见长人鱼身出曰 - Narrative opening describing a legendary event involving the Yellow River and a mythical figure.
    • 吾河精岂河伯耶 - Rhetorical question asking if the speaker is the spirit of the river, possibly referring to the River God.
    • 冯夷华阴潼乡人也得仙道化为河伯 - Statement about Feng Yi, a local from Huayin, becoming a river god after achieving immortality.
    • 岂道同哉 - Rhetorical question suggesting that both figures have achieved the same spiritual level.
    • 仙夷乘龙虎水神乘鱼龙其行恍惚万里如室 - Description of the modes of transportation and the mystical nature of their journeys.
    • 夏桀之时为长夜宫于深谷之中男女杂处十旬不出听政 - Description of a palace built by King Jie of Xia, emphasizing its isolation and moral decay.
    • 天乃大风扬沙一夕填此宫谷又为石室瑶台 - Description of a natural disaster that destroyed the palace.
    • 关龙逄谏桀言曰 - Statement about Guan Long Pang advising King Jie.
    • 吾之有民如天之有日日亡我则亡 - Metaphorical statement about the relationship between a ruler and his people.
    • 以龙逄为妖言而杀之 - Statement about King Jie's reaction to Guan Long Pang's advice.
    • 其后复于山谷下作宫在上耆老相与谏桀又以为妖言而杀之 - Continuation of the story about the palace and the fate of the elders who advised against it.
    • 夏桀之时费昌之河上见二日在东者烂烂将起在西者沈沈将灭 - Description of a celestial phenomenon observed by Fei Chang.
    • 疾雷之声昌问于冯夷曰何者为殷何者为夏 - Description of Fei Chang asking Feng Yi about the suns.
    • 冯夷曰西夏东殷于是费昌徙族归殷 - Feng Yi's response and Fei Chang's decision to move his family to the Shang dynasty.
    • 武王伐纣至盟津渡河大风波武王操戈秉麾麾之风波立霁 - Description of King Wu of Zhou crossing the Yellow River during his campaign against the Shang dynasty.
    • 鲁阳公与韩战酣而日暮援戈麾之日返三舍 - Description of a battle where the sun was made to return by the intervention of a general.
    • 太公为灌坛令武王梦妇人当道夜哭问之曰吾是东海神女嫁于西海神童今灌坛令当道废我行我行必有大风雨而太公有德吾不敢以暴风雨过以毁君德 - Dream of King Wu about a woman who claims to be a goddess and warns of a storm.
