    • 武王明日召太公三日三夜果有疾风暴雨从太公邑外过 - Fulfillment of the dream and the storm that passed by the town.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 夏禹 - The legendary founder of the Xia dynasty, known for his efforts in flood control.
    • 河伯 - The River God, a deity associated with the Yellow River.
    • 冯夷 - A legendary figure who became the River God after achieving immortality.
    • 夏桀 - The last ruler of the Xia dynasty, known for his tyranny.
    • 关龙逄 - An advisor to King Jie of Xia, known for his wise counsel.
    • 费昌 - A figure who observed a celestial phenomenon and moved his family to the Shang dynasty.
    • 武王伐纣 - The campaign of King Wu of Zhou against the Shang dynasty.
    • 鲁阳公 - A general who made the sun return during a battle.
    • 太公 - Laozi, a legendary figure and advisor to King Wu of Zhou.
    • 东海神女 - A goddess from the East Sea.
    • 西海神童 - A deity from the West Sea.
    • 灌坛令 - A position held by Laozi, a wise and virtuous official.

    Proper Nouns

    • 夏禹 - The legendary founder of the Xia dynasty.
    • 河伯 - The River God.
    • 冯夷 - A legendary figure who became the River God.
    • 夏桀 - The last ruler of the Xia dynasty.
    • 关龙逄 - An advisor to King Jie of Xia.
    • 费昌 - A figure who observed a celestial phenomenon.
    • 武王 - King Wu of Zhou.
    • 鲁阳公 - A general who made the sun return.
    • 太公 - Laozi, a legendary figure and advisor to King Wu of Zhou.
    • 东海神女 - A goddess from the East Sea.
    • 西海神童 - A deity from the West Sea.
    • 灌坛令 - A position held by Laozi.

    The Catalogue of Rare Things, Volume Seven
    Compiled by Zhang Hua of the Jin Dynasty

    Strange Tales
    In ancient times, Yu the Great, the founder of the Xia dynasty, was observing the Yellow River when he saw a tall figure emerge from the water with a fish-like body. The figure said, "I am the spirit of the river. Am I the River God?" Feng Yi, a man from Huayin, a village in Huayin, attained immortality and transformed into the River God. Is it not the same path to enlightenment? The immortal Feng Yi rides on dragons and tigers, while the water god rides on fish and dragons; their journeys are ethereal and far-reaching, as if traveling a thousand miles in a single room.

    During the reign of King Jie of Xia, a palace was built in a deep valley where men and women lived together for ten months without attending to state affairs. One night, a strong wind blew sand, filling the palace and the valley in one night. The palace was then rebuilt as a stone chamber and a jade terrace. Guan Long Pang advised King Jie, saying, "My people are like the sun to me; if the sun sets, I perish." King Jie considered this advice as nonsense and had Guan Long Pang executed. Later, another palace was built in a valley, and the elders of the town advised King Jie against it, but he also had them executed for spreading false rumors.

    During the reign of King Jie of Xia, Fei Chang, on the banks of the Yellow River, saw two suns: one in the east, shining brightly, and one in the west, slowly setting. There was a loud clap of thunder. Fei Chang asked Feng Yi, "Which is the Shang dynasty, and which is the Xia dynasty?" Feng Yi replied, "The west is Xia, and the east is Shang." Thus, Fei Chang moved his family to the Shang dynasty.

    King Wu of Zhou, in his campaign against King Zhou of Shang, reached Menglun and crossed the Yellow River. A great storm arose, but King Wu, wielding his spear and holding his flag, commanded the storm to cease, and it immediately stopped.

    The Duke of Lu Yang, fighting against the state of Han, was engaged in a fierce battle at dusk. He raised his spear and commanded the sun to return, and it did, moving back three hundred li.

    Laozi, serving as the magistrate of Guantan, had a dream where a woman blocked the road and wept at night. King Wu asked her, "I am the goddess of the East Sea, married to the boy of the West Sea. Now, the magistrate of Guantan blocks my path. If I pass, there will be a great storm, but Laozi has virtue, and I dare not pass with a storm to destroy his virtue." The next day, King Wu summoned Laozi, and for three days and nights, a violent wind and heavy rain passed by the town outside Laozi's jurisdiction. [37]

    晋文公出大蛇当道如拱文公反修德使吏守蛇吏梦天杀蛇曰何故当圣君道觉而视蛇则自死也
    　　齐景公伐宋过泰山梦二人怒公谓太公之神晏子谓宋祖汤与伊尹也为言其状汤晳容多发伊尹黒而短即所梦也景公进军不听军鼓毁乃散师不果伐
    　　徐偃王志云徐君宫人娠而生卵以为不祥弃之水濵独孤母有犬名鹄苍猎于水濵得所弃卵衔以东归独孤母以为异覆暖之遂沸成儿生时正偃故以为名徐君宫中闻之乃更録取长而仁智袭君徐国后鹄苍临死生角而九尾实黄龙也偃王乃葬之徐界中今见云狗垄偃王既主其国仁义着闻欲舟行上国乃通沟陈蔡之间得朱弓矢以巳得天瑞遂因名为弓自称徐偃王江淮诸侯皆服从偃从者三十六国周王闻之遣使乘驿一日至楚使伐之偃王仁不忍鬭害其民为楚所败逃走彭城武原县东山下百姓随之者以万数后遂名其山为徐山山上立石室有神灵民人祈祷今皆见存 [38]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [38]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 修德 - Practicing virtue; idiomatic expression requiring contextual explanation.
    • 不忍斗害其民 - Could not bear to fight and harm his people; idiomatic expression requiring contextual explanation.
    • 当圣君道 - Blocking the path of a sage ruler; metaphorical expression requiring careful translation.
    • 以为不祥 - Considered an omen; cultural context required.
    • 随之者以万数 - Numbered in the tens of thousands; contextual explanation required.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋文公 - Duke Wen of Jin, a ruler of the State of Jin during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 齐景公 - Duke Jing of Qi, a ruler of the State of Qi during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 泰山 - Mount Tai, one of the Five Great Mountains of China, considered sacred.
    • 太公之神 - The spirits of Duke Tai, a historical figure.
    • 晏子 - Yanzi, a famous statesman and diplomat of the State of Qi.
    • 宋祖汤与伊尹也 - The ancestors of Song, Tang and Yi Yin, historical figures from the Shang Dynasty.
    • 徐偃王 - Duke Xu Yan, a ruler of the State of Xu during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 鹄苍 - A dog named Hu'cang, a legendary figure.
    • 彭城 - Pengcheng, an ancient city in present-day Xuzhou, Jiangsu Province.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋文公 - Duke Wen of Jin.
    • 齐景公 - Duke Jing of Qi.
    • 泰山 - Mount Tai.
    • 太公之神 - The spirits of Duke Tai.
    • 晏子 - Yanzi.
    • 宋祖汤与伊尹也 - The ancestors of Song, Tang and Yi Yin.
    • 徐偃王 - Duke Xu Yan.
    • 鹄苍 - A dog named Hu'cang.
    • 彭城 - Pengcheng.

    Duke Wen of Jin encountered a large snake blocking the road, as if it were making an arch. Upon returning, he practiced virtue and instructed officials to guard the snake. An official dreamed that heaven killed the snake, saying why did it block the path of a sage ruler. Upon waking and seeing the snake, it was found dead.

    Duke Jing of Qi, on his campaign against Song, passed by Mount Tai and dreamed of two angry men, whom he identified as the spirits of Duke Tai. Yanzi said that the two were the ancestors of Song, Tang and Yi Yin, and described their appearance. Tang had a fair complexion and many hairs, while Yi Yin was dark and short, matching the description of the dream. Duke Jing did not advance his army, and the drumming was destroyed, so he dispersed his troops and did not ultimately attack Song.

    The records of Duke Xu Yan say that a palace woman of Duke Xu gave birth to an egg, which was considered an omen and discarded by the water. A woman named Dugu had a dog named Hu'cang, who while hunting by the water found the discarded egg and carried it back to the east. Dugu considered this unusual and warmed the egg, which then boiled and hatched into a child. The child was born with his face turned to the west, so he was named Xu Yan. The palace heard of this and took the child, who grew up to be kind and wise, eventually succeeding Duke Xu. When Hu'cang was about to die, it grew horns and a nine-tailed tail, revealing itself as a yellow dragon. Duke Xu Yan buried it in the borders of Xu, and it is now known as Dog Mound. After becoming ruler, Duke Xu Yan was known for his benevolence and righteousness, and he wished to travel by boat to the upper regions, so he dug canals between Chen and Cai. He obtained a red bow and arrow, which he believed was a sign from heaven, and thus named himself Xu Yan. The rulers of the Huai and Yangtze regions all submitted to him, and he had thirty-six countries following him. When King Zhou of Zhou heard of this, he sent an envoy who traveled by horseback in a day to reach Chu, ordering an attack on Duke Xu Yan. Out of kindness, Duke Xu Yan could not bear to fight and harm his people, and was defeated by Chu, fleeing to the east of Wu Yuan County in Pengcheng. The people who followed him numbered in the tens of thousands, and later the mountain was named Xu Mountain. On the mountain, a stone chamber was built, and it is said to be inhabited by divine beings. People still pray there today, and it is said to be inhabited by divine beings. [38]

    昔夸父与日相逐走渴饮于河谓不足北饮大泽未至渴而死弃其策杖化为邓林
    　　澹台子羽渡河赍千金之璧河伯欲之至中流阳侯波起两蛟挟船子羽左操璧右操劒击蛟皆死既渡三投璧于河伯河伯跃而归之子羽毁而去
    　　荆轲字次非渡河蛟夹船次非不走断其头而风波静除【周日用曰余甞行经荆将军墓墓与羊角哀冢邻昔安伯施云为秦将军所迁乃在此也其地在苑陵之源曽见其墓碑将军名轲次非字也】
    　　东阿王勇士有蕃丘防过神渊使饮马马沈防朝服拔劒入水二日一夜杀二蛟一龙而出雷随击之七日夜眇其左目
    　　汉滕公薨出葬东都门外公卿送丧驷马不行局地悲鸣跑蹄下地得石室有铭曰佳城郁郁三千年见白日吁嗟滕公居此室遂葬焉 [39]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [39]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 與日相逐走 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to chase the sun,' symbolizing a futile or impossible pursuit.
    • 北飲大澤 - Literally 'drink from the northern great marsh,' indicating a long journey or effort.
    • 棄其策杖化為鄧林 - 'Discard his staff and transform into the鄧林,' a mythological transformation, likely referring to a legendary forest.
    • 澹台子羽渡河赍千金之璧 - 'Zantai Ziyu crossed the river carrying a thousand-jin jade,' where '赍' means to carry or bring.
    • 陽侯波起 - 'Yanghou waves rise,' referring to a legendary wave monster.
    • 兩蛟夾船 - 'Two dragons seize the boat,' indicating a supernatural event.
    • 左操璧右操劍 - 'Holding the jade in the left hand and the sword in the right,' a balanced and ready stance.
    • 擊蛟皆死 - 'Strike the dragons and they all die,' indicating a heroic act.
    • 三投璧於河伯 - 'Three times throw the jade to the river god,' a ritual act.
    • 河伯躍而歸之 - 'The river god leaps and returns it,' indicating divine acknowledgment or favor.
    • 子羽毀而去 - 'Ziyu destroys (the jade) and leaves,' a symbolic act of renunciation.
    • 荊軻字次非 - 'Jing Ke, also known as Cifei,' indicating a dual name.
    • 蛟夾船次非不走 - 'Dragons seize the boat, Cifei does not move,' indicating bravery and steadfastness.
    • 斷其頭而風波靜除 - 'Cut off their heads and the waves calm down,' indicating a resolution of chaos.
    • 東阿王勇士有蕃丘防 - 'A brave warrior named Fanchu Fang from Dong'e King,' indicating a specific individual and their title.
    • 使飲馬馬沈 - 'Send to drink water, the horse sinks,' indicating a tragic event.
    • 朝服拔劍入水 - 'Dress in official robes, draw the sword, and enter the water,' indicating a dramatic and heroic action.
    • 兩日一夜殺二蛟一龍 - 'For two days and one night, kill two dragons and one dragon,' indicating a prolonged and intense battle.
    • 雷隨擊之 - 'Thunder follows and strikes,' indicating divine intervention.
    • 七日夜眇其左目 - 'For seven days and nights, blind his left eye,' indicating a severe consequence.
    • 漢滕公薨出葬東都門外 - 'Han Prince Teng died and was buried outside the eastern capital gate,' indicating a significant historical event.
    • 公卿送喪 - 'Officials accompany the funeral,' indicating a formal and respectful send-off.
    • 駟馬不行局地悲鳴 - 'Four-horse carriage does not move, the ground mourns and neighs,' indicating a poignant scene.
    • 跑蹄下地得石室 - 'Kick the hooves to the ground and find a stone chamber,' indicating a discovery.
    • 有銘曰佳城鬱鬱三千年見白日 - 'There is an inscription saying, 'The fine tomb is lush and will be seen in the white light for three thousand years,''' indicating a prophecy or epitaph.
    • 吁嗟滕公居此室遂葬焉 - 'Alas, Prince Teng resides in this room and is buried here,' indicating a final resting place.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 夸父 - A legendary figure from Chinese mythology known for his pursuit of the sun.
    • 鄧林 - A legendary forest, possibly referring to a mythological place.
    • 澹台子羽 - A historical figure, likely a noble or official.
    • 陽侯 - A legendary wave monster.
    • 荊軻 - A famous assassin from the Warring States period.
    • 次非 - Another name for Jing Ke.
    • 東阿王 - A title, likely referring to a regional ruler.
    • 蕃丘防 - A specific individual, likely a warrior.
    • 漢滕公 - A historical figure, likely a noble or official.
    • 東都門 - The eastern gate of the capital city during the Han dynasty.
    • 佳城 - A term for a fine tomb or mausoleum.

    Proper Nouns

    • 夸父 - A legendary figure.
    • 鄧林 - A legendary forest.
    • 澹台子羽 - A historical figure.
    • 陽侯 - A legendary wave monster.
    • 荊軻 - A famous assassin.
    • 次非 - Another name for Jing Ke.
    • 東阿王 - A title.
    • 蕃丘防 - A specific individual.
    • 漢滕公 - A historical figure.
    • 東都門 - The eastern gate of the capital city.
    • 佳城 - A term for a fine tomb.

    昔夸父与日相逐走，渴饮于河，谓不足，北饮大泽，未至渴而死，弃其策杖，化为邓林。

    　　澹台子羽渡河，赍千金之璧，河伯欲之。至中流，阳侯波起，两蛟挟船。子羽左操璧，右操剑，击蛟皆死。既渡，三投璧于河伯，河伯跃而归之，子羽毁而去。

    　　荆轲字次非，渡河，蛟夹船，次非不走，断其头而风波静除。【周日用曰：余甞行经荆将军墓，墓与羊角哀冢邻。昔安伯施云：为秦将军所迁，乃在此也。其地在苑陵之源，曽见其墓碑，将军名轲，次非字也。】

    　　东阿王勇士有蕃丘防，过神渊，使饮马，马沈。防朝服拔剑入水，二日一夜，杀二蛟一龙而出。雷随击之，七日夜眇其左目。

    　　汉滕公薨，出葬东都门外，公卿送丧，驷马不行，局地悲鸣，跑蹄下地，得石室，有铭曰：“佳城郁郁，三千年见白日。吁嗟滕公，居此室，遂葬焉。” [39]

    衞灵公葬得石椁铭曰不逢箕子灵公夺我里
    　　汉西都时南宫寝殿内有醇儒王史威长死葬铭曰明明哲士知存知亡崇陇髙原非宁非康不封不树作灵乘光厥铭何依王史威长
    　　元始元年中谒者沛郡史岑上书讼王宏夺董贤玺绶之功
    　　灵帝和光元年辽西太守黄翻上言海边有流尸露冠綘衣体貌完全后翻感梦云我伯夷之弟孤竹君也海水壊吾棺椁求见掩藏民有襁褓视者无疾而卒
    　　汉末闗中大乱有发前汉时冢者宫人犹活既出平复如旧魏郭后爱念之録着宫内常置左右问汉时宫中事説之了了皆有次序后崩哭泣过礼遂死焉【见述异説】
    　　汉末发范明友冢奴犹活明友霍光女聓説光家事废立之际多与汉书相似此奴常游走于民间无止住处今不知所在或云尚在余闻之于人可信而目不可见也
    　　大司马曹休所统中郎谢璋部曲义兵奚侬息女年四嵗病疫故埋葬五日复生太和三年诏令休使父母同时送女来视其年四月三日复病死四日埋葬至八日向墟邑采桑闻儿生活今能饮食如常 [40]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [40]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 衞灵公葬得石椁铭曰不逢箕子灵公夺我里 - A ruler of the State of Wei during the Spring and Autumn period; an inscription on a stone coffin; a reference to the historical figure Jizi, a minister of the Shang dynasty; a lament that Wei Linggong did not meet Jizi; metaphorical reference to the loss of something significant.
    • 汉西都时南宫寝殿内有醇儒王史威长死葬铭曰明明哲士知存知亡崇陇髙原非宁非康不封不树作灵乘光厥铭何依王史威长 - A pure or learned Confucian scholar; a wise and enlightened person; aware of both existence and extinction; a high and elevated place, possibly referring to a burial site; neither peaceful nor healthy, suggesting a state of unrest; no mound or trees are erected, indicating a simple burial; becoming a spirit that rides the light; what is the basis of this inscription?
    • 元始元年中谒者沛郡史岑上书讼王宏夺董贤玺绶之功 - The first year of the Yuan Shi era (AD 1); a court official responsible for receiving visitors; a county in the Han dynasty; a proper name, likely a historian or official; alleging that Wang Hong took credit for depriving Dong Xian of his seals and ribbons.
    • 灵帝和光元年辽西太守黄翻上言海边有流尸露冠綘衣体貌完全后翻感梦云我伯夷之弟孤竹君也海水壊吾棺椁求见掩藏民有襁褓视者无疾而卒 - The first year of the He Guang era during the reign of Emperor Ling (AD 172); the governor of Liaoxi; a proper name, likely the governor; a floating corpse; a hat and red robe, possibly indicating high status; completely intact in appearance; dreaming and saying; the brother of Bo Yi, a legendary figure from the Shang dynasty; the sea damaged my coffin; requesting to be buried and hidden; someone in swaddling clothes, likely a child; died without illness.
    • 汉末闗中大乱有发前汉时冢者宫人犹活既出平复如旧魏郭后爱念之録着宫内常置左右问汉时宫中事説之了了皆有次序后崩哭泣过礼遂死焉【见述异説】 - The region around Chang'an, the capital of the Western Han dynasty; great chaos; someone who opened a tomb from the Western Han dynasty; palace women still alive; returned to normal as before; the Empress Guo of the Wei dynasty; loved and remembered her; recorded in the palace; always placed by her side; asked about matters in the Han dynasty palace; spoke about them clearly; all in order; after her death; cried excessively beyond propriety; and died; as recorded in the *Shu Yi Shuo* (Strange Tales).
    • 汉末发范明友冢奴犹活明友霍光女聓説光家事废立之际多与汉书相似此奴常游走于民间无止住处今不知所在或云尚在余闻之于人可信而目不可见也 - A historical figure, possibly a relative of the Han dynasty; a powerful regent during the Western Han dynasty; daughter-in-law; the time of abdication and enthronement; much like the *Book of Han*; this slave often wandered among the common people; no fixed residence; now unknown where he is; some say he is still alive; I heard this from others; it is credible but cannot be seen with the eyes.
    • 大司马曹休所统中郎谢璋部曲义兵奚侬息女年四嵗病疫故埋葬五日复生太和三年诏令休使父母同时送女来视其年四月三日复病死四日埋葬至八日向墟邑采桑闻儿生活今能饮食如常 - A high-ranking military officer; a middle-ranking official; a military unit; a child who died of illness and was buried, then revived after five days; a decree issued in the third year of the Taihe era (AD 233); the child died again on the third day of the fourth month and was buried on the fourth day, then revived on the eighth day while picking mulberries; the child is now able to eat and drink as usual.

    Wei Linggong's stone coffin bore an inscription: "I did not meet Ji Zi, and Linggong seized my village."

    During the Western Han period, in the inner palace of the South Palace, there was a learned Confucian scholar named Wang Shih Weichang, who died and was buried with an inscription that read: "A wise and enlightened man, aware of both existence and extinction, he was interred on a high and elevated plain, neither peaceful nor healthy. No mound or trees were erected; he became a spirit that rides the light. What is the basis of this inscription? Wang Shih Weichang."

    In the first year of the Yuan Shi era, a court official from Pei Jun named Shi Cen submitted a petition, accusing Wang Hong of taking credit for depriving Dong Xian of his seals and ribbons.

    During the first year of the He Guang era under Emperor Ling, the governor of Liaoxi, Huang Fan, reported that a floating corpse with a hat and red robe, completely intact in appearance, was found by the sea. Afterward, Huang Fan dreamed that the corpse said, "I am the brother of Bo Yi, the lord of Guchu. The sea damaged my coffin; I request to be buried and hidden." A child in swaddling clothes who had seen the corpse died without illness.

    At the end of the Han dynasty, during the chaos in Guanzhong, a tomb from the Western Han era was opened, and palace women were found to be still alive. After they were released, they returned to normal as before. The Empress Guo of the Wei dynasty loved and remembered them, recording their stories in the palace and always placing them by her side. She asked them about matters in the Han dynasty palace, and they spoke about them clearly, all in order. After her death, she cried excessively beyond propriety and died.

    At the end of the Han dynasty, the tomb of Fan Mingyou was opened, and a slave was found to be still alive. Fan Mingyou was the daughter-in-law of Huo Guang. The slave spoke about the time of abdication and enthronement, much like the Book of Han. The slave often wandered among the common people, with no fixed residence. Now, it is unknown where he is; some say he is still alive. I heard this from others, and it is credible but cannot be seen with the eyes.

    Under the command of Grand Marshal Cao Xiu, the middle officer Xie Zhang, leading a group of righteous soldiers, had a daughter named Xi Nong who was four years old. She fell ill and was buried, but five days later, she came back to life. In the third year of the Taihe era, an edict was issued, ordering Cao Xiu to send the girl's parents to see her. In the third month, she fell ill again and died on the fourth day. She was buried on the eighth day, and while gathering mulberry leaves in the village, it was heard that the child was alive and could eat and drink as usual. [40]

    京兆都张潜客居辽东还后为驸马都尉闗内侯表言故为诸生太学时闻故太尉常山张颢为梁相天新雨后有鸟如山防飞翔近地市人掷之稍下堕民争取之即为一员石言县府颢令捶破之得一金印文曰忠孝侯印颢表上之藏于官库后议郎汝南樊行夷校书东观表上言尧舜之时旧有此官今天降印宜可复置
    　　孝武建元四年天雨粟孝元竟宁元年南阳郡中雨谷小者如黍粟而青黒味苦大者如大豆赤黄味如麦下三日生根叶状如大豆初生时也
    　　代城始筑立板榦一旦亡西南四五十板于泽中自立结草为外门因就营筑焉故其城周三十七里为九门故城处为东城

    　　博物志卷七
    　　钦定四库令书
    　　博物志卷八
    　　晋　张华　撰
    　　史补
    　　黄帝登仙其臣左彻者削木黄帝帅诸侯以朝之七年不还左彻乃立颛顼左彻亦仙去也
    　　尧之二女舜之二妃曰湘夫人舜崩二妃啼以涕挥竹竹尽斑 [41]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [41]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 故为诸生太学时 - A phrase indicating that the person was once a student at the Imperial Academy.
    • 忠孝侯印 - A seal inscribed with the words 'Seal of the Lord of Loyalty and Filial Piety'.
    • 孝武建元四年天雨粟 - A historical reference to the fourth year of Emperor Wu's Jianyuan era, when it rained millet.
    • 孝元竟宁元年南阳郡中雨谷小者如黍粟而青黑味苦大者如大豆赤黄味如麦下三日生根叶状如大豆初生时也 - A historical reference to the first year of Emperor Yuan's Jinguang era, when it rained grain in Nanyang Prefecture, with small grains like millet and bitter in taste, and large grains like soybeans and yellow in color.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 京兆 - Refers to the capital region of Chang'an, which was the center of the Western Han dynasty.
    • 太学 - The Imperial Academy, the highest institution of learning in ancient China.
    • 太尉 - One of the Three Excellencies in the Han dynasty, responsible for military affairs.
    • 梁相 - The governor of a certain region, likely a reference to the state of Liang.
    • 忠孝侯 - A title indicating loyalty and filial piety, often given to officials who demonstrated these virtues.
    • 孝武 - Emperor Wu of Han, known for his military conquests and cultural achievements.
    • 建元 - The first era name of Emperor Wu, marking the beginning of his reign.
    • 孝元 - Emperor Yuan of Han, known for his Confucian policies.
    • 竟宁 - The last era name of Emperor Yuan, marking the end of his reign.
    • 南阳郡 - A prefecture in central China, known for its fertile land and strategic importance.
    • 代城 - The city of Dai, located in northern China, known for its historical significance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 京兆都张潜 - A proper name, likely referring to a person from the Jingzhao region (长安附近地区，今陕西西安一带) who served as a guest in Liaodong and later became a驸马都尉 (驸马都尉，即皇帝女婿的官职) and 关内侯 (关内侯，一种爵位).
    • 孝武 - Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 建元 - The first era name of Emperor Wu.
    • 孝元 - Emperor Yuan of Han.
    • 竟宁 - The last era name of Emperor Yuan.
    • 南阳郡 - A prefecture in central China.
    • 代城 - The city of Dai in northern China.
    • 忠孝侯印 - A seal inscribed with the words '忠孝侯印' (Seal of the Lord of Loyalty and Filial Piety).

    Zhang Qian, a high-ranking official from the Jingzhao region, served as a guest in Liaodong and later became a驸马都尉 (驸马都尉，即皇帝女婿的官职) and 关内侯 (关内侯，一种爵位). He reported that when he was a student at the Imperial Academy, he heard that Zhang Hao (张颢), a former Grand Commandant (太尉) from Changshan, served as the governor (梁相) of a certain region. After a rain, a bird resembling a mountain creature flew near the ground, and people threw stones at it. It fell and turned into a stone, which was reported to the county government. Zhang Hao ordered the stone to be broken, and a golden seal inscribed with the words "忠孝侯印" (Seal of the Lord of Loyalty and Filial Piety) was found. Zhang Hao submitted the seal to the imperial court, where it was stored in the official treasury. Later, Fu Xingyi (樊行夷), a议郎 (议郎，一种官职) from Runan, who was compiling books at the Eastern Observatory, submitted a report stating that during the reigns of Yao and Shun, there was an official position called "忠孝侯" (Lord of Loyalty and Filial Piety), and since a seal had now appeared, it was appropriate to reinstate this position.

    In the fourth year of Emperor Wu's Jianyuan era, it rained millet. In the first year of Emperor Yuan's Jinguang era, in Nanyang Prefecture, it rained grain. The smaller grains were like millet and were green and black in color, with a bitter taste. The larger grains were like soybeans and were red and yellow in color, with a taste like wheat. After three days, they sprouted roots and leaves, resembling the initial stages of soybean growth.

    The city of Dai began to be constructed with wooden stakes. Suddenly, forty to fifty stakes disappeared to the southwest and reappeared in a marsh. The people built a grass gate and continued to construct the city walls. Eventually, the city was completed, with a circumference of thirty-seven li and nine gates. The original site of the city became known as East City.

    The Wondrous Tales of the World, Volume VII
    The Imperially Approved Four Treasuries
    The Wondrous Tales of the World, Volume VIII
    Compiled by Zhang Hua of the Jin Dynasty
    Supplement to History

    Emperor Huang ascended to immortality, and his minister Zuo Che (左彻) carved wood to create a statue of Huang. Huang led the feudal lords to pay homage to the statue, but he did not return for seven years. Zuo Che then enthroned Zuanxu (颛顼) and also ascended to immortality.

    The two daughters of Emperor Yao were the two consorts of Emperor Shun, known as the Ladies of Xiang (湘夫人). After Shun's death, the two ladies wept and their tears stained the bamboo, turning it all black.

    This translation aims to be faithful to the original text while ensuring that it reads naturally in English. Idiomatic expressions and cultural allusions are preserved, and proper nouns are rendered consistently. Syntactic ambiguities are addressed by breaking down complex sentences into more readable English while maintaining the original meaning. [41]

    处士东槐责禹乱天下事禹退作三章彊者攻弱者守攻城围郭盖禹始也
    　　太姒梦见商之庭产乃小子发取周庭梓树树之于阙间梓化为松栢棫柞觉惊以告文王文王曰慎勿言冬日之阳夏日之隂不召而万物自来天道尚左日月西移地道尚右水潦东流天不享于殷自发之生于今十年夷羊在牧水潦东流天下飞蝗满埜命之在周其信然乎
    　　武王伐殷舍于畿逢大雨焉革舆三百乘甲三千一日一夜行三百里以战于牧野
    　　成王冠周公使祝雍曰辞达而勿多也祝雍曰近于民逺于佞近于义啬于时惠于财任贤使能
    　　陛下离显先帝光耀以奉皇天之嘉禄钦顺仲夏之吉日遵并大道郊域康阜万国之休灵始加元服推逺童稚之幼志积文武之宠德肃勤高祖之清庙六合之内靡不防德嵗嵗与天无极
    　　右孝昭周成王冠辞
    　　止雨祝曰天生五谷以养人民今天雨不止用伤五谷如何如何灵而不幸杀牲以赛神灵雨则不止鸣鼓攻之朱緑绳萦而胁之 [42]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [42]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 处士东 - Ambiguous phrase; '处士' refers to a reclusive scholar or hermit, and '东' likely refers to a direction or a person named '东.'
    • 槐责禹乱天下事 - '槐责禹' is unclear; '槐' could be a name or a metaphor, and '责禹' means 'blame Yu' (referring to Yu the Great). '乱天下事' means 'disorder the affairs of the world.'
    • 退作三章 - '退' means 'retreat' or 'step back,' and '作三章' means 'compose three chapters' or 'create three sections.'
    • 强者攻弱者守 - '强者攻' means 'the strong attack,' and '弱者守' means 'the weak defend.'
    • 攻城围郭 - '攻城' means 'attack the city,' and '围郭' means 'surround the city walls.'
    • 太姒梦见商之庭产 - '太姒' is the wife of King Wen of Zhou, and '梦见' means 'dreamed of.' '商之庭' refers to the courtyard of the Shang dynasty, and '产' means 'give birth to a dragon.'
    • 小子发取周庭梓树树之于阙间 - '小子发' refers to King Wu of Zhou, and '取周庭梓树' means 'take the paulownia tree from the Zhou courtyard.' '树之于阙间' means 'plant it between the gates.'
    • 梓化为松柏棫柞 - '梓化为' means 'the paulownia transformed into,' and '松柏棫柞' refers to pine, cypress, and other trees.
    • 武王伐殷舍于畿逢大雨焉 - '武王伐殷' means 'King Wu of Zhou attacked the Shang,' and '舍于畿逢' means 'camped near the capital.' '大雨' means 'heavy rain.'
    • 革舆三百乘甲三千 - '革舆' means 'chariots,' and '三百乘' means 'three hundred.' '甲三千' means 'three thousand soldiers.'
    • 一日一夜行三百里以战于牧野 - '一日一夜' means 'in one day and night,' and '行三百里' means 'travel three hundred miles.' '以战于牧野' means 'to fight at Muye.'
    • 成王冠周公使祝雍曰辞达而勿多也 - '成王冠' means 'the crowning of King Cheng,' and '周公使祝雍' means 'Zhou Gong sent the priest Zhu Yong.' '辞达而勿多也' means 'the words should be clear and not excessive.'
    • 近于民远于佞近于义啬于时惠于财任贤使能 - '近于民' means 'close to the people,' '远于佞' means 'distant from flattery,' '近于义' means 'close to righteousness,' '啬于时' means 'economical with time,' '惠于财' means 'generous with wealth,' '任贤使能' means 'appoint the wise and use the capable.'
    • 陛下离显先帝光耀以奉皇天之嘉禄 - '陛下' refers to the emperor, '离显' means 'shine brightly,' '先帝' means 'the late emperor,' '光耀' means 'glory,' '以奉皇天之嘉禄' means 'to receive the blessings of the Heavenly Emperor.'
    • 钦顺仲夏之吉日遵并大道郊域康阜万国之休灵 - '钦顺' means 'reverently follow,' '仲夏之吉日' means 'auspicious day in midsummer,' '遵并大道' means 'follow the great way,' '郊域康阜' means 'prosperous suburbs,' '万国之休灵' means 'the spirits of all nations.'
    • 始加元服推远童稚之幼志弘积文武之宠德 - '始加元服' means 'beginning to wear the adult cap,' '推远' means 'distance oneself from,' '童稚之幼志' means 'childish aspirations,' '弘积' means 'broadly accumulate,' '文武之宠德' means 'the favored virtues of Wen and Wu.'
    • 肃勤高祖之清庙六合之内靡不防德岁岁与天无极 - '肃勤' means 'reverently serve,' '高祖之清庙' means 'the ancestral temple of the founder,' '六合之内' means 'within the universe,' '靡不防德' means 'no one is without virtue,' '岁岁与天无极' means 'year after year, matching the heavens.'
    • 右孝昭周成王冠辞 - '右' means 'right,' '孝昭' refers to Emperor Zhao of Han, and '周成王冠辞' means 'the crowning speech of King Cheng of Zhou.'
    • 止雨祝曰天生五谷以养人民今天雨不止用伤五谷如何如何 - '止雨' means 'stop the rain,' '祝曰' means 'the priest said,' '天生五谷' means 'Heaven produces grains,' '以养人民' means 'to nourish the people,' '今天雨不止' means 'today the rain does not stop,' '用伤五谷' means 'harming the grains,' '如何如何' means 'what should we do?'
    • 灵而不幸杀牲以赛神灵雨则不止鸣鼓攻之朱绿绳萦而胁之 - '灵而不幸' means 'if the spirits are not pleased,' '杀牲以赛' means 'sacrifice animals to appease,' '神灵雨则不止' means 'the rain from the spirits does not stop,' '鸣鼓攻之' means 'beat drums to attack it,' '朱绿绳萦而胁之' means 'bind it with red and green ropes and threaten it.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 处士 - A reclusive scholar or hermit in Chinese culture.
    • 太姒 - The wife of King Wen of Zhou, known for her wisdom and virtue.
    • 武王伐殷 - The campaign led by King Wu of Zhou against the Shang dynasty, leading to the establishment of the Zhou dynasty.
    • 成王冠 - The crowning ceremony of King Cheng of Zhou, a significant event in Zhou history.
    • 高祖之清庙 - The ancestral temple of the founder of a dynasty, symbolizing respect for ancestors.
    • 孝昭周成王冠辞 - The crowning speech of King Cheng of Zhou, preserved as a historical document.
    • 止雨祝 - A ritual to stop the rain, often involving sacrifices and prayers.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 处士东槐责禹乱天下事禹退作三章强者攻弱者守攻城围郭盖禹始也
    • 太姒梦见商之庭产小子发取周庭梓树树之于阙间梓化为松柏棫柞觉惊以告文王文王曰慎勿言冬日之阳夏日之隂不召而万物自来天道尚左日月西移地道尚右水潦东流天不享于殷自发之生于今十年夷羊在牧水潦东流天下飞蝗满埜命之在周其信然乎
    • 武王伐殷舍于畿逢大雨焉革舆三百乘甲三千一日一夜行三百里以战于牧野
    • 成王冠周公使祝雍曰辞达而勿多也近于民远于佞近于义啬于时惠于财任贤使能
    • 陛下离显先帝光耀以奉皇天之嘉禄钦顺仲夏之吉日遵并大道郊域康阜万国之休灵始加元服推远童稚之幼志弘积文武之宠德肃勤高祖之清庙六合之内靡不防德岁岁与天无极
    • 右孝昭周成王冠辞
    • 止雨祝曰天生五谷以养人民今天雨不止用伤五谷如何如何灵而不幸杀牲以赛神灵雨则不止鸣鼓攻之朱绿绳萦而胁之

    The Hermit Dong Gui blamed Yu the Great for causing chaos in the world, leading Yu to compose three chapters. The strong attacked and the weak defended, laying siege to cities and encircling walls—this was the beginning of Yu's reign.

    Queen Tai Si dreamed that the courtyard of the Shang dynasty gave birth to a dragon. Then, the young prince Fa took a paulownia tree from the Zhou courtyard and planted it between the gates. The tree transformed into pine, cypress, and other trees. She woke up in shock and informed King Wen. King Wen said, "Do not speak of it." In winter, the sun is on the left, and in summer, the moon is on the right. Without being summoned, all things come into being. Heaven favors the left, and the sun and moon move westward. Earth favors the right, and water flows eastward. Heaven no longer favors the Shang dynasty. Since the time of its birth, ten years have passed, and wild sheep graze in the fields. Water flows eastward, and locusts fill the wilds. The fate lies with the Zhou dynasty. Is this truly the case?

    King Wu of Zhou attacked the Shang and camped near the capital, where it rained heavily. He had three hundred chariots and three thousand soldiers. In one day and night, they traveled three hundred miles to fight at Muye.

    At the crowning of King Cheng, Zhou Gong sent the priest Zhu Yong, saying, "The words should be clear and not excessive." Zhu Yong said, "Be close to the people, distant from flattery, close to righteousness, economical with time, generous with wealth, and appoint the wise and use the capable."

    The emperor shines brightly with the glory of the late emperor, receiving the blessings of the Heavenly Emperor. Reverently follow the auspicious day in midsummer, adhering to the great way. The suburbs are prosperous, and the spirits of all nations are at peace. Beginning to wear the adult cap, distance oneself from childish aspirations and broadly accumulate the favored virtues of Wen and Wu. Reverently serve the ancestral temple of the founder, within the universe, no one is without virtue, year after year, matching the heavens.

    This is the crowning speech of King Cheng of Zhou.

    The priest said, "Heaven produces grains to nourish the people. Today, the rain does not stop, harming the grains. What should we do?" If the spirits are not pleased, sacrifice animals to appease them. If the rain from the spirits does not stop, beat drums to attack it, binding it with red and green ropes and threatening it. [42]

    请雨曰皇皇上天照临下土集地之灵神降甘雨庶物羣生咸得其所
    　　礼记曰孔子少孤不知其父墓母亡问于邹曼父之母乃合葬于防防墓又崩门人后至孔子问来何迟门人实对孔子不应如是者三乃潸然流涕而止曰古不修墓蒋济何晏夏侯王肃皆云无此事注记者谬时贤咸从之【周日用曰四士言无者后人何故而述之在愚所见实未之误矣且征在与梁纥埜合而生事多隐之况孔子生而父已死既隐何以知之非问曼父之母安得合葬于防也】
    　　孔子东游见二小儿辩鬭问其故一小儿曰我以日始出时去人近而日中时逺也一小儿曰我以日出时逺而日中时近一小儿曰日初出时大如车盖及日中时如盘盂此不为逺者小而近者大乎一小儿曰日初出沧沧凉凉及其中如探汤此不为近者而逺者凉乎孔子不能决两小儿笑曰孰谓汝多知乎亦出列子【周日用曰日当中向者炎气下也譬犹火气上而两旁者其炎凉可悉耳足明初出近而当中逺矣岂圣人不能决乎】 [43]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [43]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 請雨曰 - Formulaic expression meaning 'the prayer for rain says.'
    • 皇皇上天 - Refers to the supreme heaven, often used in religious or ritual contexts.
    • 照臨下土 - Means 'illuminating the lower earth,' symbolizing divine oversight.
    • 集地之靈 - Refers to the gathering of the earth's spirits, invoking a connection between heaven and earth.
    • 神降甘雨 - Means 'the gods send down sweet rain,' a common motif in prayers for rain.
    • 庶物羣生咸得其所 - Means 'all things and all living beings find their proper place,' emphasizing harmony and order.
    • 少孤 - Means 'lost his father at a young age,' a common expression in biographies of Confucius.
    • 不知其父墓 - Means 'did not know the location of his father's tomb,' highlighting a personal and cultural detail.
    • 乃合葬於防 - Means 'and were later buried together in Fang,' a specific historical event.
    • 潸然流涕 - Means 'shed tears involuntarily,' expressing deep emotion.
    • 古不修墓 - Means 'ancient people did not repair tombs,' a cultural practice or belief.
    • 注记者谬 - Means 'the annotators are mistaken,' a critical comment on historical sources.
    • 时贤咸从之 - Means 'contemporary scholars all followed this view,' indicating a consensus or debate.
    • 东游 - Means 'traveling east,' a geographical detail in Confucius's travels.
    • 辩鬭 - Means 'arguing' or 'debating,' describing the interaction between the children.
    • 日始出时去人近而日中时逺也 - Means 'the sun is closer when it first rises and farther when it is at midday,' a common misconception.
    • 日初出大如车盖及日中时如盘盂 - Means 'the sun is as large as a carriage canopy when it first rises and as small as a plate when it is at midday,' another misconception.
    • 日初出沧沧凉凉及其中如探汤 - Means 'the sun feels cool when it first rises and hot when it is at midday,' another misconception.
    • 不能决 - Means 'unable to decide,' indicating Confucius's admission of uncertainty.
    • 孰谓汝多知乎 - Means 'who said you know so much?' a rhetorical question from the children.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 禮記 - Refers to the *Rites of Zhou*, a key classical text on rituals and governance.
    • 孔子 - Confucius, the great Chinese philosopher and educator.
    • 邹曼父 - A historical figure, possibly a contemporary of Confucius.
    • 防 - A specific location in ancient China, likely associated with Confucius's family.
    • 蒋济、何晏、夏侯、王肃 - Prominent scholars and commentators from the Han and Wei dynasties, known for their interpretations of classical texts.
    • 列子 - A classical text attributed to Li Yuese, containing fables and philosophical stories.

    Proper Nouns

    • 禮記 - The *Rites of Zhou*, a classical text on rituals.
    • 孔子 - Confucius, the great Chinese philosopher.
    • 邹曼父 - A historical figure, possibly a contemporary of Confucius.
    • 防 - A specific location in ancient China.
    • 蒋济、何晏、夏侯、王肃 - Prominent scholars and commentators.
    • 列子 - A classical text attributed to Li Yuese.

    The prayer for rain says, "The supreme heaven illuminates the lower earth, gathers the earth's spirits, and sends down sweet rain so that all things and all living beings find their proper place."

    The Rites of Zhou says that Confucius was orphaned at a young age and did not know the location of his father's tomb. After his mother's death, he asked about it from the mother of Zou Manfu. They were later buried together in Fang. The tomb later collapsed. When his disciples arrived late, Confucius asked why they were late. The disciples honestly replied, but Confucius did not respond three times. He then shed tears involuntarily and said, "Ancient people did not repair tombs." Jiang Ji, He Yan, Xiahou Yuan, and Wang Su all said there was no such thing. The annotators were mistaken, and contemporary scholars all followed this view. [Zhou Riyong said: If the four scholars say there is no such thing, why did later people record it? In my opinion, this is not an error. Moreover, Zeng Yan and Liang He had a secret union and gave birth to a child, and many things were hidden. Since Confucius was born and his father had already died, how could he know? It was only after asking the mother of Zou Manfu that he could be buried together in Fang.]

    Confucius traveled east and saw two children arguing. He asked why they were arguing. One child said, "I think the sun is closer when it first rises and farther when it is at midday." The other child said, "I think the sun is farther when it first rises and closer when it is at midday." Another child said, "The sun is as large as a carriage canopy when it first rises and as small as a plate when it is at midday. Isn't this proof that the farther something is, the smaller it appears, and the closer it is, the larger it appears?" Another child said, "The sun feels cool when it first rises and hot when it is at midday. Isn't this proof that the closer something is, the hotter it is, and the farther it is, the cooler it is?" Confucius could not decide. The two children laughed and said, "Who said you know so much?" This story also appears in the Liezi. [Zhou Riyong said: When the sun is at midday, the heat rises upward, like fire rising and the sides being cool. It is clear that the sun is closer when it first rises and farther when it is at midday. How could the sage not be able to decide?] [43]

    子路与子贡过郑神社社树有鸟神牵率子路子贡説之乃止
    　　春秋哀公十有四年春西狩获麟公羊传曰有以告者孔子曰孰为来哉孰为来哉【卢氏曰以其时非应故孔子泣而感之麟口吐三防盖天使报圣人】
    　　左传曰叔孙氏之车子鉏商获麟以为不祥
    　　燕太子丹质于秦秦王遇之无礼不得意思欲归请于秦王王不听谬言曰令乌头白马生角乃可丹仰而叹乌即头白俯而嗟马生角秦王不得已而遣之为机发之桥欲陷丹丹驱驰过之而桥不发遁到闗闗门不开丹为鸡鸣于是众鸡悉鸣遂归
    　　詹何以独茧丝为纶芒针为钩荆篠为竿剖粒为饵引盈车之鱼于百仞之渊汨流之中纶不絶钩不申竿不挠 [44]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [44]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為之一空 - Set phrase meaning completely emptied; fields were left barren.
    • 孰為來哉 - Rhetorical question meaning 'who has come?' used to express surprise or wonder.
    • 烏頭白馬生角 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'a crow's head and a white horse growing horns,' indicating something unlikely or impossible.
    • 為機發之橋 - Trap or mechanism designed to catch someone.
    • 為雞鳴 - Imitation of the crowing of a rooster.
    • 獨茧絲為纶芒針為钩荆篠為竿剖粒為饵 - Fishing technique using a single silkworm thread for the line, a sharp needle for the hook, a bamboo rod, and small pieces of food as bait.
    • 引盈车之魚于百仞之渊汨流之中 - Catching a large quantity of fish from a deep and fast-flowing river.
    • 纶不絶钩不申竿不挠 - Line did not break, hook did not bend, and rod did not bend, indicating the effectiveness of the fishing technique.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 子路與子貢 - Zilu and Zigong, two of Confucius' disciples mentioned in the Analects and other classical texts.
    • 鄭神社 - Shrine in Zheng, a reference to a specific location in ancient China.
    • 麒麟 - Qilin, a mythical creature in Chinese mythology associated with peace and prosperity.
    • 叔孫氏之車子鉏商 - Chushang, a retainer of the Shusun clan, a powerful family during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 燕太子丹 - Yan太子 Dan, the crown prince of the state of Yan during the Warring States period, known for his involvement in the assassination attempt on the Qin king.
    • 秦王 - King of Qin, who later became the first emperor of the Qin dynasty.
    • 詹何 - Zhan He, a famous fisherman in ancient China known for his skillful fishing techniques.

    Proper Nouns

    • 子路 - Disciple of Confucius.
    • 子貢 - Disciple of Confucius.
    • 鄭 - Ancient state in China.
    • 麒麟 - Mythical creature in Chinese mythology.
    • 叔孫氏 - Powerful family during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 鉏商 - Retainer of the Shusun clan.
    • 燕太子丹 - Crown prince of the state of Yan.
    • 秦王 - Ruler of the state of Qin.
    • 詹何 - Famous fisherman in ancient China.

    Zilu and Zigong passed by a shrine in Zheng. There was a bird deity that led them, but they were persuaded to stop.

    During the reign of Duke Ai of Lu in the fourteenth year of his rule, in spring, while hunting in the west, a qilin was captured. According to the Gongyang Commentary, someone reported this to Confucius, who said, "Who has come? Who has come?" (The Lüshi commentary states that because the time was not appropriate, Confucius wept and was moved, and the qilin spat out three arrows, which were believed to be a divine message to the sage.)

    The Zuo Commentary states that Chushang, a retainer of the Shusun clan, captured a qilin and considered it an ill omen.

    Yan太子 Dan was held hostage in Qin. The king of Qin treated him rudely, and Dan had no way to express his desire to return home. He requested permission to leave from the king, but the king refused, saying, "Let a crow's head and a white horse grow horns, and only then will I let you go." Dan looked up and sighed, and his head turned white. He looked down and lamented, and the horse grew horns. The king of Qin, unable to do otherwise, sent him away, setting up a trap in the form of a bridge to catch Dan. But when Dan rushed across it, the bridge did not collapse. He fled to the border, where the gates were closed. Dan imitated the crowing of a rooster, and all the chickens in the area began to crow. Thus, he managed to escape.

    Zhan He used a single silkworm thread for his fishing line, a sharp needle for the hook, a bamboo rod, and small pieces of food as bait. He caught a full cartload of fish from a deep and fast-flowing river. The line did not break, the hook did not bend, and the rod did not bend. [44]

    薛谭学讴于秦青未穷青之防于一日遂辞归秦青乃饯于郊衢抚节悲歌声震林木响遏行云薛谭乃谢求返终身不敢言归秦青顾谓其友曰昔韩娥东之齐匮粮过雍门鬻歌假食而去余响绕梁三日不絶左右叹其神妙去过逆旅凡人辱之韩娥因曼声哀哭一里老幼喜欢忭舞弗能自禁乃厚赂而遣之故雍门人至今善歌哭效娥之遗声也
    　　赵襄子率徒十万狩于中山借艿燔林燀赫百里有人从石壁中出随烟上下若无所经涉者襄子以为物徐察之乃人也问其奚道而处石奚道而入火其人曰奚物为石火襄子曰而向之所出者石也所入者火也其人曰不知也魏文侯闻之问于子夏曰彼何人哉子夏曰以商所闻于夫子和者同于物物无得而伤阂者游金石之间及蹈于水火皆可也文侯曰吾子奚不为之子夏曰刳心去智商未能也虽然试语之有暇矣文侯曰夫子奚不为之子夏曰夫子能而不为之也文侯大悦
    　　更羸谓魏王曰臣能射为虚发而下鸟王曰然可试于此乎适闻有鸟从东来羸虚发而下之也 [45]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [45]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 學讴于秦青 - Learn to sing from Qin Qing, a specific reference to learning music.
    • 未穷青之防于一日 - Did not exhaust Qin Qing's teachings in one day, indicating the brevity of the learning period.
    • 遂辞归 - Thus he left to return home, a formal way of saying he decided to leave.
    • 秦青乃饯于郊衢 - Qin Qing then held a farewell feast at the outskirts, a formal farewell ceremony.
    • 抚节悲歌 - Struck the rhythm and sang mournfully, a poetic description of singing.
    • 声震林木 - The sound shook the trees, a hyperbolic expression of the loudness.
    • 响遏行云 - The echo stopped the passing clouds, another hyperbolic expression of the powerful sound.
    • 谢求返 - Apologized and requested to return, indicating a change of heart.
    • 顾谓其友 - Turned to his friend and said, a formal way of describing a conversation.
    • 昔韩娥东之齐 - Once, Han E went east to Qi, a historical reference to a famous singer.
    • 匮粮 - Ran out of food, indicating a lack of resources.
    • 鬻歌假食 - Sold songs for food, a metaphorical way of describing singing for sustenance.
    • 余响绕梁三日不绝 - The lingering sound echoed around the beam for three days without ceasing, a hyperbolic expression of the lasting effect of her singing.
    • 左右叹其神妙 - Those around her marveled at her divine skill, indicating admiration.
    • 逆旅 - Inn, a place where travelers stay.
    • 凡人辱之 - Ordinary people insulted her, indicating mistreatment.
    • 曼声哀哭 - Sang mournfully, a poetic description of crying.
    • 喜欢忭舞 - Were delighted and danced joyfully, indicating a positive reaction.
    • 弗能自禁 - Could not control themselves, indicating an involuntary response.
    • 厚赂而遣之 - Paid her generously and sent her away, a formal way of describing compensation.
    • 故雍门人至今善歌哭 - Therefore, the people of Yongmen still excel at singing and crying, indicating a lasting legacy.
    • 效娥之遗声也 - Imitating Han E's lingering sound, indicating the influence of her singing.
    • 赵襄子率徒十万狩于中山 - Zhao Xiangzi led ten thousand soldiers on a hunt in Zhongshan, a historical reference to a military expedition.
    • 借艿燔林 - Burned the forest with firewood, a metaphorical way of describing a large fire.
    • 燀赫百里 - Blazed for a hundred li, a hyperbolic expression of the extent of the fire.
    • 从石壁中出 - Came out from the stone wall, indicating a mysterious appearance.
    • 随烟上下 - Rose and fell with the smoke, a poetic description of movement.
    • 若无所经涉者 - As if they had not traveled anywhere, indicating a lack of physical presence.
    • 以为鬼物 - Thought it was a ghost, indicating a supernatural interpretation.
    • 徐察之 - Carefully observed it, indicating a closer look.
    • 乃人也 - It was a person, indicating a realization.
    • 奚道而处石 - What path did they take to be in the stone? a question about the method.
    • 奚道而入火 - What path did they take to enter the fire? another question about the method.
    • 刳心去智 - Carve out the heart and remove intelligence, a metaphorical way of describing a state of emptiness.
    • 更羸谓魏王 - Geng Lai told King Wei of Wei, a formal way of describing a conversation.
    • 臣能射为虚发而下鸟 - I can shoot and bring down birds with a mere release of the bowstring, indicating a claim of skill.
    • 适闻有鸟从东来 - Just heard that a bird came from the east, indicating a recent observation.
    • 虚发而下之 - Released the bowstring and brought it down, indicating the action of shooting.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 薛谭 - A historical figure, a student of Qin Qing.
    • 秦青 - A historical figure, a renowned musician and teacher.
    • 韩娥 - A historical figure, a famous singer known for her powerful voice.
    • 齐 - The state of Qi, a major power during the Warring States period.
    • 雍门 - A place in the state of Qi, known for its music and culture.
    • 赵襄子 - A historical figure, a ruler of the state of Zhao.
    • 中山 - The state of Zhongshan, a small state between the states of Zhao and Yan.
    • 魏文侯 - A historical figure, a ruler of the state of Wei.
    • 子夏 - A historical figure, a disciple of Confucius and a prominent scholar.
    • 夫子 - A term of respect for a teacher or sage, referring to Confucius.
    • 更羸 - A historical figure, a skilled archer and advisor to King Wei of Wei.

    Proper Nouns

    • 薛谭 - A historical figure, a student of Qin Qing.
    • 秦青 - A historical figure, a renowned musician and teacher.
    • 韩娥 - A historical figure, a famous singer known for her powerful voice.
    • 齐 - The state of Qi, a major power during the Warring States period.
    • 雍门 - A place in the state of Qi, known for its music and culture.
    • 赵襄子 - A historical figure, a ruler of the state of Zhao.
    • 中山 - The state of Zhongshan, a small state between the states of Zhao and Yan.
    • 魏文侯 - A historical figure, a ruler of the state of Wei.
    • 子夏 - A historical figure, a disciple of Confucius and a prominent scholar.
    • 夫子 - A term of respect for a teacher or sage, referring to Confucius.
    • 更羸 - A historical figure, a skilled archer and advisor to King Wei of Wei.

    薛谭学习唱歌，没有在一天内掌握秦青的所有技巧，于是决定回家。秦青在郊外举行了告别宴会，敲击节奏，悲歌高唱，声音震响树木，回声遏止行云。薛谭道歉并请求返回，从此再也不敢说回家。秦青转头对他的朋友说：“从前，韩娥向东前往齐国，粮食用尽，经过雍门卖唱求食，她的歌声余音绕梁三日不绝，周围的人都赞叹她的神妙。后来，她在一家旅店中受到侮辱，韩娥于是悲伤地哭泣，一里内的老幼都高兴地跳舞，无法自控，于是她得到了丰厚的报酬并离开了。因此，雍门的人至今擅长唱歌和哭泣，模仿韩娥的余音。”

    赵襄子率领十万士兵在中山狩猎，借火焚烧森林，火焰蔓延百里。有人从石壁中出现，随着烟雾上下，好像没有经过任何地方。赵襄子以为是鬼物，仔细观察后，发现是一个人。赵襄子问他：“你是如何在石头中出现的？又是如何进入火中的？”那人回答说：“这是什么石头和火？”赵襄子说：“你刚才出现的地方是石头，进入的地方是火。”那人回答说：“我不知道。”

    魏文侯听说这件事，问子夏：“那个人是谁？”子夏回答说：“根据我对夫子的了解，和谐的人与万物相同，不会受到伤害，可以在金石之间游走，甚至可以踏入水火。”魏文侯说：“你为什么不这样做？”子夏回答说：“我还没有达到那种境界。”虽然如此，可以试着说说，有空的时候。魏文侯说：“夫子为什么不这样做？”子夏回答说：“夫子有能力但不愿意做。”魏文侯非常高兴。

    更羸对魏王说：“我能射箭不用瞄准就能射下鸟。”魏王说：“真的吗？可以在这里试试吗？”更羸说：“我刚听到有鸟从东边飞来，于是射箭，鸟果然被射下。” [45]

    澹台子羽子溺水死欲葬之灭明曰此命也与蝼蚁何亲与鱼鼈何雠遂使勿葬
    　　列传云聂政刺韩相白虹为之贯日要离刺庆忌彗星袭月专诸刺呉王僚鹰击殿上
    　　齐桓公出因与管仲故道自炖煌西涉流沙徃外国沙石千余里中无水时则有沃流处人莫能知皆乘骆驼骆驼知水脉遇其处辄停不肯行以足蹋地人于其蹋处掘之辄得水
    　　楚熊渠子夜行射寝石以为虎伏矢为没羽
    　　汉武帝好仙道祭祀名山大泽以求神仙之道时西王母遣使乘白鹿告帝当来乃供帐九华殿以待之七月七日夜漏七刻王母乘紫云车而至于殿西南面东向头上太华髻青气郁郁如云有三青鸟如乌大立侍母旁时设九微灯帝东面西向王母索七桃大如弹丸以五枚与帝母食二枚帝食桃辄以核着膝前母曰取此核将何为帝曰此桃甘美欲种之母笑曰此桃三千年一生实唯帝与母对坐其从者皆不得进时东方朔窃从殿南厢朱鸟牖中窥母母顾之谓帝曰此窥牖小儿甞三来盗吾此桃帝乃大怪之由此世人谓方朔神仙也 [46]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [46]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 澹台子羽子溺水死欲葬之 - Describes a situation where a person named Zihui of Dantai drowned and was to be buried.
    • 灭明曰此命也与 - A rhetorical question by Muming, suggesting that this was fate.
    • 蝼蚁何亲与鱼鼈何雠 - Another rhetorical question by Muming, asking whether the drowned person had any close ties with ants or enmity with turtles, implying that death is impartial.
    • 遂使勿葬 - Muming's final decision to not bury the body, based on his interpretation of fate.
    • 列传云聂政刺韩相白虹为之贯日 - A reference to the biography stating that Nie Zheng assassinated the prime minister of Han, causing a white rainbow to pass through the sun.
    • 要离刺庆忌彗星袭月 - Another reference to the biography stating that Yao Li assassinated King Qing Ji, causing a comet to strike the moon.
    • 专诸刺呉王僚鹰击殿上 - Another reference to the biography stating that Zhu assassinated King Wuyao, causing eagles to strike the palace.
    • 齐桓公出因与管仲故道自炖煌西涉流沙往外国 - Describes Duke Huan of Qi traveling west from Dunhuang through the desert to a foreign land.
    • 骆驼知水脉遇其处辄停不肯行以足蹋地人于其蹋处掘之辄得水 - Explains how camels know where water is underground and how people can find water by digging where the camel has stepped.
    • 楚熊渠子夜行射寝石以为虎伏矢为没羽 - Describes a story where Xiong Qiu of Chu shot a sleeping stone, mistaking it for a tiger, and the arrow was embedded in the stone.
    • 汉武帝好仙道祭祀名山大泽以求神仙之道 - Describes Emperor Wu of Han's interest in Daoist practices and his efforts to find immortality through rituals.
    • 西王母遣使乘白鹿告帝当来乃供帐九华殿以待之 - Describes the Queen Mother of the West sending a messenger on a white deer to inform the emperor of her arrival, and the emperor preparing a grand hall for her.
    • 七月七日夜漏七刻王母乘紫云车而至于殿西南面东向 - Describes the Queen Mother arriving on a purple cloud chariot at midnight on the seventh day of the seventh month, facing east.
    • 头上太华髻青气郁郁如云有三青鸟如乌大立侍母旁 - Describes the Queen Mother's appearance, including her hairstyle and the three blue birds standing beside her.
    • 时设九微灯帝东面西向王母索七桃大如弹丸以五枚与帝母食二枚 - Describes the setting of nine micro lamps, the emperor facing west, and the Queen Mother giving the emperor five out of seven peaches, eating two herself.
    • 帝食桃辄以核着膝前母曰取此核将何为帝曰此桃甘美欲种之母笑曰此桃三千年一生实唯帝与母对坐其从者皆不得进时东方朔窃从殿南厢朱鸟牖中窥母母顾之谓帝曰此窥牖小儿尝三来盗吾此桃帝乃大怪之由此世人谓方朔神仙也 - The emperor's reaction to the peach seeds and the Queen Mother's explanation, leading to the belief that Fang Shuo was a deity.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 澹台子羽子 - A historical figure, Zihui of Dantai, who drowned.
    • 灭明 - Muming, a historical figure who made the decision not to bury Zihui.
    • 列传 - Refers to the biographies in historical records.
    • 聂政 - Nie Zheng, a famous assassin in Chinese history.
    • 要离 - Yao Li, another famous assassin.
    • 专诸 - Zhu, yet another famous assassin.
    • 齐桓公 - Duke Huan of Qi, a famous ruler of the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 管仲 - Guan Zhong, a famous statesman and advisor to Duke Huan of Qi.
    • 炖煌 - Dunhuang, an important city on the Silk Road.
    • 楚熊渠子 - Xiong Qiu of Chu, a historical figure.
    • 汉武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han, a famous emperor of the Han dynasty.
    • 西王母 - The Queen Mother of the West, a mythical figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 东方朔 - Fang Shuo, a famous scholar and court jester during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han.

    Proper Nouns

    • 澹台子羽子 - Zihui of Dantai
    • 灭明 - Muming
    • 聂政 - Nie Zheng
    • 要离 - Yao Li
    • 专诸 - Zhu
    • 齐桓公 - Duke Huan of Qi
    • 管仲 - Guan Zhong
    • 炖煌 - Dunhuang
    • 楚熊渠子 - Xiong Qiu of Chu
    • 汉武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han
    • 西王母 - The Queen Mother of the West
    • 东方朔 - Fang Shuo

    Zihui of Dantai drowned and was to be buried. Muming said, "Is this fate? What connection does he have with ants, and what enmity does he have with turtles?" He then decided not to bury him.

    The biographies record that Nie Zheng assassinated the prime minister of Han, causing a white rainbow to pass through the sun; Yao Li assassinated King Qing Ji, causing a comet to strike the moon; and Zhu assassinated King Wuyao, causing eagles to strike the palace.

    Duke Huan of Qi traveled west from Dunhuang through the desert to a foreign land. The journey was over a thousand miles of sand and stones, with no water. Occasionally, there were places where water flowed, but no one could find them. They all rode camels, which knew where water was underground. When they found such a place, the camels would stop and refuse to move, and people would dig where the camels had stepped and find water.

    Xiong Qiu of Chu was walking at night and shot a sleeping stone, mistaking it for a tiger. The arrow was embedded in the stone.

    Emperor Wu of Han was fond of Daoist practices and sought immortality through rituals, offering sacrifices to famous mountains and marshes. At one time, the Queen Mother of the West sent a messenger riding on a white deer to inform the emperor that she was coming. The emperor prepared a grand hall in the Nine Flowered Palace to await her. On the seventh day of the seventh month at midnight, the Queen Mother arrived on a purple cloud chariot, facing east. She had a high and elegant hairstyle, with a blue mist surrounding her like clouds, and three blue birds as large as crows standing beside her. The emperor lit nine micro lamps and faced west. The Queen Mother asked for seven peaches, each as large as a pea, and gave five to the emperor and ate two herself. The emperor, after eating the peaches, placed the seeds in front of him. The Queen Mother asked, "What will you do with these seeds?" The emperor replied, "These peaches are sweet and I want to plant them." The Queen Mother laughed and said, "These peaches take three thousand years to bear fruit. Only you and I can sit together while they grow. Your attendants cannot enter." At that moment, Fang Shuo secretly peered through a window in the south wing of the palace, and the Queen Mother turned to the emperor and said, "This little boy who peeks through the window has come three times to steal my peaches." The emperor was greatly surprised and from then on, people believed that Fang Shuo was a deity. [46]

    君山有道与呉包山潜通上有美酒数斗得饮者不死汉武帝斋七日遣男女数十人至君山得酒欲饮之东方朔曰臣识此酒请视之因一饮至尽帝欲杀之朔乃曰杀朔若死此为不验以其有验杀亦不死乃赦之

    　　博物志卷八
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　博物志卷九
    　　晋　张华　撰
    　　杂説上
    　　老子云万民皆付西王母唯王者圣人真人仙人道人之命上属九天君耳
    　　黄帝治天下百年而死民畏其神百年以其数百年故曰黄帝三百年 [47]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [47]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 潛通 - Hidden communication or connection.
    • 得飲者不死 - Idiomatic expression indicating that drinking the wine grants immortality.
    • 一飲至盡 - Drank it all at once.
    • 殺朔若死此為不驗 - If I am killed, this wine will not be proven effective.
    • 以其有驗殺亦不死 - Since it is effective, even if I am killed, I will not die.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 君山 - Junshan Mountain, a mountain in Hunan Province, China, known for its historical and mythological significance.
    • 呉包山 - Wu Baoshan, another mountain in the region, often associated with legends and myths.
    • 漢武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han, one of the most famous emperors of the Han Dynasty, known for his military conquests and cultural achievements.
    • 東方朔 - Dongfang Shuo, a famous scholar and court jester during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, known for his wit and wisdom.
    • 博物志卷八 - Volume 8 of the 'Book of Miscellaneous Records,' a classical Chinese text compiled by Zhang Hua during the Jin Dynasty.
    • 欽定四庫全書 - 'Complete Library in Four Branches of Literature,' a massive collection of Chinese literature commissioned by the Qing Dynasty.
    • 博物志卷九 - Volume 9 of the 'Book of Miscellaneous Records.'
    • 晉 張華 撰 - Compiled by Zhang Hua of the Jin Dynasty.
    • 雜說上 - First section of miscellaneous sayings or anecdotes in the text.
    • 老子云 - A statement attributed to Laozi, the founder of Daoism, whose teachings are recorded in the 'Tao Te Ching.'
    • 萬民皆付西王母 - All common people are entrusted to the Queen Mother of the West, a figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 唯王者聖人真人仙人道人之命上屬九天君耳 - Only kings, sages, true men, immortals, and Daoists are entrusted to the Nine Heavens, a higher spiritual realm.
    • 黃帝治天下百年而死 - The legendary Yellow Emperor, a mythological figure in Chinese history, who ruled for 100 years before dying.
    • 民畏其神百年以其數百年故曰黃帝三百年 - The people revered the Yellow Emperor for 100 years, and because of his long rule, he is said to have reigned for 300 years.

    Proper Nouns

    • 數斗 - Several dou (a unit of volume).
    • 齋七日 - Fasted for seven days.
    • 遣男女數十人 - Sent dozens of men and women.

    Jinshan has a hidden path that connects secretly with Wu Baoshan. On top of it, there is a fine wine of several dou; whoever drinks it will not die. Emperor Wu of Han fasted for seven days and sent dozens of men and women to Jinshan, where they obtained the wine and wanted to drink it. Dongfang Shuo said, "I recognize this wine. Please let me see it." He then drank it all at once. The emperor wanted to kill him. Dongfang Shuo said, "If I am killed, this wine will not be proven effective. Since it is effective, even if I am killed, I will not die." Thus, he was pardoned.

    Volume 8 of the Book of Miscellaneous Records
    Imperially Approved Four Treasuries Complete Library
    Volume 9 of the Book of Miscellaneous Records
    Compiled by Zhang Hua of the Jin Dynasty
    Miscellaneous Sayings Section 1

    Laozi said that all common people are entrusted to the Queen Mother of the West, while only kings, sages, true men, immortals, and Daoists are entrusted to the Nine Heavens.

    The Yellow Emperor ruled for 100 years before dying. The people revered his spirit for 100 years, and because of his long rule, he is said to have reigned for 300 years. [47]

    上古男子三十而妻女二十而嫁曽子曰弟子不学古知之矣贫者不胜其忧富者不胜其乐
    　　昔西夏仁而去兵城郭不脩武士无位唐伐之西夏亡昔者都贤神道废人事其谋臣不用防是从忠臣无禄神巫用国
    　　榆烱氏之君孤而无徒曲沃进伐之以亡
    　　昔有巢氏有臣而贵任之专国主断已而夺之臣怒而生变有巢氏以亡昔者清阳强力贵美女不治国而亡
    　　昔有洛氏宫室无常囿池广大人民困匮商伐之有洛以亡
    　　神仙传曰説上据箕尾为宿嵗星降为东方朔傅説死后有此宿东方生无嵗星
    　　曽子曰好吾者知吾美矣恶我者知吾恶矣
    　　思士不妻而感思女不夫而孕后稷生乎巨迹伊尹生乎空桑
    　　箕子居朝鲜其后伐燕复之朝鲜亡入海为鲜国师两妻黒色珥两青蛇盖勾芒也
    　　汉兴多瑞应至武帝之世特甚麟鳯数见王莽时郡国多称瑞应嵗嵗相寻皆由顺时之欲承防求媚多无实应乃使人猜疑
    　　子胥伐楚燔其府库破其九龙之钟 [48]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [48]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 上古男子三十而妻女二十而嫁 - Traditional practice in ancient China where men married at thirty and women at twenty.
    • 曾子曰弟子不学古知之矣 - Statement by Zengzi (曾子), a Confucian disciple, indicating that his students do not need to study ancient texts to understand certain truths.
    • 贫者不胜其忧富者不胜其乐 - Saying about the emotional states of the poor and the rich.
    • 昔西夏仁而去兵城郭不脩武士无位 - Historical reference to the Western Xia (西夏) dynasty, which was known for its peace and lack of military preparedness.
    • 唐伐之西夏亡 - The Tang dynasty attacked and conquered the Western Xia.
    • 昔者玄都贤鬼神道废人事其谋臣不用龟防是从忠臣无禄神巫用国 - Reference to the ancient kingdom of Xuandu (玄都), where the rulers relied on divination and neglected human affairs.
    • 榆炯氏之君孤而无徒曲沃进伐之以亡 - Reference to the kingdom of Yuyong (榆炯), whose ruler was isolated and eventually conquered by Quwo (曲沃).
    • 昔有巢氏有臣而贵任之专国主断已而夺之臣怒而生变有巢氏以亡 - Reference to You Chao (有巢氏), a legendary ruler who was overthrown by his own officials.
    • 昔者清阳强力贵美女不治国而亡 - Reference to Qingyang (清阳), a ruler who favored beauty and neglected state affairs.
    • 昔有洛氏宫室无常囿池广大人民困匮商伐之有洛以亡 - Reference to the kingdom of Lu (有洛), which was destroyed by the Shang dynasty due to its extravagant lifestyle.
    • 神仙传曰说上据箕尾为宿岁星降为东方朔傅说死后有此宿东方生无岁星 - Reference to the *Shenxian Zhuan* (神仙传), a book about immortals, and the constellation of Jiwěi (箕尾) and the planet Jupiter (岁星).
    • 曾子曰好吾者知吾美矣恶我者知吾恶矣 - Statement by Zengzi about the perception of others.
    • 思士不妻而感思女不夫而孕后稷生乎巨迹伊尹生乎空桑 - References to historical figures like Houji (后稷) and Yi Yin (伊尹) who were born under unusual circumstances.
    • 箕子居朝鲜其后伐燕复之朝鲜亡入海为鲜国师两妻黑色珥两青蛇盖勾芒也 - Reference to Ji Zi (箕子), who fled to Korea and later returned to conquer Yan (燕) and establish a new state.
    • 汉兴多瑞应至武帝之世特甚麟凤数见王莽时郡国多称瑞应岁岁相寻皆由顺时之欲承防求媚多无实应乃使人猜疑 - Reference to the Han dynasty, where many auspicious signs were reported, especially during the reign of Emperor Wu, and later during the reign of Wang Mang.
    • 子胥伐楚燔其府库破其九龙之钟 - Reference to Sun Wu (子胥), who attacked Chu and destroyed its treasures.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 曾子 - A prominent Confucian disciple and philosopher.
    • 西夏 - An ancient kingdom in China, known for its peace and lack of military preparedness.
    • 玄都 - An ancient kingdom where the rulers relied on divination and neglected human affairs.
    • 榆炯 - An ancient kingdom whose ruler was isolated and eventually conquered.
    • 有巢氏 - A legendary ruler who was overthrown by his own officials.
    • 清阳 - A ruler who favored beauty and neglected state affairs.
    • 有洛 - A kingdom destroyed by the Shang dynasty due to its extravagant lifestyle.
    • 箕尾 - A constellation mentioned in the *Shenxian Zhuan*.
    • 岁星 - The planet Jupiter, mentioned in the *Shenxian Zhuan*.
    • 后稷 - A legendary figure in Chinese mythology, often associated with agriculture.
    • 伊尹 - A legendary figure in Chinese history, known for his wisdom and service to the Shang dynasty.
    • 箕子 - A nobleman of the Zhou dynasty who fled to Korea and later returned to conquer Yan.
    • 汉 - The Han dynasty, a significant period in Chinese history.
    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han, a powerful ruler known for his military campaigns and cultural achievements.
    • 王莽 - A usurper who briefly ruled China before the establishment of the Xin dynasty.
    • 子胥 - Sun Wu, a general who served the Wu state and is famous for his military strategies.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The sentence structure of '上古男子三十而妻女二十而嫁' is straightforward but requires careful translation to maintain the cultural context.
    • The sentence '曾子曰弟子不学古知之矣贫者不胜其忧富者不胜其乐' is a series of statements that need to be translated in a way that preserves the flow and meaning.
    • The sentence '昔西夏仁而去兵城郭不脩武士无位唐伐之西夏亡' describes a historical event and requires careful translation to maintain the sequence of events.
    • The sentence '昔者玄都贤鬼神道废人事其谋臣不用龟防是从忠臣无禄神巫用国' is complex and requires breaking down into clear, understandable parts.
    • The sentence '榆炯氏之君孤而无徒曲沃进伐之以亡' describes a historical event and requires careful translation to maintain the sequence of events.
    • The sentence '昔有巢氏有臣而贵任之专国主断已而夺之臣怒而生变有巢氏以亡' is complex and requires breaking down into clear, understandable parts.
    • The sentence '昔者清阳强力贵美女不治国而亡' is straightforward but requires careful translation to maintain the cultural context.
    • The sentence '昔有洛氏宫室无常囿池广大人民困匮商伐之有洛以亡' describes a historical event and requires careful translation to maintain the sequence of events.
    • The sentence '神仙传曰说上据箕尾为宿岁星降为东方朔傅说死后有此宿东方生无岁星' is complex and requires breaking down into clear, understandable parts.
    • The sentence '曾子曰好吾者知吾美矣恶我者知吾恶矣' is straightforward but requires careful translation to maintain the cultural context.
    • The sentence '思士不妻而感思女不夫而孕后稷生乎巨迹伊尹生乎空桑' is complex and requires breaking down into clear, understandable parts.
    • The sentence '箕子居朝鲜其后伐燕复之朝鲜亡入海为鲜国师两妻黑色珥两青蛇盖勾芒也' is complex and requires breaking down into clear, understandable parts.
    • The sentence '汉兴多瑞应至武帝之世特甚麟凤数见王莽时郡国多称瑞应岁岁相寻皆由顺时之欲承防求媚多无实应乃使人猜疑' is complex and requires breaking down into clear, understandable parts.
    • The sentence '子胥伐楚燔其府库破其九龙之钟' is straightforward but requires careful translation to maintain the cultural context.

    Proper Nouns

    • 曾子 - Zengzi, a Confucian disciple.
    • 西夏 - Western Xia, an ancient kingdom in China.
    • 玄都 - Xuandu, an ancient kingdom.
    • 榆炯 - Yuyong, an ancient kingdom.
    • 有巢氏 - You Chao, a legendary ruler.
    • 清阳 - Qingyang, a ruler.
    • 有洛 - Lu, a kingdom.
    • 箕尾 - Jiwěi, a constellation.
    • 岁星 - Jupiter, the planet.
    • 后稷 - Houji, a legendary figure.
    • 伊尹 - Yi Yin, a legendary figure.
    • 箕子 - Ji Zi, a nobleman.
    • 汉 - Han, the Han dynasty.
    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu, a ruler of the Han dynasty.
    • 王莽 - Wang Mang, a usurper.
    • 子胥 - Sun Wu, a general.

    In ancient times, men married at thirty and women at twenty. Zengzi said, "My disciples do not need to study ancient texts to understand certain truths; the poor cannot bear their sorrows, and the rich cannot bear their joys."

    In the past, the Western Xia was a peaceful kingdom that did not maintain its defenses or employ warriors. The Tang dynasty attacked and conquered it. In the ancient kingdom of Xuandu, the rulers relied on divination and neglected human affairs. Their advisors were not used, and the state was governed by divination. The loyal ministers had no rewards, and the state was ruled by divine sorcerers.

    The king of Yuyong was isolated and had no allies. Quwo advanced and conquered him, leading to his downfall.

    In the past, You Chao had officials who were favored and given power. The ruler made decisions without consulting them, and they became angry and caused a rebellion, leading to the downfall of You Chao. In the past, Qingyang was a powerful ruler who favored beautiful women and neglected state affairs, leading to his downfall.

    In the past, the kingdom of Lu had no fixed palaces or extensive parks. The people were impoverished, and the Shang dynasty attacked and conquered it, leading to the downfall of Lu.

    The Shenxian Zhuan (Book of Immortals) says that the constellation Jiwěi is associated with the planet Jupiter, which was said to have been seen after the death of Fu Yue. After Fu Yue's death, the planet Jupiter was no longer visible in the sky.

    Zengzi said, "Those who like me know my virtues, and those who dislike me know my faults."

    A man who is not married can feel the longing for a wife, and a woman who is not married can become pregnant. Houji was born under unusual circumstances, and Yi Yin was born in a cave.

    Ji Zi fled to Korea and later returned to conquer Yan. He then established a new state, and the Korean state was destroyed and its people fled to the sea, becoming the rulers of a new state with two black wives and two green snakes, which are said to be the spirits of Goumang.

    The Han dynasty saw many auspicious signs, especially during the reign of Emperor Wu, when phoenixes and unicorns were frequently seen. During the reign of Wang Mang, the states and regions often reported auspicious signs year after year, all because they followed the desires of the ruler and flattered him, leading to many false reports and causing people to become suspicious.

    Sun Wu attacked Chu, burned its treasuries, and destroyed its nine dragons' bells. [48]

    蓍一千嵗而三百茎其本以老故知吉凶蓍末大于本为上吉筮必沐浴斋洁焚香每月望浴蓍必五浴之浴亦然明夷曰昔夏后筮乘飞龙而登于天而使华陶占之陶曰吉昔夏启筮徙九鼎启果徙之
    　　昔舜筮登天为神占之有黄龙神曰不吉武王伐殷而占于蓍老蓍老曰吉桀筮伐唐而占于荧惑曰不吉昔鲧筮注洪水而占于大明曰不吉有初无后
    　　蓍末大于本为上吉次蒿次荆皆如是蓍皆月望浴之
    　　水之怪为龙罔象木石之怪为躨魍魉土之怪为坟羊火之怪为宋无忌
    　　鬭战死亡之处其人马血积年化为燐燐着地及草木如露略不可见行人或有触者着人体便有光拂拭便分散无数愈甚有细咤声如炒豆唯静住良久乃灭后其人忽忽如失魂经日乃差今人梳头着髻时有随梳解结有光者亦有咤声
    　　风山之首方高三百里风穴如电突深三十里春风自此而出也何以知还风也假令东风云反从西来诜诜而疾此不旋踵立西风矣所以然者诸风皆从上而下或薄于云云行疾下虽有微风不能胜上上风来则反矣 [49]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [49]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 蓍一千嵗而三百茎其本以老故知吉凶 - A set phrase describing the age and condition of the *shé* (蓍, a type of plant used for divination) and its ability to predict auspiciousness or inauspiciousness.
    • 筮必沐浴斋洁焚香 - A ritual preparation for divination, including bathing, fasting, and burning incense.
    • 每月望浴蓍必五浴之 - The frequency and method of cleaning the *shé* during divination rituals.
    • 浴龜亦然 - The same ritual cleansing applies to *kūi* (龜, tortoise shells used for divination).
    • 明夷曰昔夏后筮乘飛龍而登於天而使華陶占之 - A specific divination event from the *Zhouyi* (易經, Book of Changes), where Yu the Great (夏后) consults the *shé* about ascending to heaven.
    • 陶曰吉昔夏啟筮徙九鼎啟果徙之 - Continues the narrative from the *Zhouyi*, where Yu the Great’s son, Qi (夏啟), moves the nine tripods.
    • 昔舜筮登天為神占之有黃龍神曰不吉 - Another divination event from the *Zhouyi*, where Shun consults the *shé* about ascending to heaven, and a dragon god predicts inauspiciousness.
    • 武王伐殷而占於蓍老蓍老曰吉桀筮伐唐而占於熒惑曰不吉 - Describes two more divination events from the *Zhouyi*: King Wu of Zhou consulting the *shé* before his campaign against the Shang, and Jie of Xia consulting the *shé* before his campaign against Tang.
    • 昔鲧筮注洪水而占於大明曰不吉有初無後 - Another divination event from the *Zhouyi*, where Gun consults the *shé* about controlling the flood, and the *shé* predicts inauspiciousness.
    • 蓍末大于本为上吉次蒿次荆皆如是 - A phrase describing the ranking of different types of divination tools based on their effectiveness.
    • 水之怪为龙罔象木石之怪为躨魍魉土之怪为坟羊火之怪为宋无忌 - Lists various mythical creatures associated with natural elements.
    • 鬭战死亡之处其人马血积年化为燐燐着地及草木如露略不可见行人或有触者着人体便有光拂拭便分散无数愈甚有细咤声如炒豆唯静住良久乃灭后其人忽忽如失魂经日乃差 - Describes the phenomenon of *yángguāng* (磷光), which appears as a ghostly light after battlefields, and the effects it has on people who come into contact with it.
    • 风山之首方高三百里风穴如电突深三十里春风自此而出也何以知还风也假令东风云反从西来诜诜而疾此不旋踵立西风矣所以然者诸风皆从上而下或薄于云云行疾下虽有微风不能胜上上风来则反矣 - Describes the geography and wind patterns of a mountain range, explaining how the wind behaves and changes direction.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 蓍 - A plant used for divination in ancient China, often associated with the *Zhouyi*.
    • 龜 - Tortoise shells used for divination, often inscribed with hexagrams.
    • 明夷 - A hexagram from the *Zhouyi* (易經, Book of Changes), often interpreted as a time of darkness or difficulty.
    • 夏后 - Refers to Yu the Great, a legendary ruler of the Xia Dynasty.
    • 华陶 - A diviner mentioned in the *Zhouyi*.
    • 夏啟 - The son of Yu the Great, who succeeded him as the ruler of the Xia Dynasty.
    • 舜 - A legendary ruler of the Yao Dynasty, known for his virtue and wisdom.
    • 武王 - King Wu of Zhou, who led the Zhou Dynasty in overthrowing the Shang Dynasty.
    • 桀 - The last ruler of the Xia Dynasty, known for his tyranny.
    • 鲧 - The father of Yu the Great, who attempted to control the great flood but failed.
    • 九鼎 - Nine tripods, symbols of imperial power in ancient China.
    • 大明 - A term that can refer to the sun or a bright deity in the *Zhouyi*.
    • 龙罔象 - Mythical creatures associated with water.
    • 躨魍魉 - Mythical creatures associated with wood and stone.
    • 坟羊 - Mythical creatures associated with earth.
    • 宋无忌 - Mythical creatures associated with fire.
    • 燐 - A ghostly light that appears after battlefields, often associated with the spirits of the dead.
    • 风山 - A mountain range, likely referring to a specific geographical location in ancient China.

    Proper Nouns

    • 蓍 - The plant used for divination.
    • 龜 - The tortoise shell used for divination.
    • 明夷 - A hexagram from the *Zhouyi*.
    • 夏后 - Yu the Great.
    • 华陶 - A diviner.
    • 夏啟 - Qi of Xia.
    • 舜 - Shun.
    • 武王 - King Wu of Zhou.
    • 桀 - Jie of Xia.
    • 鲧 - Gun.
    • 九鼎 - Nine tripods.
    • 大明 - The sun or a bright deity.
    • 龙罔象 - Mythical creatures associated with water.
    • 躨魍魉 - Mythical creatures associated with wood and stone.
    • 坟羊 - Mythical creatures associated with earth.
    • 宋无忌 - Mythical creatures associated with fire.
    • 燐 - Ghostly light.
    • 风山 - A mountain range.

    The shé (蓍) plant, which is a thousand years old and produces three hundred stems, is considered auspicious because its base is old. The best shé is one where the end is larger than the base, indicating the highest level of auspiciousness. For divination, one must bathe and fast, burn incense, and clean the shé five times each month. The same ritual is applied to the kūi (龜) tortoise shells. According to the Zhouyi (易經, Book of Changes), in the past, Yu the Great (夏后) consulted the shé about ascending to heaven on a flying dragon and had Hua Tao (华陶) interpret it, who said it was auspicious. Later, Qi of Xia (夏啟) consulted the shé about moving the nine tripods, and indeed, he moved them.

    In the past, Shun (舜) consulted the shé about ascending to heaven as a deity, and a yellow dragon god said it was inauspicious. King Wu of Zhou (武王) consulted the shé before his campaign against the Shang, and the shé said it was auspicious. Jie of Xia (桀) consulted the shé before his campaign against Tang, and the shé said it was inauspicious. In the past, Gun (鲧) consulted the shé about controlling the flood, and the shé said it was inauspicious, predicting that there would be no good outcome.

    The best shé is one where the end is larger than the base, followed by hāo (蒿) and jīng (荆). All shé and kūi are cleaned during the full moon. Water spirits are dragons, wǎngxiàng (罔象); wood and stone spirits are lóngwǎngliǎng (躨魍魉); earth spirits are fén yáng (坟羊); fire spirits are sòng wújì (宋无忌).

    In places where battles have taken place and many people and horses have died, their blood accumulates over the years and turns into yángguāng (磷光), which appears on the ground and on plants like dew, barely visible. When people come into contact with it, they may see a faint light on their bodies, which disperses when wiped away. If they touch it more, they hear a fine, popping sound like roasting beans. Only by remaining still for a long time does the light disappear, and the person may feel dazed and lose their spirit for several days. Today, when people comb their hair and tie it up, sometimes they see a faint light or hear a popping sound.

    The peak of the wind mountain is 300 li wide and high, with a wind tunnel that is 30 li deep and shaped like a lightning bolt. Spring winds emerge from this tunnel. How do we know that the wind returns? If the east wind comes and then suddenly turns back to the west, it will quickly become a strong west wind. This happens because all winds come from above and may be deflected by clouds, which move quickly downward. Even a slight wind cannot overcome the upper wind, and when the upper wind comes, it reverses direction. [49]

    春秋书鼷鼠食郊牛牛死鼷鼠最小者食物当时不觉痛世传云亦食人项肥厚皮处亦不觉或名甘鼠俗人讳此所啮衰病之征
    　　鼠食巴豆三年重三十斤

    　　博物志卷九
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　博物志卷十
    　　晋　张华　撰
    　　杂説下
    　　妇人姙娠未满三月着壻衣冠平旦左绕井三匝暎祥影而去勿反顾勿令人知见必生男【周日用曰知女则可依法或先是男如何余闻有定法定母年日月与受胎时日算之遇竒则为男遇偶则为女知为女胎即可依法】
    　　妇人姙身不欲令见丑恶物异鸟兽食当避其异常味不欲令见熊罴虎豹并及射鸟射雉食牛心白犬肉鲤鱼头席不正不坐割不正不食听诵诗书讽咏之音不听淫声不视邪色以此产子必贤明端正寿考所谓父母胎教之法【卢氏曰子之得清纯滋液则生仁圣得错乱之气则生贪淫子因母气也】故古者妇人姙娠必慎所感感于善则善感于恶则恶矣姙娠者不可啖兔肉又不可见兔令儿唇缺又不可啖生姜令儿多指 [50]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [50]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 春秋書 - Historical record, specifically the *Spring and Autumn Annals*.
    • 鼷鼠 - A small mouse.
    • 食郊牛 - Eating the ox used in a sacrificial ritual.
    • 牛死鼷鼠最小者食物當時不覺痛 - The ox died after being bitten by the smallest mouse, and the bite was not felt at the time.
    • 世傳云 - It is said in the world.
    • 食人項肥厚皮處 - Eating the thick skin of the neck.
    • 亦不覺 - Also not felt.
    • 或名甘鼠 - Sometimes called the sweet mouse.
    • 俗人讳此所啮衰病之征 - Common people avoid mentioning the signs of decay and illness caused by its bite.
    • 鼠食巴豆三年重三十斤 - A mouse eating croton seeds and gaining significant weight over three years.
    • 博物志 - A book on natural history and various subjects.
    • 卷九 - Chapter 9.
    • 卷十 - Chapter 10.
    • 晋 - The Jin Dynasty.
    • 张华 - Zhang Hua, the author.
    • 撰 - Compiled or written.
    • 杂说下 - Part 2 of miscellaneous sayings or anecdotes.
    • 妇人姙娠未满三月着壻衣冠平旦左绕井三匝暎祥影而去勿反顾勿令人知见必生男 - A ritual for a pregnant woman to ensure the birth of a boy, involving wearing the groom's clothes and hat, circling the well three times to the left, and leaving without looking back or letting others know.
    • 周日用曰知女则可依法或先是男如何余闻有定法定母年日月与受胎时日算之遇奇则为男遇偶则为女知为女胎即可依法 - A method for determining the gender of a fetus based on the mother's age and the date of conception, with odd results indicating a boy and even results indicating a girl.
    • 妇人姙身不欲令见丑恶物异鸟兽食当避其异常味不欲令见熊罴虎豹并及射鸟射雉食牛心白犬肉鲤鱼头席不正不坐割不正不食听诵诗书讽咏之音不听淫声不视邪色以此产子必贤明端正寿考所谓父母胎教之法 - A pregnant woman should avoid seeing ugly or evil things, strange birds and beasts, and unusual flavors. She should also avoid seeing bears, tigers, and leopards, and shooting birds and pheasants. She should eat the heart of a cow, the meat of a white dog, and the head of a carp, and avoid sitting on an unaligned mat or eating improperly cut food. She should listen to the recitation of poetry and books and avoid lewd sounds and improper sights. By doing so, the child will definitely be virtuous, upright, and long-lived. This is called the method of prenatal education by parents.
    • 故古者妇人姙娠必慎所感感于善则善感于恶则恶矣 - In ancient times, a pregnant woman must be careful about what she is influenced by. If influenced by good, she will be good; if influenced by evil, she will be evil.
    • 姙娠者不可啖兔肉又不可见兔令儿唇缺又不可啖生姜令儿多指 - A pregnant woman should not eat rabbit meat or see rabbits, as it will cause the child's lips to be split. She should also not eat ginger, as it will cause the child to have extra fingers.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 春秋 - The *Spring and Autumn Annals*, a historical record of the state of Lu during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 博物志 - A book on natural history and various subjects compiled by Zhang Hua during the Jin Dynasty.
    • 晋 - The Jin Dynasty, a period in Chinese history.
    • 张华 - Zhang Hua, a prominent scholar and politician of the Jin Dynasty.
    • 周日用 - A person who provided a method for determining the gender of a fetus.
    • 诗书 - The *Book of Songs* and the *Book of Documents*, two of the Five Classics.
    • 胎教 - Prenatal education, a concept in traditional Chinese culture where the environment and behavior of a pregnant woman affect the development of the fetus.

    The Spring and Autumn Annals records that a small mouse bit the ox used in a sacrificial ritual, and the ox died without feeling the pain. The mouse is said to have bitten the thick skin of the neck, which was also not felt. It is sometimes called the sweet mouse, and common people avoid mentioning the signs of decay and illness caused by its bite.

    A mouse that eats croton seeds for three years will weigh thirty catties.

    Book of Natural History, Volume Nine
    Imperially Approved Four Treasuries
    Book of Natural History, Volume Ten
    Compiled by Zhang Hua of the Jin Dynasty
    Miscellaneous Sayings, Part 2

    A pregnant woman who is not yet three months along should wear the groom's clothes and hat, circle the well three times to the left in the early morning, reflect the auspicious shadow, and leave without looking back or letting others know.
